details: It smelled fine when we bought it. Two days later, rind was green and the inside was sour.
We lost half the wheel. That's coins down the drain.
reactions: Perishables from a trader are always a gamble. You don't know how long they've been on the road.

statCheck.pass: Cheese that turns in two days was already on the edge when they sold it. A good trader would have dropped the price to move it fast instead of selling it at full.
statCheck.fail: I'm no expert on cheese aging. Just know I lost money.

entries.intro: I asked a traveler what surprised them most about this region. They said the quiet.
details: Not the landscape, not the people. Just how few voices there are on any given stretch of road.
Where they're from, you pass someone every few minutes. Here you can walk half a day and see nobody.
reactions: I've never known it any other way. Hard to miss what you've never had.

statCheck.pass: The quiet out here is actually a deterrent for some traders. They think empty roads mean danger, when usually it just means there's not much between here and the next stop.
statCheck.fail: I've never known anything different. Hard to explain what you can't compare.

entries.intro: We traded some dried fish for a stack of good firewood with a passing merchant. Even swap, everyone happy.
details: We had more fish than we could eat and they needed provisions for the road.
The wood was already seasoned and split, ready to burn. Saved me a day's work.
reactions: That's the trade I like best. No coins, no haggling, just exchanging surplus.

entries.intro: A traveler warned us about a washout on the road toward {direction}. Said it nearly swallowed their cart.
details: The ground just gave way, apparently. A stretch of path sunk into soft earth.
They had to unload the cart, carry everything past the bad spot, and reload on the other side.
reactions: Good of them to warn us. Saves the next person a nasty surprise.

entries.intro: I lent my handcart to a trader whose wheel broke, and they brought it back in better shape than I gave it.
details: Tightened the bolts, oiled the axle, even patched a crack I'd been meaning to fix for months.
They said it was the least they could do. I said they'd done considerably more than the least.
reactions: I'll lend that cart again in a heartbeat. Fair's fair.

entries.intro: There's a trader who always brings bad news along with decent goods. Hard to enjoy the purchase.
details: Every visit it's something. Prices going up, another settlement struggling, roads getting worse.
I think they genuinely mean well, but the doom talk wears on you.
reactions: I'd pay a little extra just to buy my flour without a lecture on the state of the region.

statCheck.pass: Traders who spread anxiety are sometimes softening you up for a price increase. If you believe things are getting worse, you'll pay more to stock up now.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they're just gloomy by nature. Some people are.

entries.intro: A group of travelers pitched in and helped us move a fallen tree off the road. Didn't even ask. Just started lifting.
details: It had been blocking the path for days. We didn't have enough hands to shift it ourselves.
Took about ten minutes with all of us working together. Would've taken us a full morning.
reactions: When strangers help without being asked, you remember it.

entries.intro: A merchant accused me of shorting them on grain. I weighed it right in front of them and I was over, not under.
details: They got quiet real fast after that. Apologized, but it didn't sound like they meant it.
I think they try that with every settlement, hoping someone will toss in extra to avoid the argument.
reactions: Always weigh in front of the buyer. Saves arguments and exposes the ones looking for free grain.

statCheck.pass: It's a common pressure tactic. Accuse the seller of shorting, and a nervous one will over-deliver to keep the peace. They count on people not wanting confrontation.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they made an honest mistake. Didn't feel like one, though.

entries.intro: The wool we've been selling is finally getting a reputation. A trader from {other_settlement} asked for it by name.
details: Not our settlement name, but our wool. They called it the thick-spun stuff from up the road.
It's not a brand, but it's close. People know what they're asking for.
reactions: Being known for making something good is the best kind of reputation to have.

entries.intro: A trader confided that they barely break even on this route. They keep coming because they like the people.
details: The distance is long, the orders are small, and the roads aren't great.
They said the bigger routes pay better but you're just another cart. Here, they're welcomed.
reactions: I hope we can make it worth their while beyond just being pleasant. They deserve that.

statCheck.pass: Traders who operate at the margin on a route are the most vulnerable to dropping it. If we want to keep them, we need to make their stop here more profitable, not just more friendly.
statCheck.fail: I don't know enough about the business side to help with that.

entries.intro: Someone passing through traded me a fishing hook for a handful of dried berries. Seemed uneven, but they insisted.
details: The hook is forged, not bent wire. Proper craftsmanship.
They said they had dozens and wanted something to eat more than another hook.
reactions: Value depends on what you need at the moment. That's the whole truth of trade in one sentence.

entries.intro: I noticed the traders always check their loads after leaving {other_settlement}. Twice, sometimes.
details: They count everything, tie down the covers tighter, and look relieved once they're on the road.
One of them told me it's just habit. But habits like that come from experience.
reactions: I don't need to know what happened. The checking tells its own story.

statCheck.pass: Short counting and light-fingered dockworkers are common in bigger settlements. Traders who don't double-check after loading learn the hard way.
statCheck.fail: Probably just careful people. Not everything has a reason behind it.

entries.intro: The last trader who came through was also a dentist. Pulled a tooth for {npc_name} between selling onions.
details: Had a little kit. Pliers, a pick, and something that smelled sharp. Done in two minutes.
{npc_name} paid with a sack of flour and looked more relieved than I've ever seen them.
reactions: Out here, a trader who can pull teeth is more valuable than one who can read a ledger.

entries.intro: I sold some {resource_type} to a trader who looked like they'd been on the road for a month straight.
details: Clothes caked in dust, boots coming apart, and still smiling. Didn't complain once.
They counted their coins carefully, like every one mattered. Paid fair, no haggling.
reactions: Hard not to respect someone who works that hard just to keep moving.

statCheck.pass: Traders who count coins carefully and don't haggle are usually operating on thin margins. They know exactly what they can afford and what they can't.
statCheck.fail: I don't know their situation. They seemed honest, that's enough.

entries.intro: A merchant gave me a tip. Wrap {resource_type} in damp cloth for storage. Keeps it from drying out.
details: I've been storing it wrong for years, apparently. Just tossing it in the cellar and hoping for the best.
Tried the damp cloth method and the difference after a week is obvious.
reactions: A little knowledge at the right time saves more than money.

entries.intro: A traveler asked to fill their water skin and left without saying thank you. Just filled it and walked off.
details: Didn't look back, didn't nod, nothing. Like the well was there for their personal use.
It's not the water I mind. It's the assumption.
reactions: Manners don't cost anything. That's what makes it worse when people skip them.

statCheck.pass: People who take without acknowledging are used to impersonal trade. Wells, troughs, public resources. Where they're from, those things belong to everyone.
statCheck.fail: I just think a nod costs nothing.

entries.intro: A caravan came through with goods from so far away the labels were in a language nobody could read.
details: Sealed clay pots with wax lids. We had to open one to find out what was inside. Ground pepper.
The merchant charged a fortune, but the pepper is incredible. A pinch goes further than I expected.
reactions: You pay for the distance. The further something travels, the more it costs and the more exotic it feels.

entries.intro: The trader who was supposed to bring {resource_type} showed up with the wrong thing entirely.
details: We ordered one thing and got another. They said the shipments got mixed up somewhere along the way.
Now we have something we didn't need and still don't have what we asked for.
reactions: Mistakes happen. But it's us who deal with the consequences, not them.

entries.intro: I've been teaching myself to mend harness leather so we can help traders who stop here.
details: If we can fix their gear while they rest, they'll remember us. Maybe stop more often.
It's not hard work. Just stitching and oiling. The trick is having the right awl.
reactions: Being useful to the people you depend on is just good sense.

statCheck.pass: A settlement that can repair gear becomes a maintenance stop on the route. Traders plan around it and some will detour just for reliable harness work.
statCheck.fail: I'm just trying to be useful. Whether it works out, we'll see.

entries.intro: A merchant told me the best way to judge a settlement is by how they treat the animals that come with the carts.
details: They said the places that offer water and shade for the horses get return visits. The ones that don't, don't.
I've noticed it too. We've always had a trough by the road, and the same traders keep coming back.
reactions: Hospitality extends to the things that carry the goods, not just the people.

entries.intro: We had three weeks of no traders at all. Felt like the world forgot about us.
details: No carts, no travelers, no news. Just us and whatever we already had.
By the end of the second week, every conversation was about when someone would finally show up.
reactions: When the road goes quiet that long, you start to understand how much of your life depends on it.

statCheck.pass: Three weeks without traffic usually means a disruption somewhere upstream. A washed-out crossing, a closed pass, or a settlement that's stopped producing.
statCheck.fail: No way to know from here. We just waited.

entries.intro: A traveler taught some of the children a clapping game. They've been doing it nonstop ever since.
details: It has a little song that goes with it, something about counting sheep over a bridge.
The traveler was only here for an afternoon, but they left something that'll last.
reactions: Children remember the travelers who pay attention to them.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said their prices are firm. No bargaining, no exceptions.
details: Take it or leave it, they said. And the prices weren't bad, actually.
No games, no starting high and meeting in the middle. Just a number.
reactions: Part of me hates it. Part of me wishes everyone did business that way.

entries.intro: I traded a blanket I'd woven for a bag of {resource_type}. The traveler seemed thrilled.
details: They held it up, ran their hands over the stitching, and said it was finer than what they see in {other_settlement}.
I don't know if that's true, but the way they handled it told me they valued it.
reactions: When someone appreciates your work, the trade feels like more than just an exchange.

statCheck.pass: Handmade goods from small settlements carry a premium in larger markets because they're uncommon. That blanket is probably worth twice the {resource_type} I got for it.
statCheck.fail: I'm just glad they liked it. The trade felt fair to me.

entries.intro: We found out a trader's been charging us more than they charge {other_settlement} for the same goods.
details: A traveler from there mentioned the price offhandedly. It was noticeably lower than what we pay.
When I brought it up, the trader said the distance accounts for the difference. It doesn't. It's the same road.
reactions: Now I know, and they know I know. We'll see if the price changes.

statCheck.pass: Price discrimination by settlement size is standard practice. Traders charge what each market will bear, and isolated buyers always pay more.
statCheck.fail: All I know is the numbers don't match. The rest is their problem.

entries.intro: A trader who usually brings tools came with nothing but seed stock this trip. Said there's been a run on seeds.
details: Settlements across the region are planting more than usual. Everyone's trying to grow their own supply.
I bought some, just in case. Can't hurt to have extra options in the ground.
reactions: When everyone starts doing the same thing at once, it usually means they heard something we didn't.

statCheck.pass: A widespread shift to local production usually follows a trade disruption. If multiple settlements are planting more, it's because they don't trust the supply chain to hold.
statCheck.fail: Could be nothing. Or could be everyone knows something I don't.

entries.intro: I walked a traveler to the edge of the settlement and pointed them toward {other_settlement}. They thanked me three times.
details: It was a five-minute walk. Hardly worth the gratitude.
But they said the last settlement they stopped at wouldn't even answer their questions.
reactions: The bar for kindness is apparently very low. We can manage that.

entries.intro: A trader dropped off a delivery and left a note saying they won't be back until next season.
details: No explanation, just a date. We'll have to make what they brought last until then.
The note was scrawled on the back of a receipt. Didn't even use fresh paper.
reactions: You'd think after years of business, we'd rate more than a scribble on scrap.

entries.intro: I helped a trader patch a hole in their cart cover. They taught me the stitch while we worked.
details: A sailmaker's stitch, they called it. Pulls tight and doesn't let water through.
I've already used it on my own tarp. Holds better than anything I was doing before.
reactions: Every traveler who passes through carries something useful, even if it's just a bit of know-how.

entries.intro: The rope from the last shipment smelled like it had been sitting in water. Wouldn't trust it to hold anything heavy.
details: Mildew running through the fibers. You could see it if you untwisted a strand.
I complained to the trader and they said all their rope came from the same batch. Nothing else available.
reactions: Rope you can't trust is worse than no rope. At least with nothing, you find another way.

statCheck.pass: Mildewy rope means it was stored in a hold or cellar that floods. Once the fibers absorb moisture, the rot is permanent even if you dry them out.
statCheck.fail: I just know it smells wrong and I don't trust it.

entries.intro: A family of travelers shared their campfire with me while I was coming back late. Good people.
details: They were heading toward {direction} with everything they owned. Moving to start fresh somewhere.
We sat and talked about the places we'd been, which for me wasn't many.
reactions: Strangers around a fire are just neighbors you haven't met yet.

entries.intro: A trader said they carry a different inventory depending on the season. We always get the winter stock.
details: Summer goods go to the bigger settlements first. By the time they reach us, it's already autumn.
So we get wool, salt, and preserved food while everyone else gets fresh produce and cloth.
reactions: Being last on the route means living one season behind everyone else.

statCheck.pass: Seasonal inventory rotation is deliberate. Summer goods have higher margins, so they go to settlements that buy in volume. We get the stable, boring stock because that's what's left.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand how they decide what goes where.

entries.intro: A traveler asked if we had a message board. We pointed at the post by the well where we nail things.
details: They pinned up a notice about work available in {other_settlement}. Decent wages listed.
Nobody here is going to take it, but it was interesting to see what people earn elsewhere.
reactions: Knowing what you'd make somewhere else doesn't help when you're staying put. But you still want to know.

entries.intro: The tallow candles from the last batch burn smoky and stink. I miss the old supplier.
details: These ones spit and gutter in any draft. Half the wick doesn't even catch properly.
The trader said the price is lower, which is true. But light you can't read by isn't really light.
reactions: Cheap candles in the dark teach you what cheap actually means.

statCheck.pass: Cheap tallow candles are usually rendered from scraps that weren't cleaned properly. Good tallow is clarified twice to remove the impurities that cause smoking.
statCheck.fail: I just know these ones are terrible.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a wheel of cheese so large it took two people to carry it off the cart.
details: We all chipped in and bought it together. Cut it into portions right there on the road.
Sharp, aged, crumbly in the best way. The kind of cheese that makes bread exciting.
reactions: When the whole settlement pools money for something, you know it's worth it.

entries.intro: I've been getting better at predicting when the traders will arrive. The birds give it away.
details: The crows follow the road. When they start circling in a line, something's coming.
Give it an hour after the first crow and there's your cart.
reactions: Out here you learn to read the world differently. Birds are as good as a lookout.

entries.intro: A trader asked to store some crates with us until their next trip through. We said yes.
details: Sealed crates, not heavy. They said it's easier than hauling them both directions.
They're paying us in trade credit. Next visit, we get first pick at a discount.
reactions: Using our space for their goods isn't free. But the arrangement works for everyone.

statCheck.pass: Offering storage is how small settlements become reliable stops. Once a trader stores goods with you, they have a reason to come back.
statCheck.fail: Seems like a fair enough deal. We'll see how it plays out.

entries.intro: Someone stole from a traveler while they slept here. I'm embarrassed for all of us.
details: They woke up missing a belt pouch with their travel money. Nobody saw anything.
We collected what we could and gave it to them. It wasn't enough to replace what was taken.
reactions: Word about this kind of thing travels faster than the goods on any cart.

statCheck.pass: One theft can blacklist a settlement for years among traders who share route information. The cost of what was stolen is nothing compared to the trade we'll lose.
statCheck.fail: I just hope the traders don't hold it against all of us.

entries.intro: A traveler from {other_settlement} told me they make soap there from ashes and animal fat. Same way we do.
details: Somehow that made me feel connected to them. Same method, same ingredients, different hands.
We compared techniques for a while. Theirs smells better, I'll admit.
reactions: It's reassuring to know people you've never met are solving the same problems the same way.

entries.intro: The shoe merchant came through and for the first time had my size. I nearly wept.
details: I've been wearing oversized boots stuffed with rags for two seasons. These actually fit.
They're not fancy, but they don't slip and my heels don't blister anymore.
reactions: Shoes that fit properly are a luxury people with access take for granted.

entries.intro: A trader told me the roads are getting busier across the whole region. More people moving, more goods flowing.
details: They said it happens in cycles. A few good harvests and everyone loosens up, starts spending again.
We haven't felt it here yet, but if the trend holds, more carts should come our way.
reactions: Good news from a trader is rare enough that I choose to believe it.

entries.intro: A merchant weighed our {resource_type} and said it was the heaviest lot he'd measured this route.
details: Dense, well-processed. He said the settlements closer in produce lighter stuff in bigger volumes.
We make less, but what we make is solid. He paid a premium without being asked.
reactions: There's something to be said for doing a small thing well.

entries.intro: I saw a traveler writing in a ledger after every settlement they passed through. Recording prices, goods, quantities.
details: They weren't a trader themselves. Said they collect information and sell it to merchants who plan routes.
I asked if they'd share what they'd learned, and they smiled and said that's what costs money.
reactions: Even information has a price out here. Makes sense, I suppose.

statCheck.pass: Price information is power in trade. If you know what goods cost two settlements away, you can judge whether your local trader is fair.
statCheck.fail: I wouldn't know what to do with that kind of information anyway.

entries.intro: We bartered some smoked fish for needles and thread. The kind of trade that keeps a settlement dressed.
details: The trader had needles of every size, steel ones. Better than anything we could make ourselves.
I bought extra for {npc_name} too. They've been mending with a bone awl.
reactions: The small necessities are what trade is actually for. Not luxuries. Needles.

entries.intro: A trader showed me how the prices of everything are connected. Raise one and the rest follow.
details: When grain costs more, bread costs more. When bread costs more, people spend less on everything else.
By the time it reaches us, every increase along the chain has stacked up.
reactions: We're at the end of the chain. Every bump along the way lands on us.

statCheck.pass: It's called cost pass-through. Each handler adds their increase, and by the final buyer, a small change at the source has multiplied. The further you are from the source, the more you pay for someone else's problem.
statCheck.fail: I don't fully understand it. I just know everything costs more than it should.

entries.intro: A traveler let me look through a spyglass they were carrying. I could see the hills beyond {direction} like they were right in front of me.
details: I've lived here my whole life and never seen those hills clearly. They have trees on them, proper tall ones.
They offered to sell it, but the price was more than I make in a season.
reactions: For a moment, the world was bigger. Then I handed it back and it got small again.

entries.intro: The dried meat a trader brought was so salty it made my eyes water. Had to soak it overnight to eat it.
details: They over-cured it to keep it from spoiling on a long haul. Practical, but barely edible.
With enough soaking and a good stew, it's passable. Without that effort, you'd chip a tooth.
reactions: Preserved food isn't supposed to be good. It's supposed to exist when you need it.

statCheck.pass: Over-salting preserved meat is a storage trick. It lasts indefinitely but needs soaking before eating. The trader probably bought old military surplus and is reselling it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it's so salty. I just know my tongue disagrees with it.

entries.intro: {npc_name} convinced a passing trader to stay and share dinner with the whole settlement.
details: We set up a long table outside and everyone brought something. The trader brought stories.
They talked about the other settlements on their route, what people eat, how they build. It was fascinating.
reactions: Evenings like that cost nothing and give you something to talk about for weeks.

entries.intro: I tried to sell a trader some carved spoons I'd made. They said the market for those is dead.
details: Apparently someone in {other_settlement} makes them by the dozen with some kind of jig. Faster and cheaper.
My spoons are nicer, but nice doesn't matter when the other ones cost half as much.
reactions: Handmade loses to fast-made. That's just how things go now.

statCheck.pass: Machine-cut goods always win on price because one person produces the volume of ten. Handwork survives only when it offers something the buyer can't get from a jig.
statCheck.fail: I just know they sell faster than mine. That's the whole picture.

entries.intro: A young traveler asked me what we grow here and actually listened to the answer. Really listened.
details: Most people ask to be polite and zone out by the second sentence. This one asked follow-up questions.
They wanted to know about the soil, the rainfall, what does well in our climate.
reactions: It's flattering when someone treats your ordinary life as something worth learning about.

entries.intro: The linen that came through from {other_settlement} is rougher than what we used to get. Feels like burlap.
details: The trader said the looms there changed hands. New owner, new quality.
I bought it because we needed it, not because I liked it.
reactions: When your only option is the bad option, you take it and complain later.

statCheck.pass: When a workshop changes hands, the new owner often uses cheaper materials or faster methods to recoup their investment. Quality drops until they're financially stable.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what happened there. Just know the cloth is worse.

entries.intro: A merchant taught me a knot for bundling that I use every day now. Simple, tight, releases clean.
details: Two loops, one pull, done. I'd been tying four-step knots my whole life for no reason.
They said every trader learns it first thing. It's the knot that holds the rest of the business together.
reactions: I've probably saved an hour this week alone. Small improvements add up fast.

entries.intro: A passing cart lost a sack of grain on the road and didn't notice. We found it in the ditch.
details: Good grain, dry and clean. Probably fell off when they hit a rut.
We set it aside in case they come back for it. So far, nobody's asked.
reactions: Their loss is our find, but I'd still return it. You'd want the same.

statCheck.pass: If they haven't come back in a few days, they either don't know or they've written it off. After a week, it's fair to use it.
statCheck.fail: I'll hold onto it a while longer. Feels wrong to just keep it.

entries.intro: A trader showed up with a badly injured hand and still insisted on doing business. Wouldn't rest.
details: Wrapped in dirty cloth, clearly hurting. They winced every time they lifted something.
We cleaned it and re-wrapped it for them. They looked embarrassed about the whole thing.
reactions: Some people don't know how to accept help. They think needing it is weakness.

entries.intro: I traded some of our herbs for glass jars from a passing merchant. Finally have something proper to store things in.
details: We've been using clay pots with cloth covers, which work but let moisture in.
The glass seals tight. Things stay dry and you can see what's inside without opening anything.
reactions: It's the kind of improvement you don't realize you needed until you have it.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've walked through settlements where nobody even comes out to greet them.
details: Doors closed, curtains drawn. They pass through and nobody says a word.
They said ours is one of the few where someone actually walks up and says hello.
reactions: Being friendly costs nothing. I don't understand people who don't bother.

entries.intro: The beeswax from the last trader is dark and crumbly. Barely usable for sealing.
details: Good beeswax should be pliable and golden. This looks like it was scraped from the bottom of a barrel.
When I heat it, half of it turns to ash. There's debris mixed in.
reactions: I paid full price for what's essentially half product. That's a sting I won't forget.

statCheck.pass: Dark, crumbly beeswax has been over-rendered or mixed with tallow to stretch the volume. Always ask for a sample you can heat before buying the lot.
statCheck.fail: All I know is it's not right. Won't buy from them again.

entries.intro: A merchant noticed our fence was leaning and offered to trade some iron braces for our surplus. Didn't even have to ask.
details: They saw the problem, had the solution on their cart, and proposed the deal all in one breath.
Good traders notice what you need before you've figured it out yourself.
reactions: That's the difference between someone who sells and someone who solves. I'll take the solver every time.

entries.intro: I keep a jar of coins by the door for when the traders come. It's been emptier than usual.
details: Every visit thins it out a little more, and the refill comes slower each time.
I count what's left and decide what we can afford and what we'll do without.
reactions: Trade is supposed to go both ways, but lately it feels like it mostly flows out.

statCheck.pass: A settlement that spends more than it earns on trade is in slow decline. The only way to reverse it is producing something with trade value, not just consuming what others bring.
statCheck.fail: I just see the jar getting emptier. The economics aren't my strength.

entries.intro: A traveler brought a remedy for joint pain they said was popular in {other_settlement}. It actually works.
details: Some kind of poultice you wrap around the knee or elbow. Smells like pine and something sharp.
After a day of wearing it, the ache was half what it was. I asked them to write down the recipe.
reactions: Trade isn't just goods. Remedies, recipes, tricks. The knowledge is just as valuable.

entries.intro: Two traders who used to come separately have started traveling together. Safer that way, they said.
details: They combine their loads onto one larger cart and split the road costs.
For us it means twice the selection in one stop, which is a nice change.
reactions: If it keeps them coming, I'm all for it.

entries.intro: I noticed that the same goods cost different amounts depending on which trader brings them. No consistency at all.
details: One charges fair for {resource_type}, the next wants half again as much for the same quality.
Without more traders to choose from, you just pay whatever the one in front of you asks.
reactions: Choice is a luxury. Out here, we take what comes and hope the fair one shows up first.

statCheck.pass: Price inconsistency between traders is actually useful information. If you track who charges what over time, you can time your purchases to the fair ones and avoid the rest.
statCheck.fail: I don't keep records like that. Maybe I should.

entries.intro: A traveler stopped just to let their horse graze in our field. Asked politely, and we said yes.
details: The horse ate for an hour while the traveler sat under a tree and slept. Both looked like they needed it.
They left a small bag of flour by the gate when they moved on.
reactions: Quiet transactions like that are my favorite kind. No words, just fairness.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} said they used to come through here more often, but the road's gotten worse.
details: Ruts, loose stones, a stretch where the drainage has failed and it floods at every rain.
They said it adds half a day to the trip, which isn't worth it for the volume we buy.
reactions: The road is our lifeline and it's falling apart. That should worry us more than it does.

statCheck.pass: A road that costs traders half a day is a road they'll eventually stop using. If we want to keep this route alive, maintaining our stretch is the most important investment we can make.
statCheck.fail: I know it's bad. I just don't know what we can do about it.

entries.intro: A merchant showed me a new kind of clasp for sacks that keeps grain sealed better than string.
details: It's a bent piece of wire that pinches the mouth closed. Simple but effective.
I bought ten of them for almost nothing. They're worth ten times what I paid in spilled grain saved.
reactions: The best inventions solve problems you didn't know you had a solution for.

entries.intro: We lost a trade deal because {npc_name} argued with the merchant over something petty.
details: It was about the condition of a single sack. One sack, out of a whole cart's worth.
The merchant packed up and left. Refused to deal with us at all after that.
reactions: One argument, one lost supplier. That's the math of it.

statCheck.pass: Traders talk to each other, and one bad interaction gets discussed at every stop along the route. Reputation spreads faster than any cart.
statCheck.fail: I just know they left and they're not coming back.

entries.intro: A traveler brought a small hand mirror as a trade item. I'd never seen my own face that clearly.
details: We have still water and polished metal, but neither is like glass. This was sharp, real.
I looked at myself for longer than I'd admit. Older than I thought.
reactions: Some things you're better off not knowing too clearly.

statCheck.pass: Glass mirrors are luxury goods in most of the region. Whoever brought that one is carrying high-value trade stock meant for wealthy buyers, not settlement folk.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what it was worth. More than I could pay, that's certain.

entries.intro: The dried fruit a trader brought was incredible. Sweet, chewy, like eating concentrated sunshine.
details: He said they dry it on racks in the open air near {other_settlement}. The climate is perfect for it.
I bought a bag and it was gone in three days. Should've bought two.
reactions: Food you can't get locally always tastes better. Maybe because you know you can't have it whenever you want.

entries.intro: A merchant brought news that {other_settlement} has been trading with someone further out that we've never dealt with.
details: New goods are flowing in. Metals, fabrics, things that didn't used to be available in the region.
If some of that reaches us, it could change what's possible here.
reactions: Trade routes are like rivers. New ones open up and everything downstream shifts.

statCheck.pass: When a new trade connection opens, prices drop temporarily as the market adjusts to the surplus. The window to buy cheap is short. A season at most before the new normal sets in.
statCheck.fail: I don't know enough about how these things ripple out.

entries.intro: I spent an afternoon helping a trader inventory their cart. They'd lost track of what they had.
details: Goods from five different settlements, all jumbled together. Half-empty sacks mixed with full ones.
We sorted it all and they were short on three items they thought they still had.
reactions: A disorganized trader loses money in ways they never see. That's its own kind of leak.

statCheck.pass: A disorganized trader is losing five to ten percent of their inventory to spoilage and miscount on every trip. They'll either learn to track or they'll go broke.
statCheck.fail: I just helped them sort things. Not my business how they run theirs.

entries.intro: A traveler said the sunsets here are the best on the whole route. I'd never thought about it.
details: They sat and watched the whole thing, start to finish. Said the way the light hits the hills is special.
I've seen it every evening of my life and never once stopped to actually look.
reactions: Takes an outsider to show you what you've been ignoring.

entries.intro: The olive oil that came in from the last trader is the real thing. Not cut with seed oil like the usual stuff.
details: You can tell by the color and the way it tastes on its own. Rich, peppery, almost bitter.
It cost more, but we use less of it because the flavor goes further.
reactions: Paying more for real quality actually saves money in the end. Hard to explain that to a tight purse, though.

entries.intro: A merchant asked if we'd be interested in a standing order. Same goods, same amount, delivered every six weeks.
details: The price is slightly higher than buying ad hoc, but the certainty is worth something.
We'd know exactly when things arrive and plan accordingly.
reactions: Predictability has a value. I'm not sure everyone here understands that yet.

statCheck.pass: Standing orders lock in a relationship. Guaranteed revenue for them, guaranteed supply for us. The premium is the cost of removing uncertainty.
statCheck.fail: I think it's a good idea, but I'm not the one making the decision.

entries.intro: A traveler called our bread bland. Right to my face.
details: I bake that bread. Same recipe my family's used for as long as I can remember.
They said in {other_settlement} they add herbs and oil to the dough. I call that wasting good herbs.
reactions: Good bread doesn't need dressing up. It's bread.

entries.intro: I've started keeping a tally of which traders cheat and which ones are fair. Short list on the fair side.
details: Not that most are outright dishonest. It's more the little things. Light weights, rounded-up prices, vague counts.
The truly fair ones stand out because they don't do any of that.
reactions: I don't need perfect, I just need honest. It's a lower bar than you'd think.

statCheck.pass: The little tricks add up. Light weights, rounded prices, fast counting. Over a year, a dishonest trader takes ten percent more from every buyer without anyone noticing.
statCheck.fail: I can't prove any of it. It's just a feeling from years of watching.

entries.intro: A traveler left a pair of knitting needles as a gift. Good ones, smooth wood, polished ends.
details: They said they'd been carrying them for someone who wasn't there anymore. Wanted them used, not packed.
I've been using them every evening. They fit my hands like they were made for them.
reactions: Objects carry stories you'll never fully know. That makes them more valuable, not less.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} brought fresh eggs packed in straw. Every single one survived the trip.
details: We haven't had fresh eggs from outside in months. Ours lay enough for us, but not a surplus.
I scrambled four of them with some salt and it was the best meal I'd had in a week.
reactions: The simple things. It always comes back to the simple things.

entries.intro: The road to {other_settlement} has a new bridge over the creek. Someone finally built one.
details: Used to be you'd wade through or wait for dry season. Now there's proper planks and supports.
A trader told me a group of merchants pooled coin to have it done. Pays for itself in saved time.
reactions: Amazing what gets built when the people who need it are the ones paying for it.

entries.intro: Every trader who comes through has a different opinion on what we should be growing. None of them farm.
details: One says root vegetables, the next says flax, the one after that says we should raise goats.
Easy to give advice when you're leaving by sundown.
reactions: I'll take farming counsel from someone with soil under their nails, thanks.

statCheck.pass: Traders give farming advice based on what sells at their next stop, not what grows well here. Their incentive is demand, not our soil.
statCheck.fail: I don't need a reason to ignore them. I already do.

entries.intro: A traveler told me about a settlement where they ring a bell when a cart arrives. Everyone comes out to see.
details: They described it like a small event every time. People gathering, talking, trading in the open.
We'd need a bell first, and someone willing to ring it.
reactions: It's not a bad idea. Arrival of a cart is already the biggest thing that happens most days.

entries.intro: A merchant sold me a blanket woven with a pattern I've never seen. Blue and white, geometric.
details: They said it's the traditional style from far past {other_settlement}. Every region has its own.
I hung it on the wall instead of using it for bedding. Felt wrong to hide something that pretty.
reactions: Sometimes a blanket isn't just a blanket.

entries.intro: I caught a trader watering down the lamp oil they sold us. Held it up to light and you could see through it.
details: Good lamp oil is dark and thick, but this was pale and thin. Burns fast, smokes badly, and barely gives light.
They denied it, of course. Said it was a lighter blend for summer.
reactions: Summer blend. I'll file that next to all the other creative excuses.

statCheck.pass: Diluting lamp oil with water or cheap seed oil is one of the oldest tricks in trade. You test it by putting a drop on cloth. Pure oil soaks in slowly, cut oil beads up.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how they do it. I just know what I'm seeing when I light a lamp.

entries.intro: We pooled our coin with the next settlement over and placed a joint order for {resource_type}. First time we've cooperated like that.
details: The larger order got us a better rate. The trader was happy too. One stop instead of two.
Splitting it evenly was a bit of work, but the savings made it worthwhile.
reactions: Cooperation with neighbors is obvious. It just took us too long to try it.

entries.intro: A traveler asked me how long I've lived here. When I said my whole life, they looked at me like I'd said something sad.
details: I don't think they meant to be rude. In their world, staying put means settling for less.
In mine, it means I know every stone, every field, every person. That's not nothing.
reactions: People measure lives by distance covered. I measure mine by how deep the roots go.

entries.intro: The cloth dye from the last trader runs in the rain. Found that out the hard way.
details: Wore the new shirt outside and came back with blue streaks down my arms.
The dye needs to be set with vinegar before wearing. Nobody told me that.
reactions: A shirt that paints you when it rains isn't a shirt. It's a prank.

statCheck.pass: Unfixed dye is a sign of cheap processing. Proper fixing requires mordant and time, which costs the dyer money. Skipping it passes the problem to the buyer.
statCheck.fail: All I know is I looked ridiculous and the shirt is ruined.

entries.intro: A merchant noticed we stack our firewood wrong. Showed me a method that dries it faster.
details: You leave gaps in the stack so air moves through. We'd been packing it tight, which traps moisture.
I re-stacked the whole pile and the wood at the center was already drying better within a week.
reactions: Doing something one way for years doesn't mean it's the right way. I should remember that.

entries.intro: A trader told me the route to {other_settlement} is being used by more merchants than ever.
details: They said some of the overflow might start coming our way if the main road gets congested.
We're the side road. Not the destination, but a possible detour.
reactions: Even being a detour would be an upgrade for us.

entries.intro: I bartered some candles for a sack of barley with a passing merchant. We both needed what the other had.
details: No coins changed hands. Just two people with surplus trading for what they lack.
The merchant said they prefer barter to coin when they can get it. Less to carry, less to lose.
reactions: There's an honesty to handing over something you made in exchange for something you need.

entries.intro: A trader told me something I already knew and charged me for the advice. That's a new one.
details: They said I should store {resource_type} out of direct sunlight. I've been doing that for years.
But they wrapped it in this tone like they were doing me a favor.
reactions: Unsolicited advice from someone who just arrived is rarely worth the breath it rides on.

statCheck.pass: Some traders bundle advice with sales to create a sense of obligation. If you feel they've taught you something, you're more inclined to buy.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they meant well. Didn't feel that way, though.

entries.intro: A passing musician traded a song for a meal. Best deal either of us made that day.
details: They played something slow on a small string instrument. The whole road went quiet while they played.
Nobody asked them to stop. When they finished, someone brought out a second plate without being asked.
reactions: Music and food. The two oldest currencies. Both still work.

entries.intro: I've been comparing the weight of {resource_type} between different traders. The variance is embarrassing.
details: Same price, supposedly same amount. One bag weighs a quarter less than another.
The lighter ones come from the same trader every time. Consistent, just consistently short.
reactions: I've started bringing my own scale to every deal. The looks I get are priceless.

statCheck.pass: Consistent short-weighing isn't accidental. That trader has calibrated their scale to read heavy. Over a route of twenty stops, skimming a fraction at each one adds up to a whole extra sale's worth of profit.
statCheck.fail: I can't prove how they do it. I just know the bags are light.

entries.intro: A traveler gave me a piece of chalk and told me to mark the road forks. Said it helps lost carts.
details: I've been marking arrows on the big stones at every split. It takes almost no effort.
A merchant told me last week they followed my arrows right to us. First time they'd found us without asking for directions.
reactions: A piece of chalk might be the most useful thing anyone's ever given me.

entries.intro: The tea a trader brought from {other_settlement} has a flavor I can't describe. Floral, but not sweet.
details: It's made from dried blossoms, apparently. They bloom for about two weeks and that's the entire harvest.
I'm rationing what I bought. One cup in the evening, small cup, and I sit with it like it's something precious.
reactions: When something is rare and good, you learn to savor it instead of consume it.

entries.intro: A merchant unloaded here by mistake. Goods meant for somewhere else entirely.
details: By the time they realized, half of it was already unpacked. They had to reload everything.
Took an hour and we all felt awkward standing around watching.
reactions: At least they caught it. Imagine getting the wrong delivery and the right one never showing up.

statCheck.pass: Delivery mix-ups happen when traders carry orders for multiple settlements on one cart. The sloppy ones guess instead of labeling.
statCheck.fail: I don't care why it happened. Just glad they caught it.

entries.intro: {npc_name} bought seeds from a traveler and half of them didn't sprout. Waste of good coin.
details: The ones that did come up are fine, so the seeds weren't all bad. Just old, probably.
{npc_name} says they'll demand a refund if the trader shows up again. I'd pay to watch that conversation.
reactions: Seeds are a gamble even under the best conditions. Buying them from a stranger just adds another layer of risk.

entries.intro: A pair of travelers carried a message from a family member I haven't heard from in years.
details: Just a few lines on folded paper. They're alive, they're well, and they miss us.
The travelers were heading our way anyway and said carrying it was no trouble.
reactions: A few words on paper, and the whole day felt different.

entries.intro: I bought a length of chain from a trader, and every other link is rusted through.
details: It looks solid until you pull on it. Then the weak links stretch and snap.
Wrapped up on the cart it looked fine. Uncoiled in the daylight, you can see the rot.
reactions: I'm keeping what's usable and melting the rest for scrap. Not a total loss, but close.

statCheck.pass: Rusted chain that looks solid on the outside has usually been stored wet and then dried for resale. The surface rust is treated, but the core rot stays.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how it got that bad. Just know I can't use most of it.

entries.intro: A trader complimented the way we keep our road clear. Said it's the smoothest stretch on their route.
details: We fill the holes and move the stones after every rain. It's just maintenance, nothing special.
But apparently most settlements don't bother. The road is everyone's and therefore no one's.
reactions: If keeping the road clean makes traders want to come here, that's the easiest investment we'll ever make.

entries.intro: A traveler told me the accent here is thick. Said they had trouble understanding us at first.
details: I don't hear it. To me, we talk normally and everyone else sounds odd.
They said after a day they adjusted. It's just the initial surprise.
reactions: Every settlement sounds different to the people who don't live there. That's just distance doing its work.

statCheck.pass: Accents diverge faster in isolated settlements because there's less mixing with outsiders. Give it another generation and travelers will have even more trouble understanding us.
statCheck.fail: I don't think about how we sound. It's just how we talk.

entries.intro: A merchant offered to buy something I wasn't selling. An old iron pot I use as a doorstop.
details: They said it's a forge style that's not made anymore. Collectors want them.
I said no. It holds the door better than anything else I've tried.
reactions: Everything has a value to someone. Doesn't mean everything's for sale.

entries.intro: The flour from the last delivery has weevils in it. Found them when I went to bake.
details: Tiny black dots moving through the bag. The whole thing is contaminated.
I sifted what I could, but the thought of it ruins the appetite.
reactions: Not the trader's fault, probably, but I'm still not happy about it.

statCheck.pass: Weevils usually come from the source warehouse, not the road. If the grain was stored too long before shipping, the eggs were in it before the trader loaded up.
statCheck.fail: I don't know where they came from. Just know I don't want them in my bread.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a set of clay bowls glazed in a deep green color. Never seen anything like them.
details: The glaze is smooth as glass. Food doesn't stick, and they clean up with just a wipe.
I bought two and I eat every meal out of them now. Makes dinner feel like an occasion.
reactions: A good bowl changes nothing and everything at the same time.

entries.intro: A trader asked if there's anything unique about this settlement that might attract more business. I had to think.
details: We don't have a famous product or a landmark. We're just here, between places.
I told them the air is clean and the people are fair. They said that doesn't sell.
reactions: Maybe they're right. But I'm not sure I want what comes with trying to be special.

statCheck.pass: The settlements that attract trade do it by being a reliable stop. Good water, safe rest, honest people. You don't need a famous product if your reputation for fairness travels.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what would make us stand out. I'm not a merchant.

entries.intro: We tried sending {npc_name} with our goods to sell at {other_settlement} instead of waiting for traders.
details: They got better prices than we've ever been offered here. Turns out cutting out the middleman makes a real difference.
The trip is long, though. Two days there, two back, four days of labor lost.
reactions: Better money, worse time. The math only works if you have someone to spare.

statCheck.pass: The middleman's margin on our goods is probably thirty percent or more. If {npc_name} can make the trip regularly, the savings compound. It's whether you value time or coin more.
statCheck.fail: I just know we got more there than we get here. The details are for someone better with numbers.

entries.intro: A traveler left a book of pressed flowers. Every page had a different one from a different region.
details: Colors I've never seen growing around here. Purples, deep reds, one that was almost black.
They said they'd been collecting for years. One flower per place they'd been.
reactions: That's a beautiful way to carry the places you've been with you.

entries.intro: The axe heads from the last trader's stock are good. Heavy, well-tempered, hold an edge.
details: I tested one on a seasoned log and it bit clean through. No bouncing, no glancing.
The trader said they come from a smith who works alone. Small output, but everything they make is solid.
reactions: I'd rather wait for the good smith's work than settle for something that dulls in a week.

entries.intro: A traveler told me there's a stretch of road toward {direction} where the ground stays warm even in cold weather.
details: They said the soil steams in the morning. Nobody knows why, but the plants grow tall there year-round.
I've never been that far, so I can't confirm it. But they didn't seem like the type to make things up.
reactions: The world has its odd corners. Not everything needs an explanation to be interesting.

statCheck.pass: Warm ground like that usually sits over a hot spring or a vein of decaying plant matter deep below. The heat rises through the soil and changes what grows there.
statCheck.fail: I've never been that far. It sounds like one of those stories that grows with each telling.

entries.intro: A merchant forgot a crate here and came back for it two days later, furious. As if we'd hidden it.
details: We'd moved it into the shade so the contents wouldn't spoil. That's all we did.
When they saw it was untouched, they calmed down. Didn't apologize, but at least stopped shouting.
reactions: Gratitude for honesty shouldn't be this rare, but here we are.

entries.intro: A caravan cook traded me a recipe for flatbread that uses no yeast. Just flour, water, and heat.
details: You cook it on a hot stone. Takes three minutes and it puffs up like magic.
I've been making it every morning since. No waiting for dough to rise, no fuss.
reactions: The simplest recipes are always the ones you learn last.

entries.intro: The last merchant said they'll raise prices next visit. At least they had the decency to warn us.
details: Most just show up with the new prices and act like nothing changed.
This one looked me in the eye and said the costs are going up. Honest about it, even if the news isn't good.
reactions: Honesty about bad news earns more trust than a surprise discount ever could.

statCheck.pass: Traders who warn you about price increases are testing whether you'll stock up now at the current rate. Honest or not, the warning serves them either way.
statCheck.fail: I'll take the honesty at face value. Not everything is a strategy.

entries.intro: A traveler drew a picture of our settlement as a thank-you for the meal. Actually captured it well.
details: The buildings, the fields, even the way the smoke rises from the fire pit in the center.
I pinned it to the wall. It's the only image we have of what this place looks like from the outside.
reactions: Strange to see home through someone else's hand. Familiar but different.

entries.intro: I tried to buy leather from a trader and they wouldn't sell to me. Said they were holding it for someone in {other_settlement}.
details: I offered more than what they said the reservation price was. They still said no.
A deal is a deal, they said. First come, first served.
reactions: I respect it. Doesn't make it less frustrating when you need the leather now.

statCheck.pass: Traders who honor reservations even when offered more are protecting their reputation with the original buyer. That discipline is worth more to them long-term than the extra coin.
statCheck.fail: I understand the principle. Doesn't make the waiting easier.

entries.intro: A traveler offered to carry a message to {other_settlement} for free. Said they were going that way anyway.
details: I wrote a quick letter and sealed it with wax. First time I've sent word outside the settlement in months.
They said they'd hand it to the first person they met there. Better than nothing.
reactions: Communication out here depends entirely on the goodwill of strangers. That's humbling.

entries.intro: The {resource_type} this season is the best quality we've seen in years. Every trader who handles it says so.
details: Something about the conditions this year just lined up perfectly.
We're charging accordingly, and for once nobody is arguing about the price.
reactions: Good years don't come often. You appreciate them while they last.

entries.intro: I've been watching the road more than usual lately. Just sitting and watching for movement.
details: Every dust cloud gets my attention now. Is it a cart, a traveler, or just the wind?
Most of the time it's nothing. But the times it's something make the watching worth it.
reactions: Out here, the road is the only thing connecting us to everything else. You watch it the way a sailor watches the sea.

entries.intro: A trader showed up with nothing but rope and nails. That's it. A whole cart of rope and nails.
details: I asked if they had anything else and they just shrugged. Said that's what's moving right now.
Bought some of both, honestly. You always need rope and nails eventually.
reactions: Not the most exciting trade day, but practical beats exciting.

entries.intro: A traveler complimented our water. Said it's the cleanest they've tasted on the whole route.
details: We don't do anything special with it. It comes out of the ground that way.
They filled every container they had before leaving. Asked if we'd consider trading it.
reactions: Funny to think our best trade good might be something we take for granted.

entries.intro: The last merchant weighed everything behind their cart where I couldn't see. That's never a good sign.
details: I asked to watch and they said the scale is delicate. Delicate scale, sturdy excuses.
The bag felt light when I carried it home. No way to prove anything now.
reactions: Next time I'm watching, delicate scale or not.

statCheck.pass: Hidden weighing is the simplest cheat there is. A thumb on the scale, a heavier pan, a rigged fulcrum. If you can't watch, you can't verify.
statCheck.fail: Maybe I'm being suspicious. But the bag was light.

entries.intro: We haven't seen a trader from {other_settlement} in weeks. Used to be they came through every ten days.
details: Could be the roads, could be the weather, could be they found a better route that skips us.
Whatever the reason, we're running low on things we don't produce ourselves.
reactions: You don't plan for reliability to just stop.

statCheck.pass: When a regular trader drops off, it usually means they've rerouted for profit. A new customer on a different path who pays better. We're not worth the detour anymore.
statCheck.fail: I've been watching the road, but I can't tell you why they stopped coming.

entries.intro: A passing trader taught me how to tell fresh {resource_type} from old stock. Changed how I buy.
details: It's in the texture and the color at the edges. Old stock has a dullness to it you can't fake away.
I tested it on the next delivery and caught two bags that would've gone bad within the week.
reactions: One piece of good advice saves more than a dozen good deals.

entries.intro: A traveler asked if we have any craftspeople here. When I listed who we've got, they seemed unimpressed.
details: Apparently in {other_settlement} they have specialists for everything. One person just makes hinges.
We can't afford that kind of luxury. Everyone here does three jobs.
reactions: Our work isn't fancy, but everything holds together. That should count for something.

entries.intro: A merchant brought candles that burn twice as long as ours. I don't know what they put in the wax.
details: Same size, same wick, but the flame is steadier and the wax melts slower.
I bought a handful and I'm rationing them for the evenings when I need the extra light.
reactions: Good candles are one of those quiet luxuries you don't think about until you have them.

entries.intro: The road's been churned up by heavy carts this season. Walking it takes twice as long.
details: Deep ruts full of standing water. Every step is either mud or a twisted ankle waiting to happen.
The traders don't care because they're sitting on the cart. We're the ones walking in the aftermath.
reactions: More trade is good, but the road pays the price.

entries.intro: A traveler from {other_settlement} said they'd never seen stars this bright. Kept staring up like a child.
details: Apparently in bigger settlements the fires and torches wash out the sky at night.
They sat outside for an hour just looking. Made me sit down and look too.
reactions: We've got no shortage of sky here. At least that's free.

entries.intro: I traded a good knife for a bad one and didn't realize until the trader was gone.
details: The blade looked fine on the cart, but when I tried to sharpen it, the steel is soft. Won't hold an edge.
My old knife was plain but it worked. Now I've got a pretty handle and a useless blade.
reactions: Lesson learned. Test steel before you trade for it.

statCheck.pass: Soft steel is a sign of low-carbon smelting. Cheaper to produce, looks the same, but it folds instead of cutting. Traders who carry it know exactly what they're selling.
statCheck.fail: I just know it won't cut. The why doesn't matter much to me right now.

entries.intro: A merchant told me our {resource_type} is underpriced. Said we could get more if we knew what it was worth.
details: Easy for them to say. They see the price at the destination. We only see what we're offered.
They weren't offering more, either. Just commenting.
reactions: Telling someone they're underpaid while not paying them more is a special kind of honesty.

statCheck.pass: Merchants who tell you your goods are underpriced are testing whether you'll raise your ask. If you do, they move on. If you don't, they buy at the same low price, feeling generous.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the real value. We take what we can get.

entries.intro: Had a trader come through with a cart pulled by the biggest ox I've ever seen. Shoulders like a wall.
details: The ground shook when it walked. Everyone came out just to stare at the thing.
The trader said it eats more than two normal oxen but pulls three times the load.
reactions: Made our animals look like toys. I don't think they appreciated the comparison.

entries.intro: A traveler shared their meal with me while we talked. Proper seasoned stew with things I couldn't identify.
details: They carried the spices in a little roll of cloth with separate pockets for each one.
I asked what one of them was and they said they'd been buying it from the same source for years but never learned its real name.
reactions: A warm meal and good conversation with a stranger. Doesn't happen often enough.

entries.intro: The {resource_type} supply has been irregular this season. Some weeks plenty, some weeks nothing.
details: You can't plan around a supply that doesn't keep a schedule.
I've started buying extra when it shows up, just to cover the gaps.
reactions: Hoarding isn't the word I'd use. I call it sensible caution.

statCheck.pass: Irregular supply usually means the source is having trouble. Labor issues, equipment failure, or they're splitting output between more buyers than they can serve.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what's causing it. I just deal with the results.

entries.intro: A traveler stopped to ask if we had any work. Just for a day or two, anything to earn a meal.
details: We had them help stack firewood and patch a stretch of fence. Honest work, done well.
Paid them in food and a dry place to sleep. They seemed grateful for it.
reactions: Not everyone on the road is there by choice. Some are just looking for the next steady place.

entries.intro: The honey from the last trader crystallized in the jar. Hard as a rock.
details: You have to chip it out with a knife. Still tastes fine, but it's not exactly convenient.
The trader swore it was fresh. Maybe fresh for them is a different standard.
reactions: Crystallized honey is still honey. I'm annoyed, not cheated.

statCheck.pass: Honey crystallizes when it's stored cold or when it's been diluted with water. Real fresh honey pours clean for months.
statCheck.fail: It's fine. Just not what I expected.

entries.intro: A merchant suggested we start a market day. One day a week where we lay out everything we have to trade.
details: They said other settlements do it and it draws in travelers who know which day to show up.
We barely have enough surplus to fill a single table, but the idea is sound.
reactions: It's the kind of thing that works once you're big enough. We're not big enough yet.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} told me they have trouble finding honest buyers there. Too many shortcuts, too many scams.
details: They said they prefer smaller settlements because people look you in the eye when they trade.
Might've been flattery to soften me up for a deal, but it worked.
reactions: I'll take the compliment, even if it was strategic.

statCheck.pass: Traders who compliment your settlement's honesty right before a deal are working the relationship. It costs them nothing to say and makes you feel obligated to offer fair terms.
statCheck.fail: They seemed sincere. But I suppose that's the point.

entries.intro: The salt we got from the last delivery is coarse and grey. Not the fine white stuff we usually get.
details: It works the same, honestly. Might even taste a little richer. Just looks wrong.
The trader said the fine salt is scarce right now and this is what's available.
reactions: I don't eat with my eyes. It salts the food and that's enough.

entries.intro: A traveler offered to carry a message to {other_settlement} for us. Free of charge.
details: I wrote a short note to someone I haven't spoken to in too long and handed it over.
No guarantee it arrives, but it costs nothing to hope.
reactions: Words travel faster on someone else's feet.

entries.intro: A merchant tried to trade us tools made of iron so brittle they'd snap on the first hard use.
details: I tested the edge of a chisel on a piece of wood and the tip chipped right off.
When I showed them, they didn't argue. Just packed up that lot and moved to the next item.
reactions: At least they didn't try to talk their way out of it. Small mercy.

statCheck.pass: Brittle iron means the carbon content is too high and it wasn't tempered properly. A cheap smelt with no finish work. The trader knows, the smith who made them knows.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how iron goes bad. Just know it did.

entries.intro: I spotted a family moving through. Two adults, three children, everything they own on one cart.
details: They stopped to water the animals and the children ran around like they hadn't been out of the cart in days.
The parents looked tired in a way that's more than sleep. They didn't say where they were heading.
reactions: I gave them bread and didn't ask questions. Sometimes that's all you can do.

entries.intro: The leather goods from the last trader are better than anything we can make here. By a lot.
details: Supple, well-stitched, the edges finished smooth. Our leather cracks after a season.
It's the tanning, apparently. Good tanning takes time and materials we don't have.
reactions: I'd rather buy the good stuff twice a year than make bad stuff all year.

entries.intro: A merchant got stuck in the mud just outside the settlement. Took four of us to push the cart free.
details: The axle was buried halfway to the hub. We were covered in mud up to the waist by the time it moved.
They gave us a sack of dried fish as thanks. Fair trade for forty minutes of pushing.
reactions: Dried fish tastes better when you feel like you earned it.

entries.intro: A traveler said the price of {resource_type} has dropped everywhere except here. We're being overcharged.
details: They showed me what they paid at {other_settlement} and it was nearly half what we pay.
But what am I going to do, walk there every time I need a supply run?
reactions: Being remote has its costs. Literally.

statCheck.pass: Traders charge what the market will bear. Isolated settlements pay more because there's no competition. The only fix is another supplier, and right now there isn't one.
statCheck.fail: I can't argue with prices when there's only one seller.

entries.intro: A merchant left a sample of {resource_type} and said to try it before committing to a larger order.
details: Nobody's ever done that before. Usually it's pay now, complain later.
The sample was genuinely good, which made the decision easy.
reactions: Confidence in your product is the best sales pitch there is.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they'd been walking for three weeks straight. Three weeks on foot with a pack.
details: Their boots were worn through at the soles. You could see skin through the leather.
They said they couldn't afford an animal and the cart road adds days they don't have.
reactions: Three weeks of walking makes our complaints about a muddy path feel small.

entries.intro: The wool that came in from {other_settlement} is finer than anything our sheep produce. Softer, cleaner.
details: It spins easier and the finished cloth drapes instead of standing stiff.
I asked the trader about the breed and they had no idea. Just said it's what they carry.
reactions: Better wool from somewhere else is both a gift and an insult to our flock.

entries.intro: A trader told me a joke. First time one of them has treated me like a person instead of a customer.
details: It wasn't even funny, but I laughed because the effort was genuine.
We ended up talking for an hour about nothing important. Best trade stop I've had in months.
reactions: Commerce works better when both sides remember there's a person on the other end.

entries.intro: The vinegar a trader brought is so strong it took the finish off my table. I used two drops.
details: They called it concentrated. I'd call it weaponized.
My eyes watered just opening the bottle.
reactions: A little goes a long way. In this case, a little goes too far.

entries.intro: We've been getting more travelers through here since the path got cleared after the rains.
details: Word must have spread that the route is passable again. Two carts in one week.
That used to be normal, but after months of nothing, two feels like a crowd.
reactions: I'd forgotten what a busy road sounds like. Cart wheels and conversation.

entries.intro: A merchant refuses to come here anymore because someone's dog bit them last time.
details: The dog barely nipped them. Didn't even break skin. But they've written us off entirely.
We lost a decent supplier over a dog that was just being a dog.
reactions: That dog has cost us more than it'll ever understand.

entries.intro: A traveler asked what we do here for entertainment. I pointed at the road and said we watch people like you walk by.
details: They laughed, but I wasn't entirely joking. The road is the closest thing we have to a stage.
Every cart that comes through is a little bit of the world arriving at our door.
reactions: You'd be surprised how entertaining a road can be if you pay attention.

entries.intro: A merchant brought thread dyed in colors I didn't know you could get from plants. Bright orange, deep purple.
details: They said the dyer uses minerals, not just plants. Something about soaking the thread in ground stone.
I bought the orange. I'm mending a shirt with it just because it makes me smile.
reactions: Color shouldn't matter as much as it does, but it does.

entries.intro: The last trader who came through said business is bad everywhere. I've heard that every season for years.
details: If business were actually bad, they'd stop trading. They're still showing up with full carts.
I think 'bad' means 'not as profitable as I'd like.' That's not the same thing.
reactions: Traders complaining about business is like farmers complaining about weather. It's just what they do.

statCheck.pass: A trader who says business is bad to every settlement is negotiating before you even start. If you think times are hard, you expect less and accept lower offers.
statCheck.fail: Maybe things really are bad. I've got no way to check.

entries.intro: A young traveler asked if they could apprentice here. Said they wanted to learn a trade, any trade.
details: We told them we don't have formal apprenticeships. You just help and learn as you go.
They stayed two days, helped in the fields, and left saying they'd think about coming back.
reactions: I hope they do come back. Fresh hands and fresh ideas would do us good.

entries.intro: The sacks the traders use have gotten thinner. I can see through the weave now.
details: Half the grain leaks out on the way home. I end up sweeping the path behind me.
Mentioned it to the trader and they said sacking is expensive. As if the grain isn't.
reactions: Saving on sacks and losing on grain. There's a word for that kind of economy.

entries.intro: A traveler drew a picture of the settlement as they saw it from the road. Gave it to us before leaving.
details: It's rough charcoal on paper, but you can see everything. The buildings, the fields, the smoke from the fires.
I've never seen this place from the outside before. It looks smaller than it feels.
reactions: Strange to see your own home through someone else's eyes.

entries.intro: {npc_name} got into an argument with a trader over the price of {resource_type} and nearly cost us the whole deal.
details: The trader was already loading up to leave before {npc_name} backed down.
We got the goods, but the trader left annoyed. That kind of thing doesn't get forgotten.
reactions: There's a difference between bargaining and picking a fight. Not everyone sees the line.

entries.intro: A merchant told me there's a new road being cut toward {other_settlement}. Straighter, wider.
details: If it takes traffic off the old path, we might see even fewer traders than we do now.
The old road bends through here. The new one won't.
reactions: Progress is wonderful until the new road doesn't pass through your settlement.

statCheck.pass: New roads are built to serve the largest settlements first. Places like ours only matter if we're between two big stops. If the new road bypasses us, we'll need to find another way to attract trade.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the route. Just know it's not coming through here.

entries.intro: A trader brought beeswax polish from {other_settlement}. Applied it to my table and the wood came alive.
details: The grain darkened and the whole surface has a warmth to it now. Like new furniture.
I've been polishing everything in the house. The chairs, the shelves, even the door.
reactions: It's just wax. But the difference it makes is hard to believe.

entries.intro: Two traders arrived on the same day and started undercutting each other's prices. Hasn't happened before.
details: We got {resource_type} for less than I've ever paid. Both of them were furious, but we made out well.
By the end they were barely speaking to each other.
reactions: Competition is wonderful when you're the one buying.

statCheck.pass: A price war between two traders only happens when both are desperate to move stock before it spoils or loses value. It's good for us today, but it means the supply chain is under pressure.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what drove it. Just enjoyed the prices while they lasted.

entries.intro: A traveler warned me about a merchant who short-changes settlements along this route.
details: They described the cart, the goods, even the merchant's manner of speaking.
It matched someone who came through here last month. I was one of the ones who got shorted.
reactions: At least now I know it wasn't my imagination.

statCheck.pass: Traveling cheats work the same route for a season and then switch regions before their reputation catches up. If this one's still working this area, they're running out of time.
statCheck.fail: I didn't get details beyond the description. Just enough to know to watch out.

entries.intro: We traded our surplus {resource_type} for lamp oil and soap. Felt like a good deal all around.
details: The trader even threw in a handful of tallow candles to sweeten it.
We shook hands and both walked away looking satisfied. That's how it should always go.
reactions: A fair trade leaves both sides feeling like they won. Anything else isn't really trade.

entries.intro: A stranger asked if we'd seen another traveler. Tall, red cloak, carrying a staff. We hadn't.
details: They seemed worried but wouldn't say why. Just thanked us and kept moving.
I watched them take the south road. Walked fast, didn't look back.
reactions: People pass through with their own troubles. All you can do is answer their questions and step aside.

entries.intro: The dried meat from the latest shipment was so salty I thought I was eating a salt lick.
details: They over-cured it. One bite and you're reaching for water.
I soaked a piece overnight and it was still too salty. The whole batch is like that.
reactions: It'll last forever, I'll give it that. Whether anyone will eat it is another question.

entries.intro: A trader brought news that {other_settlement} is growing. Building new structures, taking on more people.
details: They said the roads leading in are better maintained and there's more foot traffic every month.
Meanwhile we're the same size we've been for as long as I can remember.
reactions: Good for them. I mean that, mostly.

statCheck.pass: Growing settlements pull trade away from smaller ones nearby. More people means more demand, which means traders adjust their routes to serve the bigger market first.
statCheck.fail: I don't follow what it means for us. Just know they're doing better.

entries.intro: A merchant gave me a tip. Never buy {resource_type} after a long dry spell. The quality drops.
details: Something about the dryness affecting how it's processed or stored. I didn't follow all of it.
But I tested it against what I bought last month and the difference was noticeable.
reactions: I'm collecting these little tips like they're coins. Each one saves me something eventually.

entries.intro: A traveler paid for lodging by telling stories all evening. I'd have paid them to keep going.
details: They'd been everywhere, or at least they talked like it. Every settlement had a tale.
The children didn't want to go to bed. I didn't either, honestly.
reactions: A good storyteller is worth more than they know. Especially in a place where nothing happens.

entries.intro: The merchant's cart broke an axle right in front of my door. They were stuck here for two days while it got fixed.
details: We don't have a proper wheelwright, so it took longer than it should have.
They were antsy about the delay but couldn't do much besides wait and pace.
reactions: Two days of accidental company. Could've been worse. They were decent enough.

entries.intro: I asked a merchant how much they mark up {resource_type} between where they buy and where they sell. They wouldn't answer.
details: Just smiled and changed the subject. That tells me everything I need to know.
I'm not even angry about it. They have costs and risks. But the silence says the margin is fat.
reactions: Everybody deserves to make a living. I just wish I knew the numbers.

statCheck.pass: Most traveling traders work on a forty to sixty percent margin after costs. The rest goes to road time, animal feed, spoilage, and protection. They earn it, but they earn well.
statCheck.fail: I have no idea what the real margins are. They guard those numbers like treasure.

entries.intro: A traveler left behind a worn pair of sandals. Good leather, just needed new laces.
details: I relaced them with some cord I had lying around. They fit perfectly.
Been wearing them around the house. Most comfortable thing I've put on my feet.
reactions: One person's castoff is another person's best shoes.

entries.intro: A trader told me we're the last stop on their route before they turn around. The end of the line.
details: Everything past us isn't worth the trip, they said. Not enough people, not enough demand.
