We're the boundary between 'trade route' and 'empty road.'
reactions: Being the last stop is better than being past it.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} brought soap that actually lathers. The stuff we make barely bubbles.
details: It's the fat they use, apparently. Ours is too hard, theirs is rendered differently.
I used a piece and my hands felt clean for the first time in a while. Properly clean.
reactions: You don't notice what you're missing until someone shows you what good looks like.

entries.intro: A traveler tried to pay with coins from somewhere nobody here has heard of. Looked at us like we were the problem.
details: The coins were real enough, heavy metal with a face I didn't recognize.
We told them we'd take goods in trade instead, and they grudgingly handed over some dried fish.
reactions: If your money only works in one place, it's not really money. It's a souvenir.

entries.intro: A merchant brought glue. Strong glue, the kind that holds wood to wood permanently.
details: I'd been holding a chair together with cord for months. One application and it's solid again.
Bought an extra pot for future repairs. You never know when something will need fixing.
reactions: It's never the dramatic goods that matter most. It's glue and nails and cord.

entries.intro: The {resource_type} prices just went up again. Third time this year.
details: The trader blamed the roads, same as last time. The roads haven't changed.
What's changed is there's nobody else selling, so they charge what they want.
reactions: When there's only one seller, the word 'price' is just a polite way of saying 'demand.'

statCheck.pass: A sole supplier raising prices three times in a year isn't responding to costs. They're testing how high they can go before we stop buying. The answer is high, because we need the goods.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the economics. I just know it hurts more every time.

entries.intro: A pair of travelers are mapping the region. They asked about distances between settlements.
details: I told them what I know, which isn't much beyond the day-counts I've heard from traders.
They wrote it all down carefully, compared it to their notes, and seemed pleased.
reactions: If someone puts us on a proper map, maybe more traders will know we exist.

entries.intro: A trader showed me a trick for waterproofing bags with rendered fat. Simple but effective.
details: You rub it into the weave and let it dry in the sun. The fat seals the fibers.
I tried it on a grain sack and it kept dry through two days of rain.
reactions: The useful knowledge in this world travels on foot, one conversation at a time.

entries.intro: A merchant tried to sell me {resource_type} that smelled off. Wouldn't take no for an answer.
details: I said no three times and they kept lowering the price like that would fix the smell.
Finally they moved on. Probably sold it to someone less stubborn down the road.
reactions: A lower price doesn't make bad goods good. It just makes them cheaper bad goods.

entries.intro: A traveler brought a game. Small stones on a scratched board. Taught me the rules in ten minutes.
details: It's deceptively simple. Move your stones, block the other player, get across first.
I lost eight times before I won once. They said that's fast for a beginner.
reactions: I scratched my own board that evening. Now I just need someone to play against.

entries.intro: We got a delivery of nails and half of them are bent. Not from shipping. They were bent before they were packed.
details: Someone sold their rejects and the trader didn't check before buying.
I'm straightening what I can on the anvil, but it's slow work.
reactions: Every bent nail I straighten is time I should be spending on something else.

entries.intro: A traveler from {other_settlement} brought word that prices there are lower than here for almost everything.
details: They listed off what things cost and I had to stop them. It was making me angry.
Closer to the source means lower prices. We all know it. Hearing the numbers just makes it sting.
reactions: Location is destiny when it comes to trade. We drew the short straw.

entries.intro: A merchant noticed {npc_name} has a talent for mending things. Asked if they'd consider traveling to repair goods along the route.
details: {npc_name} fixed the merchant's broken harness in half the time anyone expected.
The offer was serious. Good coin, steady work, but it means leaving.
reactions: I hope they stay. But I understand why they'd go.

entries.intro: A traveler told me the weather toward {other_settlement} has been dry for months. Wells running low.
details: They said the crops look thin and people are rationing water already.
Not our problem directly, but if their harvest fails, the prices we pay go up.
reactions: Everything connects. Their dry season becomes our expensive winter.

statCheck.pass: When a producing region dries out, the supply shortage takes about two months to reach markets. By then the traders have already adjusted their prices upward. If you need to stock up, do it now before the increase hits.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how their problems affect our prices exactly. Just a feeling things will get tighter.

entries.intro: The charcoal from the last trader burns hotter and cleaner than anything I've made. Even and steady.
details: I asked how they make it and they said it's all in the kiln and the wood type.
I've been burning scraps and mixed wood. Apparently the good charcoal comes from hardwood only.
reactions: I've been doing it wrong for years and calling the result normal.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've visited twenty settlements and ours is the quietest. Not the smallest, just the quietest.
details: They said it like it was a compliment. I think they meant it that way.
We're not loud people. Never have been.
reactions: Quiet is underrated. You can hear yourself think here, and that's worth something.

entries.intro: A merchant offered to buy our clay. Apparently the color is unusual and potters in {other_settlement} want it.
details: We've been walking on it and building with it for years. Never thought of it as something to sell.
They said they'd come back with proper containers if we're willing to dig and pack it.
reactions: If someone wants to buy our dirt, I say let them.

statCheck.pass: Specialty clay can be worth more per weight than most raw goods. If the color is rare, potters will pay a premium because the fired product sells to wealthy customers.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what makes our clay special. It's just clay to me.

entries.intro: A trader left us a broken scale. Said it was no good to them anymore.
details: Turned out the arm was just bent. I straightened it and now it works fine.
First time we've had our own scale. Changes every negotiation.
reactions: The best tool I own is the one someone else threw away.

entries.intro: A traveler swore the road between here and {other_settlement} used to be shorter. Said it's grown somehow.
details: I told them roads don't grow. They said their feet disagree.
They might just be getting older. I wouldn't say that to their face, though.
reactions: The road hasn't changed. The legs carrying you over it have.

entries.intro: The last merchant tried to sell me the same {resource_type} they sold me last time, claiming it was a new lot.
details: I recognized the mark I'd scratched on the bag when I returned it for being poor quality.
They turned red and suddenly had somewhere else to be.
reactions: Marking your returns is the cheapest insurance there is.

statCheck.pass: Recycling returned goods back to the same buyer is a trick that works on anyone who doesn't label. The settlement that starts marking bags trains its traders to be honest.
statCheck.fail: I just got lucky that I'd scratched the bag. Won't count on luck next time.

entries.intro: A traveler wanted to trade stories for supper. Said they'd been collecting them from every settlement they visited.
details: I told them about the time a goat ate someone's laundry. They laughed so hard they choked on their bread.
In return they told me about a settlement where everyone names their chickens. Every single one.
reactions: Stories cost nothing to give and they make the evening shorter.

entries.intro: The rope from the last shipment is rough and stiff. Tears up your hands if you work with it bare.
details: Good rope should be tight-braided and supple. This stuff frays if you look at it wrong.
I wrapped my hands in cloth to use it. Shouldn't have to do that with new rope.
reactions: Bad rope is worse than no rope. At least with no rope you know where you stand.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} brought fabric I've never felt before. Soft as water, light as air.
details: They called it something I can't pronounce. Said it takes months to weave a single length.
I couldn't afford any, but I held it for a minute and that was something.
reactions: There are people in the world making things that beautiful, and here I am patching the same shirt.

entries.intro: A trader's guard dog got loose and chased our chickens. Lost three to sheer panic before they caught it.
details: The dog didn't kill any. The chickens ran into things trying to escape.
The trader offered to pay for the birds. At least they were honest about it.
reactions: Three chickens for a lesson in keeping dogs on leads. Expensive lesson.

entries.intro: A traveler mentioned that {other_settlement} has started using clay tokens instead of coins for small trades.
details: Each token is stamped with a value and backed by the settlement's stores.
Sounds clever until you think about what happens when you leave that settlement and the tokens mean nothing.
reactions: Every place invents its own way to complicate something simple.

statCheck.pass: Local tokens work when trade is mostly internal. The moment you need goods from outside, you need something the outsider values. Tokens lock you into your own economy.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand how their tokens work. Our way is simpler.

entries.intro: A passing trader was kind enough to sharpen our tools while their animal rested. Wouldn't take payment.
details: Said they carried a whetstone for their own gear and it was no trouble to do ours too.
The difference is noticeable. Everything cuts cleaner now.
reactions: Small kindnesses from strangers are the ones that stick with you.

entries.intro: A merchant told me our settlement has a reputation for being hard to find. That's not a selling point.
details: No markers, no signs, no clear path from the main road. Traders have to ask around.
The ones who bother to find us are the dedicated ones. The rest just pass by.
reactions: We could fix that with a few signs. But nobody's gotten around to it.

entries.intro: A traveler asked if I'd ever left this settlement. When I said no, they looked baffled.
details: To them, staying in one place your whole life is the strangest choice imaginable.
To me, having no place to stay is the strange one.
reactions: Some people are roots and some are wind. Neither one is wrong.

entries.intro: The tin cups a merchant brought are thin as paper. I bent one just picking it up.
details: They said the thinness makes them lighter for travel. Light is one thing, useless is another.
I dented it pouring water in. Actual water.
reactions: I'll stick with my heavy cup. At least it survives being used.

entries.intro: A traveler taught me a knot I'd never seen. Holds tight under load and slips free with one pull.
details: They said every sailor knows it but nobody inland does. Took me four tries to learn.
I've been retying everything with it. Rope work is better when the knots actually work.
reactions: The most useful things I know came from people who were here for an hour.

entries.intro: The last trader came through at night. Knocked on doors asking to buy anything we had.
details: They said they'd been delayed and needed to restock before the next stretch of road.
We sold them what we could spare. Wasn't much, but they were grateful.
reactions: A late knock and a desperate buyer. Makes you realize our surplus is someone else's necessity.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said our eggs taste better than theirs. Richer yolks.
details: Probably because our chickens eat insects and wild greens. Theirs eat milled feed.
They bought every egg we could spare and packed them more carefully than I've ever seen.
reactions: Nice to be better at something, even if it's just eggs.

entries.intro: A trader's cart had a wobble in the wheel. You could hear it coming from half a mile away.
details: Thump, thump, thump on every rotation. Like a slow drumbeat getting louder.
They seemed unbothered by it. Said it's been like that for weeks.
reactions: Some people fix problems. Others just get used to them.

entries.intro: A merchant sold us fish packed in salt from the coast. Haven't had fish in months.
details: The salt was caked on thick, but underneath the flesh was firm and pink.
We split it among three families. It was gone in a single evening.
reactions: A taste of something different breaks the routine in the best way.

entries.intro: A traveler warned us that the bridge toward {other_settlement} has been narrowing from erosion.
details: They said a loaded cart barely fits now. Give it another season and it won't fit at all.
Nobody's maintaining it because nobody claims it as theirs.
reactions: Shared infrastructure that nobody owns is infrastructure that falls apart.

entries.intro: A merchant and I couldn't agree on a price, so we split the difference and both felt cheated.
details: They thought I was lowballing. I thought they were gouging. We met in the middle and shook hands.
A deal where nobody's happy is still a deal, I suppose.
reactions: Maybe that's what fair actually looks like. Both sides a little disappointed.

entries.intro: A traveler said the bread in {other_settlement} is made with a grain we don't have here. Dark, heavy loaves.
details: They carried a piece and let me try it. Dense, chewy, fills you up fast.
We don't grow that grain. Our soil isn't right for it.
reactions: Every settlement has something we don't, and we have things they don't. That's why trade exists.

entries.intro: I caught a traveler taking fruit from a tree near the road without asking. They didn't think it belonged to anyone.
details: Where they're from, roadside trees are common property. Here, that's someone's tree.
I didn't make a fuss. Just told them for next time.
reactions: Different rules in different places. Can't blame someone for not knowing ours.

entries.intro: The needles a trader brought are the finest I've worked with. Thin as a hair and smooth through the cloth.
details: I've been sewing with thick, rough ones my whole life. These glide.
I bought six and I'm guarding them like they're made of silver.
reactions: Good tools make you realize how much effort you've been wasting with bad ones.

entries.intro: The merchant's animal was limping when they arrived. They pushed it too hard on the last stretch.
details: We gave the animal water and rest while the merchant traded. It looked grateful, if an animal can look grateful.
They left the next morning and the animal was still favoring the leg.
reactions: Overworking your animal saves you an hour and costs you a week. Everyone learns that the hard way.

entries.intro: A traveler told me a shortcut to {other_settlement} that saves half a day. I've lived here my whole life and never knew.
details: The path is narrow and overgrown, but it follows a ridge that avoids the low boggy stretch.
I walked it myself the next day. It's real. Half a day, just like they said.
reactions: All this time I've been taking the long way and calling it the only way.

entries.intro: A trader said they carry a different set of goods depending on the season. We always see their winter stock.
details: In summer they carry light fabrics and preserved fruits. We get the heavy wool and salted meat.
I asked if they could bring summer stock our way sometime. They said the route doesn't allow it.
reactions: We're a winter stop on someone else's calendar. Never thought about it that way.

entries.intro: A merchant noticed I was limping and gave me a salve from their personal stock. Wouldn't take anything for it.
details: Said it was made from a plant that grows near {other_settlement}. Smells terrible but works fast.
My knee felt better by evening. First time in weeks I could bend it without wincing.
reactions: Kindness from a stranger stays with you longer than a good deal.

entries.intro: The last shipment of {resource_type} arrived wet. Water damage through the whole lot.
details: The trader blamed a river crossing. I blame whoever packed it without waterproofing.
Salvaged what I could, but at least a third is ruined.
reactions: I'm paying full price for two-thirds of a delivery. The math doesn't improve with frustration.

statCheck.pass: Water-damaged goods at full price is the trader shifting their loss onto you. A fair trader would discount proportionally. If they won't, they know you can't go elsewhere.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what the fair price should be for damaged stock. I just know full price isn't it.

entries.intro: A traveler said they heard of us by name in {other_settlement}. Someone mentioned our settlement specifically.
details: They couldn't remember the context. Whether it was good or bad. Just that the name came up.
The fact that anyone out there knows we exist was enough to make my day.
reactions: Being known, even a little, makes a place feel less invisible.

entries.intro: A merchant asked us to store a crate for the next trader coming through. A relay system, they called it.
details: We hold the crate, the next trader picks it up and carries it onward. Saves the first trader from backtracking.
They paid us a small fee for the storage. Not much, but it was the easiest coin we've made.
reactions: If we became a regular relay point, that's a service we could build on.

statCheck.pass: Relay stations earn small fees from many traders over time. The real value isn't the fee. It's that every trader has a reason to stop here. That drives other business.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if it'll happen again. But the idea makes sense.

entries.intro: A traveler ate with us and said our food is too plain. No offense meant, they said. Offense taken anyway.
details: We eat what we grow. If it's not seasoned to their liking, that's not our failing.
They suggested we trade for spices. With what surplus, I'd like to know.
reactions: I'll season my food when my belly's full enough to care about taste.

entries.intro: The wax from the last merchant seals better than anything we've had. Jars stay airtight for months.
details: It melts clean, doesn't crack when it cools, and peels off in one piece when you're ready to open.
I asked the trader where it's from and they said a beekeeper near {other_settlement} who only sells through them.
reactions: Good sealing wax is one of those things you never think about until the bad stuff lets air in and your stores go rotten.

entries.intro: A trader passed through carrying nothing but empty crates. Heading somewhere to fill them, they said.
details: An empty cart is an unusual sight. It means someone made a deal in advance and is going to collect.
They moved fast, didn't stop to trade. Had somewhere specific to be.
reactions: An empty cart with a destination is worth more than a full one without.

entries.intro: The iron fittings from the latest trader are already rusting. They've been in my workshop for a week.
details: I oiled them the day I got them, but the rust was already starting underneath.
Low-quality smelt. The iron has too many impurities and holds moisture.
reactions: Iron that rusts in a week isn't iron. It's disappointment in a useful shape.

statCheck.pass: High-impurity iron comes from poorly maintained smelters that rush the process. It's cheaper for the smith, profitable for the trader, and worthless for the buyer within a season.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why some iron holds and some doesn't. I just know which batch I got.

entries.intro: A traveler said they always look forward to passing through here. Something about the view from the road as you approach.
details: They said the settlement looks peaceful from a distance. Smoke rising, fields laid out, small and tidy.
I've never approached from outside. I wonder what we look like to someone who doesn't live here.
reactions: Home probably looks different from every direction but the inside.

entries.intro: A merchant complained our road is the worst on their circuit. Every time, they lose something off the cart.
details: Jars crack, sacks shift, ropes come loose over the rough patches.
They said they add extra padding just for our stretch. That padding costs, and it goes into our prices.
reactions: We're paying for a bad road twice. Once in broken goods, once in higher prices.

entries.intro: A traveler saw me mending a fence and offered to help. Just walked up and started working.
details: Didn't introduce themselves, didn't ask. Just grabbed the other end of the post and held it steady.
We finished in half the time. They nodded, I nodded, and they walked on.
reactions: Didn't even get their name. Some of the best help comes without one.

entries.intro: A trader told me we overvalue our {resource_type}. Said nobody else charges this much.
details: I told them our quality is better. They said quality doesn't matter if nobody buys.
We compromised, but I didn't like it. Feels like we're selling ourselves short.
reactions: Knowing your worth and getting your price are two different things.

statCheck.pass: Traders who tell you you're overpriced are negotiating. If your goods genuinely weren't worth it, they wouldn't bother bargaining. They'd just leave.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they're right. Maybe we are asking too much.

entries.intro: The tinker who comes through twice a year sharpened every blade in the settlement. Even the kitchen knives.
details: Sets up a grinding wheel on the back of their cart and works through every tool brought to them.
Charges almost nothing. Says the volume makes up for the low rate.
reactions: Twice a year isn't enough. But it's twice more than nobody.

entries.intro: A merchant told me there's talk in {other_settlement} of forming a merchant guild to standardize prices.
details: If that happens, the bargaining stops. Whatever the guild sets, that's what we pay.
Right now at least we can negotiate. A guild takes that away.
reactions: Organized sellers and disorganized buyers. I can guess who wins.

statCheck.pass: A merchant guild fixes prices at whatever the strongest traders want. The small independents get forced to match or leave. Buyers in small settlements lose what little leverage they had.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand guilds well enough to know how it would hit us.

entries.intro: A traveler's child picked wildflowers and gave them to me while the parents traded. Made my whole week.
details: A handful of weeds, technically. But the child arranged them carefully and presented them like a treasure.
I put them in a cup of water on the table. They lasted three days.
reactions: The best gifts cost nothing and come from people who don't know any better.

entries.intro: A trader said they've been doing this route for fifteen years. Same road, same settlements, same goods.
details: I asked if they ever get tired of it. They said the road changes enough to keep it interesting.
Different weather, different people, same path. I suppose that's how they see it.
reactions: Fifteen years of the same route. In a way, they're as rooted as we are. Just moving roots.

entries.intro: A merchant delivered twice the amount of {resource_type} we ordered. Swears we asked for this much.
details: We didn't. But arguing with someone who's already unloaded doesn't get you far.
We kept the extra and paid for it. Now we've got more than we need and thinner coin.
reactions: Next time I'm writing the order down and making them sign it.

statCheck.pass: Over-delivery and insistence is a trick. Deliver more than ordered, claim it was the agreement, and force the buyer to pay or send it back at their own cost. Most people just pay.
statCheck.fail: Could have been a real mistake. Doesn't matter now. We paid.

entries.intro: A traveler staying the night asked a hundred questions about how we do things here. Genuinely curious.
details: How we store grain, how we split water, how we decide who does what. Real questions about real work.
I don't think anyone's ever been that interested in the boring parts of our life before.
reactions: It's strange when someone treats your ordinary life as fascinating.

entries.intro: We got cheated on weight again. The {resource_type} bag was supposed to be full and it's a quarter short.
details: I weighed it on our own scale this time. No question. They're skimming.
The problem is they're our only source right now, so we either accept it or go without.
reactions: Being cheated by someone you can't replace is its own kind of helpless.

statCheck.pass: A trader who skims knowing you can't switch is extracting everything the monopoly allows. The only answer is a second supplier, and they know there isn't one.
statCheck.fail: I know the bag is light. I just don't have options.

entries.intro: A traveler fell asleep under a tree and missed the trader they were supposed to meet. Found them still snoring at noon.
details: They were furious with themselves. Kicked the tree, which didn't help anything.
The trader had come and gone. Left a note scratched in the dirt, 'waited, left.'
reactions: Missing a deal because of a nap. There's a parable in there somewhere.

entries.intro: A merchant brought dried herbs from {other_settlement} that smell like nothing I've encountered before.
details: Sharp and sweet at the same time. They said a pinch in hot water makes a drink that helps you sleep.
I tried it and slept deeper than I have in months.
reactions: If that trader comes back, I'm buying everything they have.

entries.intro: A merchant and I got into a disagreement over the count. I said twenty, they said eighteen.
details: We counted together, twice, out loud. It was nineteen. Neither of us was right.
We settled on nineteen and moved on. More time spent counting than the two items were worth.
reactions: The time you lose arguing over small amounts is worth more than the difference.

entries.intro: A traveler said they were heading somewhere they'd never been. No plan, no destination, just walking until something felt right.
details: They had enough supplies for a week and a good pair of boots. That was their whole plan.
I couldn't decide if that was brave or foolish. Maybe it doesn't matter which.
reactions: There's a kind of freedom in not knowing where you're going. Not one I'd choose, but I understand the pull.

entries.intro: The potter's clay from {other_settlement} cracks in our kilns. Something about the moisture content.
details: It works fine there, but our fire runs hotter and drier. The clay can't take the difference.
We wasted a whole batch of pots before figuring that out.
reactions: Not everything that works somewhere else works here. Soil, fire, water. They're all local.

entries.intro: A merchant brought writing supplies. Ink, quills, and paper. Most of us don't read, but we bought them anyway.
details: I bought ink because I've always wanted to try drawing. Never had the means before.
The paper feels too precious to use. I keep picking it up and putting it back down.
reactions: Having the tools without the skill. I suppose that's where everyone starts.

entries.intro: Two travelers got into a shouting match in front of us over who had the right to sell {resource_type} here first.
details: We just stood and watched. Nobody asked us which one we preferred.
The louder one won, if you can call it winning. The other left without selling anything.
reactions: When two sellers fight, the buyer should benefit. We didn't.

entries.intro: A trader told me their favorite part of the route is the stretch just before they reach us. Open sky, gentle slope.
details: They said the light hits differently here in the afternoon. Something about the angle of the land.
I've never noticed because I'm always looking at the ground, not the sky.
reactions: Maybe I should look up more.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a sample of cheese from {other_settlement} that's hard as a rock but melts on the tongue.
details: Aged for over a year, they said. The taste is sharp and deep, nothing like our soft cheese.
I asked how they make it and they said it's the same milk, just more patience.
reactions: Patience turns ordinary ingredients into something worth trading for. I should remember that.

entries.intro: {npc_name} has been trading with every passing merchant, stockpiling things nobody asked for.
details: Sacks of this, bundles of that. Half of it is piled up with no clear purpose.
When I asked what it's all for, they said they're thinking ahead. Ahead to what, I'd like to know.
reactions: Either they're the smartest person here or the biggest hoarder. Time will tell.

statCheck.pass: Hoarding trade goods before others catch on is either speculation or paranoia. If {npc_name} has heard something about supply drying up, the stockpile makes sense.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they're up to. Just seems like a lot of spending for no clear reason.

entries.intro: A trader admitted they overcharged us last time. Gave us a discount this visit to make up for it.
details: I didn't even remember the overcharge. They could have said nothing and I'd never have known.
The discount wasn't huge, but the honesty was worth more than the coin.
reactions: That trader just bought loyalty that'll last longer than any single sale.

entries.intro: A traveler walked in barefoot. Said their boots fell apart two days ago and they've been walking on cloth wraps since.
details: Their feet were raw. You could see blisters through the wrapping.
We didn't have spare boots, but we gave them thicker cloth and some grease to keep the wet out.
reactions: Walking barefoot for two days because you can't stop. That's the real cost of the road.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} wanted to buy our scrap wood. All of it.
details: We've been burning it or leaving it to rot. They said in their settlement firewood is scarce.
We loaded their cart with everything we didn't need and they paid fair coin for it.
reactions: Our waste is someone else's shortage. Strange how trade fills the gaps.

entries.intro: A trader tried to haggle with me using charm instead of numbers. All smiles and compliments.
details: They said I had good taste, sharp eyes, a keen sense of value. I said the price is the price.
The smile dimmed a little after that. They paid what I asked.
reactions: Flattery is free, which is exactly what it's worth in a trade.

entries.intro: We've been getting more travelers heading away from {other_settlement} than toward it. Makes you wonder.
details: Nobody's said anything specific, but the direction of foot traffic tells a story.
People leave places for reasons, even if they don't share them with strangers.
reactions: I'm not going to worry about somewhere else's problems. We've got our own.

statCheck.pass: When foot traffic reverses direction from a settlement, it usually means work dried up. People don't leave stable situations. The ones walking are the ones who couldn't afford to stay.
statCheck.fail: Could be anything. I'm just watching the road like I always do.

entries.intro: A traveler carried a small wooden box with a compass inside. Showed me how it works.
details: A needle floating on water, always pointing the same direction. I'd heard of them but never seen one.
They said it's saved them from getting lost more times than they can count.
reactions: A needle that knows where it's going. More than I can say for most of us.

entries.intro: A trader who hasn't been through in over a year showed up like nothing happened. Same cart, same goods.
details: I asked where they'd been and they said 'around.' A whole year summed up in one word.
Prices are higher than when they left, but at least they're back.
reactions: A returning trader is better than a new one. At least you know what you're getting.

entries.intro: A merchant brought seeds they claim grow twice as fast as what we plant now. I'm skeptical.
details: Fast-growing usually means shallow roots or poor flavor. Nature doesn't give something for nothing.
I bought a small handful to test. If they work, I'll eat my words. If they don't, I've wasted a handful of coin.
reactions: Promises that sound too good usually are.

statCheck.pass: Fast-growing seed strains often sacrifice disease resistance for speed. One bad season and you lose the whole crop. The safe money is on tested seed, even if it's slower.
statCheck.fail: I'll know in a few weeks whether it was worth it.

entries.intro: A merchant's loaded cart spooked our goats and they scattered. Took an hour to round them up.
details: Something on the cart was clanking. Loose pots, I think. The noise set them off.
One goat got halfway to the next field before we caught it.
reactions: Goats don't forget a scare. They'll be jumpy for days.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} said they might start including us on their regular route. We'd be a monthly stop.
details: It depends on whether they can fit us in without adding too much travel time.
We'd know when to expect them and could plan our needs in advance.
reactions: A monthly trader would change everything. I'm trying not to get my hopes up.

entries.intro: A traveler sat with me and said nothing for a long time. Just sat and watched the road together.
details: After a while they said this is the first time they'd sat still in two weeks.
We didn't need to talk. Sometimes company is enough.
reactions: Silence between two people who don't know each other can be surprisingly comfortable.

entries.intro: The pine tar a merchant brought smells terrible but works beautifully for waterproofing.
details: I sealed the underside of the storage shed with it. Took a whole day and my clothes still smell.
But the shed is dry for the first time in years.
reactions: The best materials are rarely pleasant to work with.

entries.intro: A merchant's scales tipped differently depending on whether they were buying or selling. I watched carefully.
details: When they bought from us, the scale read light. When they sold to us, it read heavy.
I pointed it out and they acted shocked. Adjusted it, apologized, kept going.
reactions: Shocked. Right. That scale has been 'broken' at every stop on the route, I'm sure.

statCheck.pass: A scale that reads differently both ways is rigged with a hidden weight that shifts. The merchant tips the pan one way when buying, the other when selling. It's deliberate.
statCheck.fail: I saw what I saw. Can't prove anything beyond that.

entries.intro: A traveler brought their elderly parent along on the road. Said they couldn't leave them behind.
details: The old one moved slowly, but the traveler matched their pace without complaint.
They rested here longer than most. The parent looked tired but content.
reactions: Taking the slow road because someone you love needs the pace. That's not burden, that's family.

entries.intro: A merchant left behind a small jar of rendered tallow. It's been the best thing for cracked hands I've ever used.
details: Mixed with something I can't identify. Herbal, maybe. It soaks in and doesn't stay greasy.
My knuckles haven't cracked once since I started using it.
reactions: I'll barter hard for another jar if that merchant ever comes back.

entries.intro: A trader complained that settlements keep asking for credit. Nobody wants to pay on delivery anymore.
details: They said half their route owes them and some of it they'll never collect.
I paid upfront, as always. They looked at me like I'd given them a gift.
reactions: Paying what you owe when you owe it. Shouldn't be remarkable, but apparently it is.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've never been robbed on this stretch of road. Said it's one of the safest they travel.
details: I don't know if that's because the road is safe or because there's nothing worth stealing from anyone on it.
Either way, it's a compliment I'll take.
reactions: Safe is safe. I don't need to know the reason.

entries.intro: The last delivery included something we didn't order. A sack of dried mushrooms. Nobody wants them.
details: They're gritty and taste like bark. I tried cooking them three different ways.
The trader must have thrown them in to get rid of stock. We're stuck with them now.
reactions: Free goods you don't want aren't free. They're clutter with an excuse.

entries.intro: A traveler asked me what the best thing about living here is. I said the silence after the last cart leaves.
details: They laughed, but I meant it. The quiet after the road empties is the best part of the day.
When everything's settled and it's just us again, that's when I feel at home.
reactions: I like visitors. I like it more when they go.

entries.intro: A merchant brought us fresh fruit that's already going soft. We have maybe two days before it turns.
details: They should've sold it three stops ago. By now it's a race against rot.
We're eating fast and preserving what we can. The kitchen smells incredible at least.
reactions: Fruit on borrowed time is still fruit. Just means dessert comes before dinner.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they once lived in a settlement like ours. Gave it up to walk the roads.
details: Said the first year was the hardest. After that, the road became home.
They didn't seem sad about it. Just different. Like someone who's solved a problem a different way.
reactions: Two people can look at the same life and one calls it everything, the other calls it not enough.

entries.intro: A trader brought buttons. Simple bone buttons, but ours have all been breaking and we needed them.
details: Smooth, thick, with clean holes. The kind that last years instead of cracking after a season.
I bought a handful and replaced every button on my good coat.
reactions: A coat with good buttons feels like a different coat entirely.

entries.intro: We're sending more goods out than we're bringing in this season. First time that's happened.
details: It's not much surplus, but it's ours. Actual output that someone else wants enough to pay for.
I checked the tallies twice because I didn't believe it the first time.
reactions: A settlement that produces more than it consumes has a future. I think we're getting there.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they keep a mental list of every settlement where they've been treated badly. We're not on it.
details: The ones on the list get worse prices, fewer visits, and the oldest stock.
They said our settlement treats traders fair and that earns us first pick of the goods.
reactions: Reputation travels the road faster than any cart. Good thing ours is decent.

statCheck.pass: Every trader keeps a list, even if they don't say so. Fair dealing, honest counts, water for the animals. Those small things determine what you get offered and what skips you entirely.
statCheck.fail: I'll take their word for it. Being liked by the people who feed you isn't a bad position.

entries.intro: A traveler gave me a coin from somewhere I've never heard of. I'm keeping it as a curiosity.
details: It's a strange metal, lighter than iron but darker than bronze. The face on it wears a crown I don't recognize.
They said it's worth something where they come from. Here it's just a pretty disc.
reactions: I turn it over in my hand sometimes and wonder about the place it came from.

entries.intro: The last shipment of {resource_type} from {other_settlement} was perfect. Not a single complaint from anyone.
details: Right weight, right quality, packed properly, delivered on time. Everything we asked for.
I almost didn't know what to do with myself. I'd prepared all my usual complaints.
reactions: A perfect delivery is so rare it feels like a mistake.

entries.intro: A merchant said the most profitable thing we could do is grow something nobody else in the region grows.
details: They suggested a few things, but every option needs soil conditions or tools we don't have.
It's easy advice from someone who doesn't have to do the planting.
reactions: We'll grow what grows. Let the merchants figure out how to sell it.

statCheck.pass: Specialization advice from traders is self-serving. They want unique product they can mark up at other stops. The settlement that grows what the trader asks for is farming for the trader's profit, not their own.
statCheck.fail: I don't have the farming knowledge to evaluate what they're suggesting.

entries.intro: A traveler told me their whole life fits in the pack on their back. Everything they own.
details: I looked at my house full of things and wondered which of them I'd actually carry.
The answer was fewer than I expected.
reactions: Owning things is easy. Knowing which ones matter is the hard part.

entries.intro: A trader told me they're skipping {other_settlement} on their next circuit. Didn't say why.
details: They just crossed it off their route like it wasn't worth discussing.
That means one fewer source for anything they used to carry from there.
reactions: When a trader drops a stop, they know something we don't.

statCheck.pass: Traders cut stops when the margin disappears. Either the settlement stopped buying or the roads got bad enough to cost more than the profit.
statCheck.fail: Not my route, not my problem. But it makes you think.

entries.intro: The rope we got from the last trader is already fraying. Barely held a week.
details: I tied a simple knot and the fibers split under tension. It's not rope, it's string pretending.
We needed it for the well bucket. Now we're back to the old rope held together with prayer.
reactions: Cheap rope is the most expensive thing you can buy.

entries.intro: A merchant gave my kid a carved wooden horse. Just handed it over and said 'for the little one.'
details: It's the nicest toy the kid's ever had. Smooth, painted, with a little saddle and everything.
They said they carve them on the road to pass the time. Must have a whole cart of them.
reactions: That horse hasn't left my child's hand since. Worth more than anything on the cart.

entries.intro: Nobody's come through in over a week. Starting to wonder if the road is blocked somewhere.
details: Usually there's at least someone passing. A trader, a courier, a family moving on.
The silence makes you feel more cut off than you realize you are.
reactions: We'll manage like we always do. But I'd rather manage with traffic.

entries.intro: A traveler showed me a map that has our settlement on it. We're just a dot, but we're on there.
details: First time I've ever seen us written down anywhere. The name was spelled wrong, but still.
The traveler said the map was made by someone in {other_settlement} who charts trade routes.
reactions: A dot on a map. Feels more real than it should.

entries.intro: A trader tried to sell me {resource_type} that was damp. You could feel the moisture through the wrapping.
details: They swore it was fine. I squeezed the sack and water ran out.
Damp {resource_type} is ruined {resource_type}. No amount of drying fixes what moisture starts.
reactions: I sent them on their way. Let the next settlement fall for it.

statCheck.pass: Damp goods packed tight means they got rained on and the trader repacked without drying. They're hoping nobody checks until they're a day down the road.
statCheck.fail: I just know wet when I feel it. Don't need to know why to say no.

entries.intro: A caravan driver told me the best settlements to trade with are the ones that wave you in, not the ones that watch you approach.
details: They said you can tell within ten seconds if a place is friendly or suspicious.
According to them, we're a waving settlement. I'll take that.
reactions: We wave because we want what's on the cart. But I'll pretend it's because we're nice.

entries.intro: The {resource_type} prices have dropped for the first time in seasons. I bought more than I needed.
details: The trader said there's a surplus right now and they need to move stock before it sits too long.
I filled every container I could find. If the price goes back up, I'll be glad I did.
reactions: Low prices don't last. I've learned that the expensive way.

statCheck.pass: A sudden surplus usually means a new source opened up or a big buyer cancelled an order. Either way, it's temporary. Once the market absorbs the excess, prices snap right back.
statCheck.fail: I don't question cheap prices. I just buy fast.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they'd been walking for three weeks straight. Their boots were worn through to nothing.
details: They sat down by our well and didn't move for two hours. Just sat there drinking water and staring at the sky.
When they finally stood up, they wobbled like they'd forgotten how to be still.
reactions: Three weeks of walking and nowhere to call done. The road doesn't owe you a destination.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} brought salt that actually tastes clean. Our usual supply has a gritty edge.
details: This salt dissolves clear with no sand and no bitterness. Just clean flavor.
It costs twice as much, but you use half the amount. Evens out in the end.
reactions: I didn't know salt could taste different until I tasted good salt.

entries.intro: A merchant claims traders from {other_settlement} are undercutting everyone on {resource_type}.
details: Selling at prices nobody else can match and still make a living.
The smaller traders are losing business because nobody will pay fair price when cheap is available.
reactions: Cheap today means no options tomorrow. But try telling that to someone counting their coin.

statCheck.pass: Undercutting is a tactic, not a kindness. Once the competition quits, the prices go right back up. The cheap supplier becomes the only supplier and sets whatever rate they want.
statCheck.fail: I don't follow the business side of it. Just know prices keep shifting.

entries.intro: A traveler asked if we'd consider sending someone to {other_settlement} to learn a trade there. An apprenticeship.
details: They said the smiths there are looking for willing hands. Room and board included.
We'd lose a pair of hands here but gain a skill that comes back eventually.
reactions: It's a big decision for a small settlement. One person leaving changes the balance.

statCheck.pass: Apprenticeship offers are genuine when the host settlement is short on labor and willing to train bodies to get them. The real question is whether the person comes back or finds a reason to stay.
statCheck.fail: I don't know enough about {other_settlement} to say if it's a real offer.

entries.intro: Two merchants arrived on the same day. Both carrying {resource_type}, both wanting to sell.
details: They didn't know each other was coming. You could see the moment they realized. Faces fell at the same time.
We played it straight and bought from both at the going rate. No sense taking advantage.
reactions: A rare day when the buyer has choices. Felt strange being on that side of it.

entries.intro: A trader's cart threw a wheel right outside the settlement. Took half a day to fix.
details: We helped because what else do you do. Lifted the axle, held the wheel while they hammered.
They gave us a jar of honey as thanks. Best honey I've had.
reactions: A broken wheel and a jar of honey. That's the trade economy at its simplest.

entries.intro: A merchant told me I drive the hardest bargain on their entire route. I choose to take that as a compliment.
details: They were smiling when they said it, but there was something behind the smile.
I just know what things are worth and I don't budge. That's not hard bargaining, it's fair bargaining.
reactions: If knowing what something costs makes me difficult, then I'm difficult.

entries.intro: The candles from {other_settlement} burn twice as long as the ones we make. I can't figure out what they do differently.
details: Same tallow, near as I can tell. But theirs hold a steady flame and ours gutter and drip.
A trader said it's in the wicking. Tighter twist, slower burn.
reactions: Small differences in how you make something add up to big differences in how long it lasts.

statCheck.pass: The trick isn't just the wick. They dip their candles multiple times in thin layers instead of pouring. Each layer hardens before the next, and the result burns even and slow.
statCheck.fail: I've tried copying what the trader described but mine still come out uneven.

entries.intro: A traveler told me people in {other_settlement} are paying triple for {resource_type}. Can't believe it.
details: If that's true, someone with a cart and a strong back could make a fortune in a single trip.
But it's a long haul and there's no guarantee the price holds by the time you get there.
reactions: Easy money always sounds easy until you try to collect it.

statCheck.pass: Triple prices mean either extreme shortage or someone's cornered the supply. If it's a shortage, the price corrects fast, but if it's cornered, an outsider walking in with stock isn't welcome.
statCheck.fail: Prices like that sound made up. But traders don't usually lie about numbers.

entries.intro: The last merchant smelled worse than anything on their cart. I traded fast just to get upwind.
details: Weeks on the road in the same clothes, I suppose. The road doesn't offer many baths.
Good goods though. I can hold my breath longer than I can hold off buying what we need.
reactions: Commerce has no nose. You buy what's useful regardless of who's selling it.

entries.intro: A traveler said our well water is the sweetest they've tasted on the road. Filled every flask they had.
details: I never thought about our water being special. It's just water.
But they drank deep and filled up like they were storing it for a week.
reactions: You stop noticing what you have when you've always had it.

entries.intro: A merchant left and we realized they'd shortchanged us on {resource_type} by at least a fifth.
details: Counted it that evening and the numbers just didn't match what we agreed on.
Gone by then, of course. They knew what they were doing.
reactions: We'll remember the face. That's the only ledger that matters out here.

statCheck.pass: Shorting the count on departure is easier than rigging a scale. You agree on a number, they unload fast, and by the time you count properly they're a day away. Always count before they leave.
statCheck.fail: I should have counted right there. My own fault as much as theirs.

entries.intro: A trader said they admire how we all pitch in when a cart arrives. Organized, they called it.
details: It's not organized. We just know who needs what and the unloading sorts itself.
Years of doing the same thing means you don't need a plan. Everyone already knows their part.
reactions: I suppose routine looks like organization to someone who doesn't live it.

entries.intro: A traveler left behind a cloak. Good wool, barely worn. Nobody came back for it.
details: We kept it by the road for a few days in case they returned. They didn't.
Now it's mine. Fits well enough and the wool is thicker than anything we make.
reactions: I feel a little guilty wearing someone else's cloak. But not guilty enough to stop.

entries.intro: The {resource_type} we got last month was fine, but this month's batch is garbage. Same trader, same price.
details: The quality swings wildly and the trader acts like we shouldn't notice.
I think they sell the good stock to {other_settlement} first and we get whatever's left.
reactions: Second pick isn't a deal. It's what's left after someone else chose.

statCheck.pass: Traders with multiple stops sort quality by buyer size. The biggest customer gets first pick, and the rest get grades they wouldn't sell to the first stop.
statCheck.fail: I just know some months are better than others and there's no pattern I can see.

entries.intro: A traveler's dog followed me around for a whole afternoon. The traveler didn't seem to mind.
details: Good dog. Calm, friendly, didn't beg. Just walked next to me like we'd known each other forever.
When they left, the dog looked back at me twice before following. That stuck with me.
reactions: I miss that dog more than I've missed most people who've passed through.

entries.intro: A trader offered to buy our old tools. Said they can resell broken things to settlements that have smiths to repair them.
details: We've been throwing out worn tools for years. Turns out they're worth something to the right buyer.
Didn't get much, but getting anything for what we'd have tossed feels like winning.
reactions: One settlement's scrap is another settlement's raw material. Trade is just moving things to where they're useful.

statCheck.pass: A trader buying scrap iron is paying us a fraction of what it's worth reforged. The smith at the next stop gets cheap stock, the trader takes a cut both ways, and we're happy with scraps for scrap.
statCheck.fail: I'm just glad someone wants what we were going to throw away.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} tried to pay us in promises. Said the coin was coming next trip.
details: We've heard that before. Next trip never comes, or it comes with excuses instead of payment.
I told them we trade for things we can hold. They looked annoyed but they found the coin.
reactions: A promise from a stranger on the road isn't currency. It's air.

statCheck.pass: A trader who offers credit to a small settlement is testing whether you'll accept it. Once you do, they own the leverage. They decide when to collect and what the terms become.
statCheck.fail: I just don't trust people who have the coin but want to hold onto it.

entries.intro: We've started keeping a tally of what we buy and what we sell. The numbers aren't encouraging.
details: We bring in more than we put out every single month. That gap doesn't close on its own.
The only question is whether we make up the difference in labor or go without.
reactions: Seeing it written down makes it harder to pretend we're doing fine.

statCheck.pass: A persistent trade deficit means the settlement is consuming its reserves slowly. If those reserves run out before output improves, the settlement starts losing people to places with more opportunity.
statCheck.fail: The numbers say one thing, but we're still here. So maybe numbers aren't everything.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they mark good resting spots on their map. Our settlement has a star next to it.
details: They said the star means reliable water, decent people, and no hassle.
I asked what the other marks mean and they said some settlements get a warning sign instead.
reactions: A star from a stranger feels better than any compliment from a neighbor.

entries.intro: The thread a merchant sold us is the wrong weight for our looms. Too thick for anything but patching.
details: They sold it as weaving thread. It's closer to cord.
We'll use it for mending, but that's not what we paid for.
reactions: Wrong thread, wrong gauge, wrong trader. At least the mending pile is getting smaller.

statCheck.pass: Thick thread sold as weaving thread is usually cord stock that didn't sell at the right settlement. The trader repackages it and calls it something else because lying is easier than hauling it back.
statCheck.fail: I don't know thread grades well enough to have caught it before buying.

entries.intro: A merchant told me the road between here and {other_settlement} is the loneliest stretch they travel.
details: No settlements in between, no waypoints, no other travelers. Just empty road.
They said they sing to themselves the whole way just to hear a voice.
reactions: I forget how isolated we are until someone describes the emptiness around us.

entries.intro: We pooled coin together and placed a bulk order for {resource_type}. First time we've done that.
details: Buying together means better rates. The trader was happier too. One big sale instead of ten small ones.
The hard part was agreeing on what to order. Everyone has opinions.
reactions: Working together to buy isn't natural for us. But the savings make the awkwardness worth it.

statCheck.pass: Collective buying gives a small settlement the same leverage a bigger one has through volume. The trick is keeping the group together when people disagree on what to order.
statCheck.fail: We saved some coin. That's all I know.

entries.intro: A traveler asked to trade labor for a meal. Worked harder in two hours than some people do in a day.
details: Split a cord of firewood, restacked a wall, and swept the common area. All before the food was ready.
I would have fed them anyway, but the pride they took in earning it was something else.
reactions: Some people refuse charity even when they need it. I respect that more than I should.

entries.intro: A merchant told me {other_settlement} has started taxing traders who pass through. Just for crossing their land.
details: A fee at the gate, another for water, another to use the road on the way out.
The merchant said they've raised all their prices along the route to cover it.
reactions: When one settlement squeezes traders, every settlement on the route pays for it.

statCheck.pass: Gate taxes drive traders to find alternate routes, and the settlement that charges them gets short-term coin and long-term isolation. The traders always have options, even if the route is longer.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the politics of it. I just know things cost more now.

entries.intro: A traveler complimented our fences. Said most small settlements don't bother keeping things neat.
details: I never thought of our fences as noteworthy. They're just fences.
But apparently neat fences tell a traveler that people here care about where they live.
reactions: Funny what strangers notice that we've stopped seeing.

entries.intro: The nails from the last shipment bend if you look at them wrong. Soft iron, barely tempered.
details: I hammered three into a post and two of them folded over. Had to pull them out and start again.
Good nails hold wood together. Bad nails just make holes.
reactions: I'd rather have half as many good nails as a bucket full of bad ones.

statCheck.pass: Soft nails come from a smith who's rushing the quench. They pull the iron from heat too soon to save fuel, and the nail never gets hard enough to hold in seasoned wood.
statCheck.fail: I know they're bad. Don't know enough about smithing to say why.

entries.intro: A merchant brought news that a new road is being cut toward {other_settlement}. Could change our traffic entirely.
details: If it goes through, we could end up on a main route instead of a side path.
Or we could end up bypassed entirely if the road goes around us.
reactions: A new road is a coin flip. Heads we grow, tails we shrink.

statCheck.pass: Road builders follow the flattest path, not the most populated one. If the terrain is easier on the other side of us, the road goes there and we become a dead end.
statCheck.fail: I don't know where they're building it. Just that they are.

entries.intro: A traveler asked how far the next settlement is and I honestly couldn't give them a good answer.
details: I said maybe two days, maybe three. Depends on the weather and how fast you walk.
They looked at me like I'd told them nothing. I suppose I hadn't.
reactions: We measure distance in days here. Nobody counts steps.

entries.intro: A trader from {other_settlement} brought dried fish packed in salt. Best preserved food we've had.
details: The salt kept it firm and the flavor was strong without being overpowering.
I asked their preservation method and they said it's just time, salt, and airflow.
reactions: Simple techniques done well. That's all the difference between lasting food and waste.

entries.intro: A merchant sold us {resource_type} that looked fine on top but was rotten underneath. Packed to deceive.
details: Good layers on top, garbage underneath. The kind of trick that takes effort to set up.
We didn't catch it until we'd already opened and sorted everything.
reactions: Packed to look good from the top. That takes more effort than just selling honest goods.

statCheck.pass: Layered packing is the oldest fraud in trade. Good on top, reject underneath. The only defense is to empty the entire lot before paying, because anyone who objects to that is hiding something.
statCheck.fail: I should have checked the whole bag. I know better than to trust the surface.

entries.intro: A pair of travelers stopped here, argued loudly about which direction to go, and then left the way they came.
details: Neither one wanted to admit they were lost. They just kept pointing different ways and getting louder.
I offered directions but they both ignored me.
reactions: Some people would rather be lost together than admit one of them is wrong.

entries.intro: We sent a letter with a trader heading to {other_settlement}. No idea if it'll arrive.
details: The trader said they'd deliver it, but letters are easy to forget when you've got a cart full of goods to manage.
I didn't even seal it well. The rain alone might finish it off before anyone reads it.
reactions: Sending a letter with a stranger and hoping. That's the postal system out here.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they've been trading for twenty years and this year is the hardest they've had.
details: Roads are worse, prices are higher, and fewer people are buying anything that isn't essential.
They said they're thinking about settling down. Just quitting the road entirely.
reactions: When even the traders are struggling, nobody's doing well.

statCheck.pass: A veteran trader talking about quitting means the margins have collapsed. Travel costs have risen faster than selling prices, and the road life only works when the numbers do.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they're just tired. Twenty years on the road would wear anyone out.

entries.intro: A traveler said we're the only settlement on the route where nobody tried to cheat them.
details: Honest weights, honest prices, honest counting. That's just how we do things.
They said it's rarer than you'd think.
reactions: If basic honesty is rare enough to comment on, that says more about the road than it does about us.

entries.intro: A trader's horse was lame when they arrived. They had to stay an extra day waiting for it to rest.
details: We fed and watered the horse. The trader sat around looking anxious the whole time.
Every day off the road is money lost, they said. The horse didn't care about the schedule.
reactions: A lame horse reminds you that trade runs on legs, not will.

statCheck.pass: A trader who pushes a horse to lameness is running on tighter margins than they let on. Healthy animals don't go lame on maintained routes unless they've been overworked.
statCheck.fail: I don't know horses well enough to say what happened. Just looked tired to me.

entries.intro: The leather we ordered from {other_settlement} finally arrived, and it's beautiful work.
details: Supple, even thickness, well-cured. You can smell the quality.
Worth every coin and the long wait.
reactions: When you finally get something good after waiting this long, you appreciate it properly.

entries.intro: A traveler asked why we don't have a market. I told them we have a table by the road.
details: They seemed confused by the idea that a table is enough. It is for us.
We put out what we're selling, travelers take what they want, and coin changes hands.
reactions: A market is just a table that got ideas about itself.

entries.intro: A merchant warned us that {resource_type} quality from most sources is dropping this season.
details: They said it's across the board, not just one supplier. Everyone's cutting corners.
The only option is to stockpile what's decent while it's still available.
reactions: Bad seasons pass. But the stock you're holding when things get worse is what carries you.

statCheck.pass: Broad quality drops usually trace back to a shared upstream cause. A bad harvest season for raw materials, or a sudden demand spike that pushes production faster than quality can keep up.
statCheck.fail: I just take what the trader tells me at face value. Don't know enough to question it.

entries.intro: A traveler shared a meal with us and told stories until the fire went out. Good stories too.
details: Funny ones, mostly. Places they'd been, people they'd met, mistakes they'd made.
Nobody wanted to be the one to say it was time to sleep.
reactions: A stranger with good stories is the best kind of entertainment we get out here.

entries.intro: A trader asked me to recommend them to anyone I meet heading toward {other_settlement}. Gave me a token to pass along.
details: A little stamped disc with their mark on it. Said anyone who brings it gets a discount.
I'm not sure how often I meet someone heading that way, but I'll hold onto it.
reactions: Smart way to drum up business. Make every customer a salesman.

entries.intro: We had a trader refuse to sell to us. Said we don't buy enough to make the stop worthwhile.
details: They looked at our list, looked at their cart, and said they'd lose money stopping here for that amount.
Rolled right on past without unloading a thing.
reactions: Being told you're not worth the stop. That sits with you for a while.

statCheck.pass: A trader who tells you they're skipping your stop is calculating that the time spent unloading, trading, and reloading costs more than the sale brings in. Below a certain volume, every stop is a loss.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the numbers. Just know we got passed over.

entries.intro: A merchant brought glass jars from somewhere far off. Clear, heavy, beautiful things.
details: I've never held glass this thick. You could drop it on packed earth and it might survive.
They cost more than the contents they'll hold, but they'll last for years.
reactions: Buying a container that outlasts everything you put in it. That's thinking ahead.

entries.intro: A traveler said the road here from {other_settlement} used to be twice as wide. You can see where the brush reclaimed the edges.
details: I've walked that road my whole life and never noticed. But once they pointed it out, you can see the old line.
Roads shrink when traffic drops. The land takes back what's not being used.
reactions: A shrinking road is a quiet warning that most people walk right past.

statCheck.pass: A road that was wider means this route once carried more traffic than it does now. That traffic went somewhere else, and wherever it went is probably thriving while we're not.
statCheck.fail: I never paid attention to road width. Just walk where it's flat.

entries.intro: A merchant refused my barter offer and then offered the same goods to {npc_name} at a better price. Right in front of me.
details: I offered fair value and they wouldn't budge. Then {npc_name} walks up and gets a deal I couldn't get.
I don't know what {npc_name} said that I didn't, but it burns.
reactions: Trade isn't fair. It's favorable, and some people are better at getting there.

statCheck.pass: {npc_name} probably offered something the trader actually wanted instead of something the trader could get anywhere. Barter isn't about equal value. It's about what the other person needs right now.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what {npc_name} has that I don't. Just bothers me.

entries.intro: A traveler told me the best thing about small settlements is that everyone remembers you. The worst thing is the same.
details: I laughed because it's true. We remember every traveler who passes through, good or bad.
The one who stiffed us on a deal three years ago? We still talk about them.
reactions: Memory is the only ledger we keep, and nobody gets erased.

entries.intro: We've started drying herbs to sell to traders. Small bags, simple product, but it's something we can produce.
details: The first batch sold immediately. The trader said dried herbs are light, easy to carry, and everyone wants them.
We're already drying a second batch.
reactions: A product that costs us nothing but time and we have plenty of that.

statCheck.pass: The markup on dried herbs between us and the final buyer is enormous. What we sell for a few coins, they sell for many times that. But it's still income we didn't have before.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they sell for down the road. We're just happy to have something to trade.

entries.intro: The merchant who usually brings our {resource_type} sent someone new instead. Never seen them before.
details: Same goods, same route, but the new person didn't know our usual arrangement. We had to explain everything.
They were decent enough, but there's something about starting over with a stranger.
reactions: Trust takes years to build and one new face to reset.

statCheck.pass: When a trader sends a replacement, they're either expanding and hired help, or they're phasing out the route. If the new person doesn't know the terms, it's probably the second one.
statCheck.fail: Could be anything. I'll wait and see if the usual trader comes back next time.

entries.intro: A merchant said they keep hearing about us from other traders. We're getting a reputation for fair dealing.
details: Apparently word spreads on the route faster than I thought.
They said two other traders added us to their circuit because of what they'd heard.
reactions: A good name travels the same road the merchants do.

entries.intro: A traveler warned me the bridge on the road toward {other_settlement} is getting worse. Planks rotting through.
details: They said their cart nearly lost a wheel going across. Had to walk the horses over one at a time.
Nobody maintains it. Everyone assumes someone else will.
reactions: A bridge nobody fixes is a road nobody uses. Eventually.

statCheck.pass: Bridges rot from underneath first. The supports that sit in water weaken before the planks show damage. By the time the deck is bad, the structure below is worse.
statCheck.fail: I haven't crossed it recently. I'll take their word for it.

entries.intro: A merchant bought every scrap of wool we had. Cleaned us out in one visit.
details: Paid well for it too. Said wool is scarce on the route right now.
We let it all go because the price was good. Now we've got nothing for ourselves until the next shearing.
reactions: Sold everything we had because the price was right. Ask me again in winter if that was smart.

statCheck.pass: A buyer who takes everything you have at a good price is creating scarcity they can sell into. They profit on the markup at the next stop, and you're short until you produce more.
statCheck.fail: The price was good. I didn't think past that.

entries.intro: A traveler drew a sketch of the settlement before they left. Said it was for their journal.
details: Quick lines, just shapes and shadows. But somehow it looked exactly like here.
They showed me and I recognized every rooftop.
reactions: Someone drew a picture of where I live and it made me see it differently for a moment.

entries.intro: The flour from the last trader was half chaff. Ground so coarsely you could see the husks.
details: The bread came out dense and gritty. More filler than food.
We sifted out what we could, but there's no saving badly milled grain.
reactions: Paying flour prices for chaff is the kind of thing that makes you want to grow your own grain.

statCheck.pass: Coarse flour with visible husks means the mill stones need dressing or the miller is running grain through once instead of twice. Either way, they're selling second-rate product at first-rate price.
statCheck.fail: I know bad flour when I see it. Beyond that, milling isn't my area.

entries.intro: A merchant taught me a knot I'd never seen before. Said it's what they use to secure cargo in heavy wind.
details: Took me three tries to get it right. Once it clicked, I couldn't believe how solid it holds.
I've already re-tied half the tarps over the storage.
reactions: A single knot from a stranger, and suddenly half my problems with loose covers are solved.

entries.intro: A traveler complained about our prices and I told them to check what {other_settlement} charges. We're cheaper.
details: They acted surprised, like we should be grateful they stopped here at all.
We're not a charity stop. We're a settlement that sells what it has at fair rates.
reactions: Being small doesn't mean being desperate. We know what our goods are worth.

entries.intro: A merchant travels with a cat on the cart. Says it keeps the mice out of the grain sacks.
details: The cat just lounges on top of the cargo like it owns the whole operation.
Best-fed cat I've ever seen. Mice on a grain cart must be easy living.
reactions: A working cat that earns its meals by napping in the sun. Best job on the road.

entries.intro: We owe a trader for last month's delivery and they haven't come to collect. Makes me nervous.
details: A debt hanging over you is worse than paying it. I've had the coin set aside waiting.
If they don't come soon, I'm tempted to send it with the next traveler heading their way.
reactions: I want to owe nobody anything. An unpaid debt is a leash you can't see.

statCheck.pass: Some traders delay collection on purpose because a settlement that owes feels obligated to buy more on the next visit. The debt becomes a loyalty tool, not an oversight.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they just forgot. People get busy.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said they're thinking of settling here. Just tired of the road.
details: They've been trading for years and said the road wears you down eventually.
Asked about land, asked about work, asked who they'd need to talk to.
reactions: A new face who chose to stay instead of pass through. I'd welcome that.

statCheck.pass: Traders who settle usually have savings most residents don't. They also bring contacts, supply knowledge, and a network that benefits the whole settlement if they stay.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if they'll follow through. People say a lot of things on the road.

entries.intro: A traveler asked me if there's anything worth seeing around here. I said the sunset is pretty good.
details: They looked disappointed, like I should have a list of attractions.
We're not a destination. We're a place where people live.
reactions: The sunset is genuinely good though. They missed it by leaving before dark.

entries.intro: The {resource_type} trader switched to a smaller cart. Means less stock per visit.
details: They said the bigger cart was too hard on the road and the animals.
Makes sense for them, but it means we get less and have to stretch it further.
reactions: Their convenience is our shortage. That's the reality when you're at the end of the route.

statCheck.pass: A smaller cart isn't about the road. It's about cutting costs with fewer animals, less feed, and a lighter load. The trader is tightening their operation and we're absorbing the impact.
statCheck.fail: I don't know their reasons. I just see less on the cart.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they'd been dreaming about a proper bed for six days. We gave them a cot and they slept fourteen hours.
details: Didn't eat, didn't talk. Just collapsed and disappeared until the next morning.
When they woke up, they said it was the best sleep of their life.
reactions: A cot in a dry room is luxury when you've been sleeping on the ground.

entries.intro: A merchant told us they're raising prices next visit. Fuel for the forge costs more, transport costs more, everything costs more.
details: They at least had the courtesy to warn us ahead of time.
I appreciate the honesty even if the news isn't good.
reactions: An honest warning beats a surprise at the cart.

entries.intro: A trader brought a set of clay pipes from somewhere past {other_settlement}. Beautifully shaped, light as nothing.
details: They're fired thin and glazed smooth. Water runs through them cleaner than our wooden troughs.
I bought two just to see if they hold up. If they do, I want a dozen.
reactions: New materials make you realize how long you've been making do with old ones.

entries.intro: A merchant and I shook on a deal and they changed the terms while unloading. Said they misspoke earlier.
details: Misspoke, right. The price they quoted was clear enough before the handshake.
I paid the new price because I needed the goods. But I remember.
reactions: A handshake deal is only as good as the person on the other end of it.

statCheck.pass: The 'misspoke' trick works because the buyer is already committed. You've planned around the delivery, your people are waiting, and walking away costs you more than the markup.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if it was intentional. I just know the number changed and I lost.

entries.intro: A traveler sat with us for supper and asked everyone's name. Repeated each one back to make sure they had it right.
details: By the end of the meal they could address everyone at the table. Most travelers don't bother.
They said names are how you show someone their time with you matters.
reactions: I still remember their name too. That's how it works, I suppose.

entries.intro: A trader who comes through regularly asked about {npc_name}. Said they always looked forward to dealing with them.
details: Apparently {npc_name} is a fair negotiator and a good conversation. The trader values both.
I didn't realize our people made that kind of impression on passersby.
reactions: Being remembered fondly by the people who pass through. That's its own kind of reputation.

entries.intro: We ordered {resource_type} from a trader two months ago and it still hasn't arrived. No word, no explanation.
details: We paid half upfront, which I'm now regretting.
Could be road trouble, could be they took the coin and forgot us. No way to know.
reactions: Paying in advance is an act of faith. Sometimes the faith isn't rewarded.

statCheck.pass: A two-month delay with no word usually means the trader found a better buyer for the goods. They'll show up eventually with excuses and offer a discount to smooth things over.
statCheck.fail: I just want what I paid for. Is that too much to ask.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a bolt of cloth dyed the deepest green I've ever seen. Like looking into a forest.
details: I couldn't afford enough for a garment, but I bought a strip. It's tied around my wrist now.
The color hasn't faded in the wash either. Whatever dye they used, it holds.
reactions: A strip of beautiful cloth is a small thing. But it makes the day a little brighter.

entries.intro: A traveler said people from {other_settlement} warned them not to stop here. Said we're backwards.
details: Backwards. Because we don't have a market square and a gate with a sign on it.
The traveler stopped anyway and said we were fine.
reactions: Let them call us what they want. We don't need their approval to exist.

entries.intro: A merchant counted their stock three times before selling to me. Most careful trader I've ever met.
details: Every item checked, every weight confirmed. They said one bad count ruins a whole trip's profit.
I appreciated the thoroughness even if it took twice as long.
reactions: I trust a careful trader more than a fast one.

entries.intro: We got more rain than usual and the road is a river of mud. No carts are getting through.
details: The last trader who tried turned around after a hundred paces. Their wheels sank to the axle.
