We'll be fine for a while, but if it keeps up, some things will run short.
reactions: Weather decides who trades and who waits. We're waiting.

statCheck.pass: Mud roads need gravel or crushed stone laid in the low spots. A day of labor by four people could keep the road passable year-round, but nobody wants to be the four people.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how to fix a road. I just know when it's broken.

entries.intro: A traveler from far off tried to barter with us using goods we've never seen. Didn't know what half of them were.
details: Strange dried roots, little bundles of fiber, a powder that smells sweet. None of it familiar.
We couldn't agree on value because we didn't know what anything was worth.
reactions: Hard to trade when you can't even name what's on the table.

statCheck.pass: Foreign goods that are unknown locally can be worth a fortune or nothing, and the trick is finding someone who knows the market. If nobody does, you're guessing with real coin.
statCheck.fail: I let them go. Not going to buy what I can't identify.

entries.intro: A trader brought soap from {other_settlement} that actually lathers. Ours just sort of sits there.
details: It smells like lavender and works on grease. My hands haven't felt this clean in months.
I bought three bars. At this rate, they'll last me a long while.
reactions: Good soap feels like a luxury. Shouldn't, but it does.

entries.intro: A merchant tried to sell us last season's {resource_type} at this season's prices. We could smell the age on it.
details: It was dried out, dusty, and clearly sitting in storage since before winter.
When we called them on it, they shrugged and dropped the price by a third.
reactions: At least they didn't argue. Just quietly admitted they'd been caught.

statCheck.pass: Old stock that hasn't moved is a carrying cost for the trader. They'd rather sell it for less than haul it another stop. The third they dropped is what it was worth to begin with.
statCheck.fail: I just knew it was old. The price drop told me everything else.

entries.intro: A traveler pointed out that we have something {other_settlement} doesn't. Open space. Said they're packed in tight over there.
details: They said you can hear your neighbor's conversations through the walls.
Our houses are spread apart enough to have quiet. I never thought of that as valuable.
reactions: Space is a luxury you only recognize from the outside.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they keep a count of every settlement they've traded with. We're number forty-three.
details: Forty-three stops on one trader's list. I didn't know there were that many places out there.
They said most are our size or smaller. We're not as unusual as we think.
reactions: Forty-three settlements, all thinking they're alone out here.

entries.intro: The {resource_type} supply has been unreliable for three months running now. Can't plan around it.
details: Some weeks we get too much, some weeks nothing. The traders blame each other.
Meanwhile we're either scrambling or wasting. There's no middle.
reactions: Unreliable supply is worse than no supply. At least with nothing, you know where you stand.

statCheck.pass: Irregular supply usually means the source is splitting output between multiple buyers and we're getting whatever's left. The fix is a standing agreement with volume guarantees, but that requires coin upfront.
statCheck.fail: I can't figure out the pattern. If there is one, it's beyond me.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a mirror. Small, round, polished metal. First one anyone here has owned.
details: I looked at myself for the first time in ages. I look older than I expected.
Three people have already asked to borrow it.
reactions: Strange to meet your own face after years of only seeing your reflection in water.

entries.intro: A trader said the {resource_type} we sold them last visit got top marks at {other_settlement}. People were asking for more.
details: Apparently our product has something others don't. Consistency. Same quality every time.
The trader wants to double the order next visit.
reactions: Hearing that our work is valued somewhere we've never been. That's worth more than the coin.

entries.intro: A traveler left a stain on our best table and didn't say a word about it. Just packed up and left.
details: Some kind of ink or dye. Won't come off no matter what I scrub with.
It's a small thing, but it bothers me every time I sit down.
reactions: Leave a place better than you found it. Apparently not everyone lives by that.

entries.intro: A merchant told me the season's been good across the board. Everyone's buying, everyone's selling.
details: They had a full cart coming in and an empty one going out. That's the dream for a trader.
When times are good on the road, they're usually good everywhere.
reactions: Good seasons don't announce themselves. You just look around one day and realize things are working.

entries.intro: A traveler asked if we had any messages to send along. We didn't, but it was nice to be asked.
details: They said they carry messages for free between stops. It's how they stay welcome.
I might write something next time. Just to test if it actually gets delivered.
reactions: Free message carrying. That's the kind of kindness that builds a reputation.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said they almost didn't come this time. The road isn't worth the haul anymore.
details: We don't buy enough volume to justify the trip, they said. It's just them being loyal to the route.
Loyalty runs out eventually though.
reactions: When a trader tells you they're doing you a favor by showing up, the clock is ticking.

statCheck.pass: A trader warning about viability is asking for more business, not sympathy. They want you to consolidate orders and increase volume. It's a negotiation dressed as a warning.
statCheck.fail: I believe them. We're small and the road is long.

entries.intro: A traveler asked to fill their water skins and ended up staying for three hours. Just talking.
details: They were lonely, you could tell. Weeks on the road with nobody to talk to.
I didn't mind. It's not like I had anywhere pressing to be.
reactions: Sometimes the best thing you can give a traveler isn't water. It's just company.

entries.intro: The vinegar a merchant brought is so strong you can clean rust with it. Works better than any polish.
details: I soaked a rusty hinge overnight and it came out almost new.
We use it for preserving too, but the cleaning might be the bigger use.
reactions: Good vinegar is the most underappreciated trade good on the road.

entries.intro: A trader started selling to us on credit. Makes me uneasy.
details: They say it's normal in bigger settlements. Buy now, pay when you can.
But debt has a way of growing, and they're the ones keeping the ledger.
reactions: Credit sounds generous until you realize the trader controls the terms.

statCheck.pass: Credit creates dependency. Once you owe a trader, you can't switch to a competitor without settling the debt first. The credit is the chain and the goods are just the bait.
statCheck.fail: I just don't like owing people. Simple as that.

entries.intro: A traveler brought their own food and insisted on sharing it with us. Refused to eat ours.
details: They had smoked meat, hard cheese, and flatbread wrapped in oilcloth. Everything tasted of the road.
They said feeding people is how they thank a settlement for safe passage.
reactions: Being fed by a stranger in your own home. It felt odd, but the food was good.

entries.intro: A merchant told me traders talk about settlements the way we talk about weather. Just background noise.
details: To them, we're a stop, a line on a route. They don't think about us between visits.
I suppose I don't think about the road between carts either.
reactions: We're the scenery in someone else's life, and they're a brief event in ours.

entries.intro: The {resource_type} we've been trading for has a different weight this season. Lighter for the same volume.
details: Could be a different source, could be a different drying method. The trader won't say.
Either way, we're getting less substance for the same price.
reactions: The price stays the same but the value shrinks. That's the quiet way to cheat someone.

statCheck.pass: Changing weight-to-volume without changing price is the oldest inflation trick in trade. The product looks the same but delivers less. If nobody weighs it, nobody notices.
statCheck.fail: I notice the difference. I just don't know what to do about it.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've been to every settlement in the region and ours is the quietest. Said it like a compliment.
details: They said the bigger places are all noise and hurry. Nobody sits still.
Here, they said, you can hear yourself think.
reactions: Quiet as a compliment. I'll take it, even if it really means we're small.

entries.intro: A merchant brought needles made from better steel than anything we have. Sharp as a thorn and twice as strong.
details: My old needles bend and blunt after a week of use. These feel like they'll last seasons.
I bought a packet and I'm keeping them safe.
reactions: Good tools make you realize how much time you've wasted fighting bad ones.

entries.intro: A trader said the road between us and {other_settlement} is getting a reputation for being safe and well-traveled.
details: They said more people are using it than last year. The ruts are deeper and the brush is beaten back.
A busy road maintains itself just from the traffic.
reactions: A road people trust is the best thing that can happen to a settlement like ours.

entries.intro: A traveler stole food from us overnight and was gone by sunrise. We noticed at breakfast.
details: A wheel of cheese and a bag of grain. Not much to them, maybe, but a lot to us.
We'd offered them a meal the night before. They could have just asked.
reactions: Stealing from people who fed you. That's a special kind of low.

statCheck.pass: A traveler who steals food they could have asked for isn't hungry. They're hoarding. They're building supply for a long stretch where they can't stop.
statCheck.fail: I don't care why they did it. They did it.

entries.intro: A merchant said they measure a settlement's health by its smoke. More smoke means more cooking, more forges, more life.
details: They said they can see our smoke from half a day's walk away. Thin but steady.
I asked if that's good and they said it means we're alive. Not thriving, but alive.
reactions: Alive. Not the highest bar, but we clear it.

entries.intro: We traded with a merchant who weighs everything on a scale they let us inspect first. That should be normal, but it isn't.
details: They even showed us the counterweights and let us test them against our own.
The prices were fair and the counts were exact.
reactions: Transparent dealing. It's so rare that when it happens, you remember the trader's face.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they left {other_settlement} because it was too crowded. Couldn't breathe, they said.
details: They're looking for somewhere with space and fewer people. Asked if we had room.
We have room. Whether they'd last here is a different question.
reactions: Wanting quiet and living quiet are two different things. Most people want it until they have it.

statCheck.pass: People who leave busy settlements for quiet ones usually go back within a year because they miss the things they complained about. Quiet wears differently than they expect.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they'll decide. Everyone's different.

entries.intro: A merchant told me their donkey knows the route so well it could do the trip without them.
details: Said the donkey turns at the right forks, stops at the right water points, and knows every hill.
They said the hard part isn't navigating. It's convincing the donkey to start walking in the morning.
reactions: A donkey that knows the road better than its owner. There's a partnership built on stubbornness.

entries.intro: We tried sending our own goods to {other_settlement} with a trusted traveler. They arrived in good condition.
details: The traveler brought back coin and a note saying they'd buy more if we can supply it.
It's the first trade we've initiated ourselves instead of waiting for someone to come to us.
reactions: Reaching out instead of sitting still. That's a change for us.

entries.intro: A merchant told me every settlement thinks their road is the worst. We're no different.
details: They've been on roads that make ours look paved. Deep ruts, fallen trees, washouts.
I still think ours is bad, but maybe less bad than I assumed.
reactions: Perspective from someone who sees every road. Ours is average, and somehow that's comforting.

entries.intro: The oil a trader brought burns cleaner than what we've been using. Our lamps don't smoke anymore.
details: Clear, pale oil that gives off almost no soot. The wicks last longer too.
Everything in the house smells less like burning since we switched.
reactions: Clean-burning oil. Simple improvement, enormous difference.

entries.intro: A trader told me {other_settlement} is producing so much {resource_type} that they can't sell it all.
details: Surplus drives the price down everywhere on the route. Good for buyers, bad for anyone else trying to sell the same thing.
If we produce any {resource_type}, we can't compete with those volumes.
reactions: When someone floods the market, the rest of us just tread water.

statCheck.pass: A settlement dumping surplus is usually burning through a windfall. New equipment, a lucky season, or labor they can't sustain. The glut is temporary, but it can crush smaller producers in the meantime.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they have so much. I just know it's squeezing the rest of us.

entries.intro: A traveler left us a recipe for a poultice in exchange for a night's shelter. Works on swelling and bruises.
details: Just common plants mixed a certain way. We had everything we needed already.
I tried it on my sore knee and the swelling went down by morning.
reactions: Knowledge that costs nothing to share and saves real pain. Best trade we've made in weeks.

entries.intro: A trader said our grain stores look low from the outside. Said it like a warning.
details: I didn't like someone sizing up our reserves just by looking.
They're right, though. We are lower than we should be this time of year.
reactions: When a stranger can see your weakness from the road, you're more exposed than you thought.

statCheck.pass: Traders who note your supply levels are adjusting prices before they even open the cart. If they know you're low, they know you're desperate, and desperate buyers pay more.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they were just being observant. Either way, it made me uncomfortable.

entries.intro: A traveler offered to fix our fence gate in exchange for water. Did a better job than we'd have done.
details: They rebalanced the hinge and trimmed the bottom so it swings clean.
Took them maybe twenty minutes. That gate has been dragging for a year.
reactions: A fresh pair of hands sees problems we've learned to step around.

entries.intro: A merchant said they won't carry {resource_type} anymore. Not enough profit for the weight.
details: They're switching to lighter goods. Cloth, spices, dye. Things that pack small and sell high.
Makes sense for them, but it means one fewer source for the heavy stuff we actually need.
reactions: Traders follow the profit. Can't blame them, but it leaves us looking.

statCheck.pass: When multiple traders drop heavy goods for light luxury items, it means the whole route's economics are shifting. Settlements that need bulk materials get squeezed because nobody wants the low-margin haul.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand the business of it. I just know we need {resource_type} and nobody wants to bring it.

entries.intro: A traveler told me the stars look different from here than from where they started. Same sky, different angle.
details: They pointed out a constellation I'd never noticed. It sits low on the horizon and I've been ignoring it my whole life.
Funny how a stranger shows you something that's been above you every night.
reactions: The sky is the same everywhere, but what you see in it depends on where you're standing.

entries.intro: We found out the {resource_type} we've been paying a premium for is the same stuff {other_settlement} gets at half price.
details: Same supplier, same batch. The only difference is the distance and our lack of options.
The trader didn't even deny it when I brought it up.
reactions: The distance tax is real and there's nothing we can do about it.

statCheck.pass: The markup isn't just distance. It's leverage because {other_settlement} has competing suppliers and we don't. One source means the trader charges whatever they want.
statCheck.fail: I know we pay more. I just don't know how to change it.

entries.intro: A merchant showed me a trick for testing metal quality. Scratch it with a harder metal and look at the color of the mark.
details: Good iron leaves a clean grey line. Bad iron leaves brown or flaky residue.
I've been buying iron for years without knowing this.
reactions: One test, five seconds, and suddenly I can spot bad stock before I pay for it.

entries.intro: A trader passed through carrying a load of blankets. Every one of them hand-stitched, thick as a thumb.
details: I bought one. It cost more than I'd normally spend on a blanket, but you can feel the weight of it.
The nights have been cold and this one actually keeps the chill out.
reactions: A blanket that does its job properly. Why did I settle for less for so long?

entries.intro: A traveler said they measure how well a settlement is doing by whether people smile at them or frown.
details: They said we smiled. First settlement in four stops where that was the case.
I don't know if we're doing well or if we're just polite.
reactions: Smiling at a stranger costs nothing and apparently means something.

entries.intro: A merchant tried to bundle goods together and charge one price. I made them break it out item by item.
details: Bundles always hide something overpriced behind something cheap.
Once we separated it, two of the items weren't worth what they were asking.
reactions: Bundling is just rounding up with extra steps.

statCheck.pass: Bundle pricing works because the brain averages instead of adding. One great deal makes the whole package feel fair, even if half the items are overpriced. Always break it down.
statCheck.fail: I just know bundles make me nervous. Simpler to see each price on its own.

entries.intro: A trader coming from {other_settlement} said they saw more abandoned camps along the road than usual.
details: Cold firepits, discarded wrappings, footprints going in every direction.
They said it means people are moving but not staying anywhere long.
reactions: People passing through without stopping. That's restlessness, not travel.

statCheck.pass: Frequent abandoned camps without trade stops means displaced people, not merchants. They're moving because they have to, not because they want to. That kind of movement brings need, not business.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what it means. Just sounds unsettling.

entries.intro: A merchant's partner handles the talking while the merchant handles the goods. Best system I've seen.
details: The talker is warm, patient, and explains everything. The other one weighs, counts, and packs in silence.
Between the two of them, the transaction was done in half the usual time.
reactions: Two people who know their strengths and stick to them. That's how good trade works.

entries.intro: We bartered preserves for {resource_type} and both sides walked away satisfied. That's how it should be.
details: No coin changed hands. Just goods for goods, value for value.
The trader said they prefer barter with small settlements. Less bookkeeping.
reactions: Barter feels more honest than coin. You can see what you're giving and what you're getting.

entries.intro: A traveler warned us that a merchant on the route is marking up {resource_type} by double at every stop.
details: Buys it cheap at the source and adds a new markup each time they sell and rebuy along the way.
By the time it reaches us, the price has nothing to do with the value.
reactions: Middlemen upon middlemen. Every hand it passes through takes a cut.

statCheck.pass: Layered markups are invisible to the end buyer because each step adds a 'reasonable' increase that compounds along the route. The fix is buying closer to the source, but that requires someone to make the trip.
statCheck.fail: I don't track the chain. I just pay what I'm asked.

entries.intro: A traveler told me about a settlement that makes everything from clay. Cups, tools, pipes, tiles, all of it.
details: They said the whole place looks like it grew out of the ground. Every surface has a warm, earthy color.
We don't have good clay here, but it made me wonder what we could specialize in if we tried.
reactions: Every settlement has something. We just haven't figured out what ours is yet.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they've seen more new traders on the road this year than ever. Everyone's trying to make a living on wheels.
details: More competition means better prices for us, at least for now.
But new traders don't know the routes, don't know the customers, and half of them will quit before winter.
reactions: More traders today, fewer tomorrow. The road sorts them out.

statCheck.pass: A surge of new traders usually means hard times elsewhere. People who lost land or work turning to the road as a last option. They trade until their savings run out, then they settle wherever they stop.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why there are more of them. Just noticed the numbers.

entries.intro: A traveler gave me advice I didn't ask for about how to run the settlement. Left before I could respond.
details: Said we should grow more, trade more, build more. As if we hadn't thought of that.
Easy to hand out advice when you don't have to live with the consequences.
reactions: Advice from someone who's never stayed anywhere longer than a night. I'll file it accordingly.

entries.intro: A merchant brought charcoal packed so tight it burns for hours. Our homemade stuff is ash in minutes.
details: The difference is the wood they start with and how long they bake it. Dense wood, slow burn.
A single bag lasted me a week. Usually I'm through a bag in two days.
reactions: Good charcoal changes the math on everything you cook or heat.

entries.intro: A merchant said {other_settlement} has grown enough to attract two regular trade routes now. We still barely hold one.
details: Two routes means competition, which means better prices and more variety.
We're one bad season away from losing the single route we have.
reactions: Growth attracts trade, trade fuels growth. And we're on the wrong side of that cycle.

statCheck.pass: The threshold for a second trade route is roughly double the volume, and once a settlement crosses it, the growth compounds with more routes, lower prices, and more people. Below it, you stagnate.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand the economics. I just see them growing and us staying the same.

entries.intro: A traveler sang while they walked in. You could hear them before you could see them.
details: Not a great voice, but a happy one. The kind of singing that means someone's having a good day.
A few of us stopped what we were doing just to listen.
reactions: It's been a while since someone arrived here singing. Felt like good luck.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they lost a whole cart of goods to a river crossing last month. Started over with what they could salvage.
details: The current was stronger than it looked. The cart went over and most of the stock was ruined.
They're still rebuilding their inventory. What they have now is cobbled together from three different sources.
reactions: One bad crossing can erase a whole season of work. The road takes as much as it gives.

statCheck.pass: A trader who lost a cart and is back on the road with mixed stock is probably trading on borrowed coin. They need every sale just to break even, which means their prices are either desperate-low or markup-high to cover the debt.
statCheck.fail: Bad luck is bad luck. I hope the rest of their season goes better.

entries.intro: A traveler asked if we knew anyone who needed work done. Said they'd take any job for a few days.
details: We had a shed that needed re-roofing and a fence line that needed straightening.
They worked steadily for two days, took food and a small pouch of coin, and moved on.
reactions: Honest labor for honest pay. Wish every interaction with a stranger went that smoothly.

entries.intro: A merchant told me the salt supply across the region is getting tight. Prices will climb.
details: Salt is one of those things you can't substitute and can't go without.
They offered to lock in this month's price if we bought extra now.
reactions: When a trader warns you about a shortage and offers to sell you extra in the same breath, you have to wonder who benefits.

statCheck.pass: Traders who announce coming shortages and then offer to sell extra are creating urgency to move stock. If the shortage were real and confirmed, they wouldn't be offering surplus. They'd be hoarding it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if it's real or a sales tactic. Either way, we do need salt.

entries.intro: A traveler said they carry a journal and write about every place they stop. I asked what they wrote about us.
details: They said, 'Quiet, kind, practical.' Three words.
I thought about it the rest of the day. Three words and somehow it felt right.
reactions: Quiet, kind, practical. I've heard worse descriptions.

entries.intro: A merchant's load shifted on a hill and half their goods scattered down the slope.
details: Sacks split, jars cracked, and a barrel rolled until it hit a tree.
We helped them gather what was salvageable. About a third was lost.
reactions: The road takes a percentage of everything that moves on it.

entries.intro: A trader told me the demand for {resource_type} is climbing in every settlement they visit. Everyone wants the same thing.
details: When everyone wants the same goods, prices rise and supply thins.
The trader said they're rationing what they carry to spread it across more stops.
reactions: Getting a piece of something everyone wants. That's the challenge of being last on the route.

statCheck.pass: Universal demand spikes usually mean someone big is buying at the source and choking supply downstream. When a settlement or guild corners the source, everyone else scrambles for scraps.
statCheck.fail: I just know it's harder to get than it used to be.

entries.intro: A traveler thanked us for being the only settlement that didn't try to overcharge them for water.
details: We don't charge for water and never have. It's from a well, not a shop.
Apparently that's unusual enough to be worth mentioning.
reactions: Charging travelers for well water. I can't imagine thinking of that as a business.

entries.intro: A merchant said they'd come more often if we improved the road leading in. Hinted heavily.
details: We don't have the hands or the tools to maintain a road. We can barely keep the settlement standing.
But they're right that a smoother road brings more carts.
reactions: Fix the road, get more trade. Simple on paper, impossible in practice.

statCheck.pass: A trader who asks you to improve the road is telling you you're on the margin of their route. The road is the tiebreaker between visiting and skipping. Even a small improvement might keep them coming.
statCheck.fail: I know the road needs work. Don't need a trader to tell me that.

entries.intro: A traveler brought a harmonica and played it by the fire. Half the settlement came to listen.
details: Simple tunes, nothing fancy. But out here, live music is rare enough to feel like an event.
The kids especially. They'd never heard one before.
reactions: An instrument, a fire, and an audience that's easy to impress. Everyone left happier.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they're combining our stop with {other_settlement} into one trip. Saves them a day.
details: Means they arrive later and leave faster. Less time for us to think about what we need.
Efficiency for the trader, inconvenience for the buyer.
reactions: We'll adapt like we always do, but I liked having more time to decide.

entries.intro: A traveler left us a bag of seeds as a parting gift. Said they collected them along the road.
details: We don't know what half of them are. Planted a few in a test bed to see what grows.
If even one turns into something useful, the gift was more than generous.
reactions: Mystery seeds from a stranger. Either we get a new crop or an interesting weed.

entries.intro: A merchant weighed our {resource_type} and said it's better quality than what {other_settlement} produces. Hard to believe.
details: We don't do anything special. Just harvest it, process it, and pack it like we always have.
Maybe doing things simply and consistently is the trick.
reactions: Better than {other_settlement}. I'll be repeating that to anyone who listens.

entries.intro: A traveler asked me what the worst thing about living here is. I said waiting for the next cart.
details: The gap between traders is when you feel how dependent you are on the outside world.
Everything you can't make yourself, you wait for. And waiting isn't easy.
reactions: Dependence on the road is the price of not being able to do everything ourselves.

statCheck.pass: The honest answer is that the worst part isn't the waiting. It's knowing that if the traders stopped coming, we'd last maybe two seasons before we couldn't sustain ourselves.
statCheck.fail: The waiting is bad enough. I don't like to think past that.

entries.intro: A merchant let me look through their whole inventory before buying. Most don't bother.
details: Opened every crate, unwrapped every bundle. Said if I'm paying for it, I should see it all.
I found things I didn't know I wanted until I saw them.
reactions: A merchant who lets you browse is a merchant who trusts their stock.

entries.intro: A trader told me they're the third generation in their family to run this route. Same road their grandparents walked.
details: The cart's been rebuilt twice, but the iron fittings are original. Grandfather's iron, they said.
They know every turn, every rest point, every settlement on the way.
reactions: Three generations on the same road. That's a different kind of home.

entries.intro: A merchant said they've stopped carrying luxury goods to small settlements. Nobody buys them.
details: Dyes, fine cloth, spices. All cut from the cart for our route.
They said it's not worth the weight when essentials are what sell.
reactions: We don't buy luxuries because we can't. Not because we don't want them.

statCheck.pass: A settlement that only buys essentials is one the trader has already written off for growth. They bring what sells and nothing more, and the selection gets thinner every visit.
statCheck.fail: I just know the cart has less interesting stuff on it every time.

entries.intro: A traveler showed me a coin trick. Flipping it off the back of the hand to land in a cup from three paces.
details: They said they practiced it every night by the fire. Thousand tries before they got consistent.
I tried fifty times and hit the cup once. They laughed but said that's a good start.
reactions: A pointless skill practiced to perfection. I admire the dedication.

entries.intro: A merchant offered us a deal. Take all the odds and ends nobody else wanted at a flat rate. We took it.
details: Mismatched nails, half-rolls of twine, a few cracked jars still good for storage.
Individually they're not worth much. Together they fill a dozen small needs.
reactions: The leftovers nobody else wanted turned out to be exactly what we needed.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they choose routes based on the people, not the profit. Said we're on their list because we're good company.
details: They said some settlements pay better but the people are sour.
They'd rather earn less and eat with people who smile.
reactions: Being chosen for company over coin. I'll remember that.

entries.intro: The dried meat from the last trader was so salty it made my mouth burn. Even soaking it didn't help.
details: Overly salted to hide the fact that it was turning before they cured it.
We choked some of it down and gave the rest to the dogs. Even they looked skeptical.
reactions: Salt is for preserving, not for hiding. There's a line and they crossed it.

statCheck.pass: Excess salt on cured meat means the meat was already spoiling when they salted it. They packed it heavy to mask the taste and the smell. It's concealment, not preservation.
statCheck.fail: I know over-salted when I taste it. The rest is obvious.

entries.intro: A traveler said this is the last settlement before the road gets truly empty. We're the edge of familiar ground.
details: Past us, it's open country with no regular stops for days.
They filled up on everything. Water, food, rest. Like they were preparing for something harder.
reactions: Being the last stop before the unknown. I suppose there's a kind of importance in that.

statCheck.pass: Last-stop settlements have more leverage than they realize. Travelers heading into open country will pay a premium because the alternative is going without. That's a position worth using wisely.
statCheck.fail: I didn't think about what being last on the road means for us. Maybe it matters more than I assumed.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said they almost skipped us this trip. Didn't think it was worth the detour.
details: We bought enough to change their mind, but only just. They weighed the coin against the extra day and barely tipped our way.
They were honest about it, at least. Said next time depends on what we have to offer.
reactions: Being an afterthought on someone's route isn't great, but it beats being dropped entirely.

statCheck.pass: When a trader tells you they almost skipped you, they're negotiating. They want you to buy more next time so the detour stays justified. It's a warning dressed as honesty.
statCheck.fail: I just hope they keep coming. We don't have many options.

entries.intro: A traveler asked to borrow our tools for an afternoon. Returned them cleaner than when they left.
details: Sharpened the blade, oiled the handle, even wrapped the grip with new cord.
I tried to pay them for the work and they said the loan was payment enough.
reactions: Someone who treats borrowed things better than their own. Rare quality.

entries.intro: Heard from a trader that {other_settlement} raised their tolls for passing carts. Traders are adjusting prices everywhere to cover it.
details: What {other_settlement} charges at their gate ends up in our prices. We're paying for their decision.
The trader said three settlements on the route have already complained, but nothing's changed.
reactions: Someone else's toll, our coin. That's how the chain works.

statCheck.pass: Settlements that raise tolls are usually trying to fix a budget gap, but the cost rolls downhill. Every stop past them pays a little more for everything. The settlements at the end of the route get squeezed the hardest.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand who sets these things. I just pay more.

entries.intro: A merchant complimented our packing. Said our goods arrive in better shape than most.
details: Tight bundles, clean wrapping, and we label everything. It's not hard, just careful.
They said most places hand over loose goods in sacks that leak and shift.
reactions: Nice to be good at something, even if it's wrapping things in cloth.

statCheck.pass: Good packing is leverage. A trader who knows your goods survive the road intact will pay a premium because they lose less to breakage. We could be charging more.
statCheck.fail: I don't think about it as a selling point. Just how we do things.

entries.intro: A traveler slept in our common area and was gone before anyone woke up. Didn't take anything, but didn't say goodbye either.
details: Left the blankets folded and the fire banked. Polite but invisible.
Some people move through the world like they don't want to leave a mark.
reactions: A guest who leaves no trace. I suppose that's better than the alternative.

entries.intro: The {resource_type} shipment we expected last week still hasn't arrived. No word from the trader either.
details: Could be a delay, could be they sold it somewhere else along the route for a better price.
We planned around that delivery. Now we're adjusting everything.
reactions: Counting on a shipment that doesn't come. You'd think I'd learn.

statCheck.pass: A missed delivery without notice usually means someone outbid us on the road. Traders sell to the best offer, and verbal agreements with small settlements get broken first.
statCheck.fail: I just wish they'd sent word so we could plan.

entries.intro: A traveler taught one of the kids how to whittle. Sat with them for a whole afternoon, patient as anything.
details: By sundown the kid had carved something that was either a bird or a fish. They were proud of it either way.
The traveler left the knife as a gift. Said every whittler needs their own blade.
reactions: A stranger's patience with someone else's child. That sticks with you.

entries.intro: A merchant said they won't extend credit to us anymore. Coin up front or nothing.
details: We've always paid eventually, but apparently 'eventually' isn't good enough.
They said too many settlements owe them and they can't carry the weight.
reactions: Can't blame them, but it means we'll go without until we have the coin in hand.

statCheck.pass: A trader cutting off credit means their own margins are thin. They need the coin to buy stock for the next leg. When credit dries up for us, it's because someone above them tightened the chain.
statCheck.fail: We pay when we can. That used to be enough.

entries.intro: A traveler from {other_settlement} said they've been walking for eight days straight. Looked it, too.
details: Sunburned, boots worn thin, and a pack that looked like it weighed more than they did.
They ate two bowls of stew and fell asleep sitting up.
reactions: Eight days of walking. That's commitment or desperation, and I didn't ask which.

entries.intro: We traded some dried herbs for a bolt of cloth. Needed the cloth more than the herbs, so it worked out.
details: The merchant was surprised we had dried herbs at all. Said most settlements don't bother.
Apparently there's good demand on the route for anything preserved and light to carry.
reactions: Knowing what the road wants is half the trick of trade.

statCheck.pass: Dried herbs sell at three or four times their weight in grain along the route. If we produced them deliberately instead of as scraps, we'd have real bargaining power.
statCheck.fail: I just traded what we had extra. Didn't think about it as a strategy.

entries.intro: A merchant told me prices in {other_settlement} are climbing for everything. Not just one thing, everything.
details: Food, tools, cloth, fuel. All up. The merchant said the mood there is tense.
When everything costs more and pay stays the same, people get short with each other.
reactions: Rising prices everywhere. At least it's not just us.

statCheck.pass: Broad price increases across a whole settlement usually mean their leadership imposed new taxes or fees that get passed to buyers. It's not supply problems. It's policy problems.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why everything's more expensive. It just is.

entries.intro: A traveler brought news that the road between here and {other_settlement} is in better shape than last year.
details: Fewer ruts, less overgrowth. Someone must be maintaining a stretch of it.
Better road means faster carts and fresher goods when they arrive.
reactions: A decent road is one of those things you only notice when it's bad. Nice to have it be good for once.

entries.intro: A trader tried to sell us watered-down lamp oil. You could see it separate in the jar.
details: They got offended when I pointed it out, like I was the one being unreasonable.
We sent them on their way. Life's too short for bad oil and worse attitudes.
reactions: Selling diluted goods and getting angry when caught. That takes nerve.

statCheck.pass: A trader who cuts their product and sells at full price isn't struggling. They're testing you. If you buy it without noticing, they'll bring worse next time.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if it's common. I just know it was obvious.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} brought a type of rope I've never seen before. Thinner than ours but twice as strong.
details: I tested it against our heaviest bundle and it held without stretching.
Bought three coils. It's the kind of thing you use once and never go back.
reactions: Better rope changes how you think about what you can carry and tie down.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they passed through {other_settlement} and the mood was grim. Nobody was talking to strangers.
details: They said the market was half-empty and people were keeping to themselves.
Wouldn't elaborate on why. Just said it felt different from their last visit.
reactions: A quiet market and closed mouths. Something's off, but it's not my problem to solve.

statCheck.pass: Settlements that go quiet and stop trading openly are usually dealing with internal disputes. Leadership problems, resource fights, or debts that split the community. It passes, but it can take a season.
statCheck.fail: Could be anything. I try not to worry about places I can't see.

entries.intro: We got a fair price for our {resource_type} this time. First fair price in months.
details: The merchant didn't haggle, didn't lowball. Just looked at the goods and named a number that made sense.
I shook on it before they could change their mind.
reactions: A deal where nobody feels cheated. That's all I ever ask for.

entries.intro: A traveler warned us that a merchant on the route has been shorting weights. Not by a lot, but enough to matter over time.
details: A pinch less on every sale adds up to a significant skim by the end of a full circuit.
We've been buying from that trader for a while now. Makes me wonder how much we've lost.
reactions: Small theft, repeated often enough, is still theft.

statCheck.pass: Weight-skimming merchants rely on small settlements not having calibrated scales to check. The fix is owning standard weights and testing every purchase. They stop skimming when they know you verify.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how to prove it. We just have to be more careful.

entries.intro: A merchant told me our settlement is one of the quieter stops on their route. Said it like a compliment.
details: No arguments over prices, no crowds pushing to get first pick. Just calm transactions.
They said some places they dread pulling the cart into.
reactions: Being the easy stop on the route. Could do worse for a reputation.

statCheck.pass: Traders reward easy stops with better stock and fair prices because the time they save here gets spent at difficult settlements. Being calm costs us nothing and earns us preference.
statCheck.fail: I suppose being easy to deal with is its own reward.

entries.intro: A traveler asked if we ever get travelers from the east. I told them no, and they looked relieved.
details: They said the eastern routes are harder and the people on them are more desperate.
I didn't press for details. The look on their face said enough.
reactions: When someone's relieved that a direction is quiet, you don't ask why.

entries.intro: A merchant brought soap from {other_settlement}. Real soap, not the rough stuff we make here.
details: It lathers properly and doesn't leave your skin feeling raw.
I bought a block and the whole house smells better now.
reactions: Good soap. It's such a small thing but it changes how the day starts.

entries.intro: Nobody's come through in two weeks. That's the longest gap I can remember.
details: No merchants, no travelers, no messengers. The road just sits there empty.
We've started rationing a few things we assumed would be restocked by now.
reactions: An empty road makes you realize how much life comes in from the outside.

statCheck.pass: A two-week gap in all traffic usually means something is blocking the route further out. A washout, a toll dispute, or a bridge down. Traders don't skip stops by choice because empty runs lose money.
statCheck.fail: Could be nothing, could be something. I just want to see a cart.

entries.intro: A traveler paid for their stay with a story about a settlement that grows flowers just for dye. That's all they do.
details: Whole fields of one color, changed every season. The trader said it looks unreal.
They sell the dye for more than we get for a season of grain.
reactions: Growing flowers for a living. There's a settlement that figured out its niche.

entries.intro: A merchant tried to pay us in trade tokens from {other_settlement}. We don't accept those here.
details: They looked surprised, like everyone should accept them. We've never even seen them before.
Coin or goods. That's what works here.
reactions: Someone else's tokens are just shaped metal to me.

statCheck.pass: Trade tokens only hold value inside the issuing settlement's network. Accepting them ties you to that network and their terms. It's a control mechanism dressed as convenience.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they're worth there. I just know they're worthless here.

entries.intro: A trader said they've been carrying less {resource_type} because the source dried up. What they have costs more.
details: Supply drops, price rises. It's simple and it hurts every time.
They said it might be a season before the source recovers, if it does.
reactions: Paying more for less. That's just the way the route works when supply tightens.

statCheck.pass: When a trader says 'the source dried up,' it often means they lost their supplier to a competitor who offered better terms. The resource exists. It's just going somewhere else first.
statCheck.fail: I take them at their word. I don't have eyes on the source.

entries.intro: A traveler helped us stack firewood without being asked. Just saw the pile and started lifting.
details: Didn't say a word until the job was done. Then asked for a cup of water and sat down.
We fed them and gave them a dry place to sleep. Seemed like the least we could do.
reactions: People who help before they're asked. That's the kind of traveler you want to see again.

entries.intro: A merchant told me the route to {other_settlement} takes a day longer now because of mud season.
details: Carts sink in, animals slip, and everything arrives damp.
They said the extra day gets added to the price of every item on the cart.
reactions: Mud. A day of mud costs us coin on every delivery.

entries.intro: A trader brought needles. Real steel ones, not the bone splinters we've been using.
details: Thin, strong, and they hold an edge on the eye so thread slides through clean.
I bought a set. My fingers already thank me.
reactions: Steel needles. Small trade, big improvement.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they'd heard of us from {other_settlement}. Said our name came up in conversation.
details: Apparently someone in {other_settlement} mentioned us favorably. I don't know who or why.
It's strange to think people we've never met know our settlement exists.
reactions: A good word from a place we've never seen. I'll take it.

entries.intro: A merchant heading to {other_settlement} sold us grain that turned out to have weevils in it. Found them when we opened the sack at home.
details: The top layer was clean, but dig down a hand's depth and they were crawling.
We sifted out what we could, but lost at least a third of the sack.
reactions: Clean on top, rotten underneath. A metaphor for that merchant's whole operation.

statCheck.pass: Layered sacks with clean grain on top and infested grain below is deliberate staging. The seller knows and packs accordingly. They count on buyers not digging until they're home.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they didn't know, or maybe they did. Either way, I'm stuck with weevils.

entries.intro: A traveler said they keep a mental list of settlements ranked by hospitality. Said we're in the top three.
details: Hot food, a dry roof, and nobody gave them trouble. That's their whole criteria.
I told them our standards are low and they said that's exactly the point.
reactions: Top three in hospitality. Low bar, apparently, but we clear it.

entries.intro: A merchant said they're seeing the same faces on the road more often. The same people, traveling more frequently.
details: Not new traders. Existing ones making tighter loops with smaller loads.
They said it means margins are thin. You have to move faster to make the same coin.
reactions: Faster loops, smaller loads. The road is getting leaner.

statCheck.pass: When traders tighten their loops, they cut the furthest stops first because every extra day on the road costs more than the trade is worth. Settlements at the end of the route lose access first.
statCheck.fail: I don't think about the business side. I just notice the carts getting smaller.

entries.intro: A traveler stayed three days instead of the usual one. Said they needed the rest more than the road.
details: Mostly slept, ate, and sat in the shade watching people go about their work.
On the third morning they looked like a different person. Rested and steady.
reactions: Sometimes a place is more valuable as a rest stop than a trade stop.

statCheck.pass: Travelers who stop for three days are usually running from something or recovering from it. Either way, the rest isn't optional. It's the difference between finishing the route and collapsing on it.
statCheck.fail: They needed rest. I didn't ask why.

entries.intro: The last trader brought {resource_type} that was noticeably lower quality than usual. I said something and they shrugged.
details: Same price, worse product. And a shrug when I called it out.
They know we don't have other options, so they bring what they want to bring.
reactions: A shrug is the most honest answer a bad trader will give you.

statCheck.pass: Declining quality at stable prices means the trader switched to a cheaper supplier and kept the markup. The savings go to them, the downgrade goes to us.
statCheck.fail: Quality's down and I can't prove why. Just have to accept it.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said they brought extra stock because a traveler told them we were running low.
details: We didn't ask for the extra. Someone just passed along what they noticed.
The merchant said word-of-mouth is the best inventory system on the road.
reactions: A stranger noticing what we need and passing the word. That's the road working the way it should.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they don't trust settlements that are too eager to trade. Says it means they're desperate.
details: We rushed out to meet them before they'd even stopped walking. I didn't think about how that looked.
They traded with us anyway, but the comment stuck.
reactions: Eagerness reads as desperation to the wrong eyes. I'll be more careful next time.

statCheck.pass: Desperate sellers get worse prices because the buyer knows they'll accept anything. Appearing calm, even when you need the trade badly, is worth the effort.
statCheck.fail: I didn't think about how it looked. I just wanted to trade.

entries.intro: We sent goods to {other_settlement} and got paid less than agreed. The trader said the price changed on the road.
details: Prices don't change on the road. Prices change when someone decides to change them.
We're too far away to argue and they know it.
reactions: Distance makes every promise easier to break.

statCheck.pass: A trader who renegotiates on arrival is counting on your inability to verify. They tell the buyer the seller wanted more, tell the seller the buyer paid less, and pocket the gap. It's the oldest move on the road.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if we got cheated or if the market really moved. There's no way to check.

entries.intro: A merchant passed through with a dog that sat beside the cart and watched the goods while the merchant traded.
details: Nobody could get near the cart without the dog standing up. Calm, but firm.
The merchant said the dog is better security than a lock.
reactions: A well-trained dog beats a complicated setup. Simple solutions are often the best.

entries.intro: A traveler from {other_settlement} brought a letter from someone I used to know. Just a few lines, but it meant a lot.
details: The traveler didn't even know what was in it. Just said someone asked them to deliver it if they passed through here.
It's been years since I heard from that person. Nice to know they remember.
reactions: A few words on paper, carried by a stranger. Still the best way to hear from someone far away.

entries.intro: A merchant said business is good everywhere except the last two stops on this route. We're one of those stops.
details: The route's economy tapers at the ends. The middle gets the best of everything.
We get what's left after the profitable stops have been served.
reactions: End of the line. Every route has to end somewhere, and it ends here.

statCheck.pass: End-of-route settlements have the least bargaining power because the trader has already made their profit. Anything sold here is bonus. They don't need our business, we need theirs.
statCheck.fail: We're just far from things. Not much to do about that.

entries.intro: A traveler showed me how they waterproof their pack with rendered fat. Simple and effective.
details: You rub it into the seams and the cloth stops wicking water. Their gear was bone dry after crossing a stream.
I tried it on my work bag. Works exactly like they said.
reactions: Free advice that actually works. That's the best kind of trade.

entries.intro: A merchant told me {other_settlement} is trading {resource_type} at a price we can't match. We'd be selling at a loss.
details: They produce more of it, process it faster, and their route to market is shorter.
We can't compete on price, so we either find something else to sell or accept the gap.
reactions: Being outproduced by someone closer to the buyer. Not much you can do about geography.

entries.intro: A traveler said this is the most honest settlement they've visited this year. Nobody tried to cheat them.
details: They said at most stops, someone tries to short-count or slip in bad goods during a trade.
We just measured out what was owed and handed it over. Apparently that's exceptional.
reactions: Being called honest by a stranger. I'm pleased and a little sad that it's remarkable.

entries.intro: A merchant left in a hurry this morning. Packed the cart before dawn and was gone without selling half their stock.
details: They seemed fine yesterday, but something changed overnight.
Left behind a coil of rope and a half-eaten meal. We kept the rope.
reactions: People don't leave that fast without a reason. But the reason's theirs, not mine.

statCheck.pass: A trader who leaves before finishing business is either running from bad news that caught up to them on the road, or they got word of a better opportunity and didn't want to waste another day here.
statCheck.fail: Strange behavior, but I'm not going to chase them for an explanation.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they map the roads as they walk. Keeps notes on distance, condition, and water sources.
details: They said good maps are worth more than most goods because everyone needs them and few people make them.
They let me look at their map. Our settlement was a small dot with the word 'friendly' next to it.
reactions: A dot on a traveler's map with a kind word. I suppose that's a legacy of sorts.

entries.intro: The candles a merchant brought burn almost twice as long as our homemade ones. The wax is different.
details: Denser, slower-melting, and the flame is steadier too. No guttering in the wind.
They cost three times as much, but I bought a dozen and I'll stretch them.
reactions: Good candles change how much you can do after dark.

entries.intro: A merchant told me there's a new trader working the route who undercuts everyone else. The established merchants aren't happy.
details: Low prices are great for us in the short term, but if the new trader drives the old ones off our route, we lose reliability.
The old traders know what we need. The new one just sells whatever's cheapest to carry.
reactions: Cheap prices now, fewer options later. Trade-offs disguised as deals.

statCheck.pass: New traders who undercut on price are usually burning savings to build a customer base. Once the competition leaves, prices climb back up and often higher, because now there's only one cart coming.
statCheck.fail: I'll buy from whoever's cheapest. That's all I can afford to think about.

entries.intro: A traveler shared their fire-starting method. A steel striker and a bit of charred cloth. Sparks every time.
details: I've been rubbing sticks together like I learned as a kid. This was embarrassingly faster.
They gave me a spare striker. Said everyone should have one.
reactions: Years of doing something the hard way and a stranger fixes it in five minutes.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they've stopped visiting a settlement on the route because the people there started demanding goods on credit and never paying.
details: After the third unpaid delivery, the merchant wrote them off. No more visits.
That settlement is now a gap on the route. Traders pass by without stopping.
reactions: A settlement that burned its credit burned its only trade line. That's a warning for the rest of us.

statCheck.pass: Traders share blacklists. Once one merchant drops a settlement, the others hear about it within a season. Burning one relationship burns them all.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the details. Just know we don't want to end up like that.

entries.intro: We received a shipment of {resource_type} packed with straw so carefully that nothing shifted in transit. Not a scratch.
details: The merchant said proper packing is part of the trade. Breakage comes out of their profit.
Every piece arrived exactly as promised. That's a first.
reactions: Goods that arrive intact. It shouldn't feel like a luxury, but it does.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they ran out of food two days before reaching us. Ate bark and whatever they could forage.
details: They miscounted the distance and packed too light. An easy mistake on a long route.
We gave them a proper meal and they ate like they hadn't seen real food in a week.
reactions: Hunger makes every settlement feel like a miracle. I'm glad we had something to offer.

entries.intro: A trader told me {other_settlement} started producing their own tools instead of buying them. Traders who sold tools there are shifting to other routes.
details: A settlement that stops buying a key product reshuffles the whole route. Traders need consistent demand to justify the trip.
Some of those traders might start coming our way instead. More options for us.
reactions: Someone else's self-sufficiency might accidentally benefit us.

statCheck.pass: When {other_settlement} stops buying tools, the traders carrying tools need new buyers to stay in business. We're in a position to negotiate hard because they need the sale more than we need the goods.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how it shakes out for us. We'll see who shows up.

entries.intro: A merchant sold us a sack of flour that was mostly sawdust. We didn't notice until the first loaf came out wrong.
details: The bread was gritty and tasted like wood shavings. Barely edible.
We lost the flour and the coin we paid for it.
reactions: Sawdust in the flour. That's not carelessness. That's a choice.

statCheck.pass: Cutting flour with sawdust is an old trick for stretching supply. A merchant who does it is planning to never come back, because one bad sale ends a customer relationship.
statCheck.fail: I'm angry and there's no one to be angry at. They're long gone.

entries.intro: A traveler offered to carry a message to {other_settlement} for us. No charge, just goodwill.
details: We've been meaning to send word there about what we need. This saved us a trip.
They said carrying messages is how travelers earn welcome at the next stop.
reactions: A message carried for free. The road has its own currency.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they can predict how a settlement is doing by what they buy. We buy a lot of essentials and no luxuries.
details: Essentials mean survival, luxuries mean surplus. We haven't had surplus in a while.
They said it without judgment, but the observation stung.
reactions: Our buying pattern is our biography. I just didn't realize everyone could read it.

statCheck.pass: A merchant who reads a settlement's health through purchases adjusts their inventory accordingly. They bring us less variety because they assume we can't afford it, which means we never see the options that could help us grow.
statCheck.fail: They probably see things I don't. Doesn't mean I want to hear it.

entries.intro: A traveler said they always stop here because the water tastes clean. Said most settlements have muddy wells.
details: I've never thought about our water being special. It's just water.
But they said they've tasted bad water in enough places to notice when it's good.
reactions: Clean water. Something so basic we forgot to appreciate it.

entries.intro: A merchant on the route from {other_settlement} told me they've been trading for fifteen years and this is the worst season they can remember.
details: Lower demand everywhere, tighter coin, and more settlements asking for credit instead of paying.
They said if next season isn't better, they might leave the road entirely.
reactions: When a fifteen-year trader talks about quitting, the rest of us should listen.

statCheck.pass: Veteran traders leaving the road means the economics have fundamentally shifted. The route can't sustain the same number of merchants anymore. Fewer traders means less competition and higher prices for everyone still buying.
statCheck.fail: I hope they stick it out. We need them more than they need us.

entries.intro: A traveler taught me a knot I've never seen before. Said it holds under load but releases with one pull.
details: I've been tying the same three knots my whole life. This one does something none of them can.
It took me ten tries to get it right, but once it clicked, I couldn't believe how simple it is.
reactions: One knot from a stranger and suddenly I'm rethinking how I tie everything.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said their last cart was searched by toll collectors who took a 'tax' of one item per category.
details: One tool, one bundle of cloth, one sack of grain. Just helped themselves.
The merchant said it's new and getting worse. Every stop adds a cost.
reactions: Taxes that look like theft, or theft that calls itself a tax. Hard to tell the difference.

statCheck.pass: Informal tolls that take goods instead of coin are usually unsanctioned. Individuals skimming the road for themselves. Legitimate tolls are coin-based and recorded.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the politics of it. Just sounds like robbery with a name.

entries.intro: A traveler brought their child along on the road for the first time. The kid was wide-eyed at everything.
details: Asked us a hundred questions about what we grow, what we eat, how we build things.
The parent smiled and let them ask. Said curiosity is the best education the road offers.
reactions: A kid seeing a new settlement for the first time. Everything's a discovery at that age.

entries.intro: A merchant overcharged us for lamp wicks and I didn't catch it until I got home and counted.
details: Short by four wicks in the bundle. At the price they charged, that's not a rounding error.
I can't confront them now. They're two days down the road.
reactions: Mistakes in the trader's favor. Funny how that always seems to be the direction.

statCheck.pass: Short-counting works because people don't verify until the trader is gone. The fix is counting everything at the cart before coin changes hands, no matter how impatient they look.
statCheck.fail: I should have counted before I paid. Won't make that mistake again.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've walked through every kind of weather this season. Snow, rain, heat, all on the same route.
details: They said the weather changes faster between settlements than you'd think.
By the time they reach us, whatever they left has become a story rather than a memory.
reactions: Living in one place, you forget the world has different weather just a few days away.

entries.intro: A merchant offered to trade us higher-quality {resource_type} if we could supply something they need for the return trip.
details: They need preserved food for the road back and we have plenty to spare.
A trade where both sides get exactly what they're short on. Rare but satisfying.
reactions: The best deals happen when both sides are filling a gap.

entries.intro: A traveler showed up with a broken wheel on their cart and no way to fix it. We spent half a day helping.
details: Had to pull the axle, reshape a spoke, and reset the rim. It wasn't quick work.
They offered coin but we settled for some dried fruit they had in the back.
reactions: Half a day of work for a handful of fruit. Not every trade happens on a scale.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they use our settlement as a midpoint rest. Not for trade, just for the break.
details: We're exactly the right distance between two bigger stops. A natural overnight.
They said the trade with us is a bonus, not the reason they stop.
reactions: Being someone's rest stop. It's not glamorous, but at least they keep coming.

statCheck.pass: A midpoint rest means we see every trader on the route whether they want our business or not. That's an advantage if we can make the stop worth extending.
statCheck.fail: At least the location keeps us on the map.

entries.intro: A trader brought leather goods from {other_settlement}. Softer, stronger, and properly treated. Better than anything we can make here.
details: Our leather cracks in the cold. This stuff bends like it's alive.
I bought a belt and a pair of gloves. The difference is immediate.
reactions: Good leather makes you realize you've been putting up with bad leather your whole life.

entries.intro: A merchant said they heard {other_settlement} might start taxing goods that pass through without being sold there. A pass-through fee.
details: If that happens, everything we buy from the other direction costs more.
Traders would either absorb the cost or, more likely, add it to our prices.
reactions: A tax we have no say in, on goods that don't even stop there. And we pay for it.

statCheck.pass: Pass-through fees are a settlement trying to extract value from trade that doesn't benefit them directly. It's a sign they're struggling to fund themselves through their own commerce.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the politics. I just know prices will go up.

entries.intro: A traveler mended a hole in our fishing net in exchange for supper. Did it faster than anyone here could have.
details: Their fingers moved like they'd done it a thousand times. Tight knots, even spacing.
The net's stronger now than before the hole. Better mending than original work.
reactions: Skills picked up on the road. That traveler's hands know more trades than most people in one place.

entries.intro: A merchant said they've been losing money on our stop for two seasons but keeps coming because they promised they would.
details: A promise made to someone here years ago, apparently. They didn't say who.
I don't know who they promised, but I'm grateful someone's word still holds.
reactions: A trader who keeps coming at a loss because of a promise. Not a business decision. A character one.

statCheck.pass: A merchant who visits at a loss to keep a promise is building something that doesn't show on a ledger. Trust. When things improve, we'll trade with them first because they showed up when it didn't pay to.
statCheck.fail: I hope they can afford to keep their word. Not everyone can.

entries.intro: A traveler told me the road from {other_settlement} used to be a proper trade highway. Now it's barely a path.
details: Overgrown, washed out in parts, and nobody maintains it.
They said it takes twice as long as it should because you spend half the time navigating around bad stretches.
reactions: A road nobody maintains is a road that slowly disappears. And with it, everything it connects.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a cheese from somewhere on the route that's the best thing I've tasted in months.
details: Sharp, crumbly, with a bite that stays on your tongue. Nothing like our mild stuff.
I bought a whole wheel. It'll be gone in a week at the rate I'm eating it.
reactions: A cheese good enough to make you rethink your own cooking. That's what trade is for.

entries.intro: A trader told me they rank settlements by how fast they get unloaded. We're slow because we inspect everything.
details: They want speed and we want to check what we're buying. Those goals don't align.
I'm not going to rush through a purchase so a trader can leave ten minutes earlier.
reactions: Taking our time costs us nothing but a trader's impatience. I can live with that.

statCheck.pass: Traders who pressure you to hurry are hoping you'll skip the inspection. Slow buyers catch defects and short counts. The impatience is the tell.
statCheck.fail: I just like to see what I'm paying for. Nothing wrong with that.

entries.intro: A merchant said {other_settlement} is stockpiling {resource_type}. Buying more than they need and not selling any.
details: When one settlement hoards, the supply for everyone else shrinks.
The trader said prices are already creeping up along the route because of it.
reactions: Someone else's stockpile, our shortage. Connected by a road and squeezed by the same chain.

statCheck.pass: Stockpiling is usually a sign that {other_settlement} expects trouble. Either a disruption in supply they've heard about before us, or they're cornering the market to resell at a premium later.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they're hoarding. I just feel the price going up.

entries.intro: A traveler showed me the soles of their boots. Worn completely flat from walking.
details: They've been on the road long enough to walk through an entire sole. That's months of steps.
We patched them with leather scraps. Not perfect, but they'll hold until the next proper stop.
reactions: Flat soles and still walking. Some people's determination shows in their shoes.

entries.intro: A merchant gave us a bonus sack of salt for being consistent customers. No extra charge.
details: They said loyalty matters on the road and they reward the settlements that always buy.
A whole sack of salt for free. That's worth more than they probably realize.
reactions: Loyalty rewarded with salt. In a place like this, that's real generosity.

entries.intro: A trader told me they've started carrying medicine alongside their usual goods. Said there's too much need on the route to ignore.
details: Basic things. Poultices, fever remedies, bandage linen. Not cures, but helps.
They said every settlement asks for it now. That wasn't true a few years ago.
reactions: When a trader starts carrying medicine because everyone needs it, that tells you something about the road.

statCheck.pass: Medicine demand climbing along a route means health conditions are worsening across multiple settlements at once. Usually a sign of poor harvests leading to weaker people. The trader sees the pattern before any single settlement does.
statCheck.fail: People getting sick more. That's all I know.

entries.intro: A traveler left us a small clay pot they made at their last stop. Said they make one at every settlement they visit.
details: It's rough but functional. Has a lid that fits snugly.
They said by the time they finish their route, every settlement will have one of their pots.
reactions: A pot from a traveler's hands. Useful and personal in a way bought goods aren't.

entries.intro: A merchant on the route from {other_settlement} told me they can't get insurance for their cart on this stretch anymore. Too many losses.
details: Without insurance, every broken wheel or spoiled load comes out of their own pocket.
They said if another load goes bad, they'll pull our settlement off the route entirely.
reactions: Uninsurable. That's a word I didn't want associated with where I live.

statCheck.pass: When insurers drop a route, it means the losses outpace the premiums. It's a calculation, not personal, but the effect is personal. Fewer traders willing to take the risk.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand how trading insurance works. I just know we might lose a trader over it.

entries.intro: A traveler asked to stay an extra night because they heard we have a good cook. Turned out they were right.
details: Word travels about food, apparently. Someone on the route mentioned our stew by name.
The traveler ate two helpings and said it was the best meal in five stops.
reactions: Famous for stew. There are worse things to be known for.

entries.intro: A merchant told me two of their regular settlements on this route stopped buying last season. Just stopped.
details: No warning, no explanation. They arrived and nobody came out to trade.
With two stops gone, the remaining ones have to buy more to keep the route viable.
reactions: Settlements going quiet. That puts pressure on the rest of us to pick up the slack.

statCheck.pass: Settlements that stop trading entirely are usually in a spiral. They ran out of coin, then credit, then the will to negotiate. By the time the trader shows up, there's nothing left to trade with.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they stopped. I just hope it doesn't happen here.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they carry a piece of stone from every settlement they've visited. Their bag has fifty of them.
details: Small, flat stones, each one different. They remember where each one came from.
They picked up a grey one from near our well and added it to the collection.
reactions: Fifty stones from fifty settlements. That's a life measured in small, heavy souvenirs.

entries.intro: The vinegar a trader brought is strong enough to actually preserve food properly. Ours is weak by comparison.
details: Everything we've pickled with our vinegar goes soft within a month. This stuff holds for a season.
I bought three jars. Preserving food better means less waste, which means less need.
reactions: Proper vinegar. The kind of thing that quietly changes how a household runs.

entries.intro: A merchant told me {other_settlement} is offering better rates to traders who visit more often. Trying to lock in regular service.
details: If {other_settlement} pays more per visit, traders will prioritize them over us.
We can't match the rates. We don't have the coin for bidding wars over merchant visits.
reactions: A settlement buying a trader's loyalty with coin. That's not trade. That's a contract we can't compete with.

statCheck.pass: Settlements that pay premiums for merchant frequency are betting that reliable supply drives growth fast enough to cover the cost. If it doesn't work, they burn through reserves for nothing.
statCheck.fail: I just know they can offer more than us. What happens next isn't up to me.

entries.intro: A traveler arrived soaking wet and shivering. We got them dry and warm before anything else.
details: They'd crossed a swollen stream and misjudged the depth. Lost half their pack to the water.
Sat by the fire for hours, not saying much. Sometimes the best help is a warm room.
reactions: A dry fire and no questions. That's what a cold stranger needs.

entries.intro: A merchant started their pitch before they'd even stopped the cart. I told them to sit down and have water first.
details: They looked surprised. Said most settlements start haggling while they're still unpacking.
We talked for a bit, drank water, and then traded. The conversation made the deal better.
reactions: Slowing down before trading. It costs nothing and changes the tone of everything after.

entries.intro: A trader told me the {resource_type} market is splitting. High-quality commands a premium, and low-quality barely sells at all.
details: There's no middle anymore. Either it's the best and someone will pay well, or it's the worst and nobody wants it.
We fall somewhere in between, which is the worst place to be.
reactions: The middle is disappearing. Either get better or get left behind.

statCheck.pass: Market splitting happens when buyers with coin chase quality and buyers without coin drop out entirely. The middle-tier producers like us lose both audiences. Too expensive for the poor, too rough for the rich.
statCheck.fail: I just know our stuff isn't selling like it used to.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they keep track of every kindness they receive on the road and repay it forward at the next stop.
details: We fed them last time, so this time they brought us a bundle of dried herbs from two settlements back.
The herbs are useful and the thought behind them more so.
reactions: Kindness passed along the road like a slow parcel. I like that system.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they heard a rumor that {other_settlement} might close their market to outside traders entirely.
details: If they go internal, every trader on this route loses a stop and the rest of us lose variety.
The merchant didn't seem to believe it, but they mentioned it like a warning.
reactions: A settlement closing its market sounds extreme, but things that sound extreme happen eventually.

statCheck.pass: Settlements that close their markets are usually protecting local producers from outside competition. It works short-term, but the isolation from goods they can't make themselves always catches up.
statCheck.fail: It's a rumor. I'll worry about it when I see it.

entries.intro: A traveler carved a small animal figure from a piece of wood while waiting for supper. Left it on the table as thanks.
details: Delicate work. You could see the muscles in the legs and the ears were sharp.
One of the kids claimed it immediately. It's their favorite thing now.
reactions: A carved figure in exchange for a meal. Some trades don't involve coin at all.

entries.intro: A merchant told me our settlement is the only one on the route that always has firewood ready for travelers. It's why they stop.
details: We keep a woodpile stocked near the common area. It's just habit.
Apparently it's not habit elsewhere. Most places make travelers gather their own.
reactions: A woodpile as a competitive advantage. The bar is low, but we're above it.

statCheck.pass: Small comforts compound. A woodpile, clean water, and a warm greeting add up to a reputation that brings traders back. Each one costs us almost nothing but together they're worth more than a discount.
statCheck.fail: We just do what feels right. Didn't know it was unusual.

entries.intro: A trader brought dye from {other_settlement} in a color I've never seen before. Deep blue, like a clear sky at dusk.
details: The dye takes well to cloth and doesn't bleed when wet. Rare quality.
We don't need dye for anything practical, but I bought a small pot anyway.
reactions: A beautiful color for no practical reason. Sometimes that's enough.
