entries.intro: A merchant on the {other_settlement} route said they're adding a second cart next season. Business is growing enough to justify it.
details: More cart means more stock, more variety, and hopefully better prices from bulk.
They said if all goes well, they'll bring things we've been asking for that never fit on one cart.
reactions: A trader growing their business. That's good for them and good for us.

entries.intro: A traveler said they lost their travel partner somewhere on the road between here and {other_settlement}. They separated and never reconnected.
details: Took different paths at a fork and each assumed the other would catch up.
The traveler's been alone for a week now. You could hear it in how much they talked.
reactions: Traveling alone after being paired. The quiet must be louder than usual.

entries.intro: A merchant told me the demand for {resource_type} is dropping everywhere. Nobody's buying what they used to.
details: They said they're sitting on stock they can't move. Every settlement has enough or doesn't want more.
If demand stays low, the price crashes and anyone producing it takes the hit.
reactions: A market nobody wants to buy into. If we produce any, we'll feel that.

statCheck.pass: Demand drops across multiple settlements at once usually mean a substitute emerged or a major buyer stopped ordering. One big buyer canceling creates a ripple that reaches every producer on the chain.
statCheck.fail: I don't follow the reasons. I just see the traders with full carts and empty pockets.

entries.intro: A traveler said they judge how safe a settlement is by whether the children play outside. Ours do.
details: They said some places the kids stay indoors and the streets are empty except for adults.
Here the kids run around without anyone worrying. The traveler said that means something.
reactions: Kids playing in the open. I never thought of it as a sign of anything, but I suppose it is.

entries.intro: A merchant said they had to turn around halfway to {other_settlement} because the route was blocked by fallen trees.
details: No way through with a loaded cart. They lost two days and came back to us instead.
We got a second visit this month, which never happens. But it cost them.
reactions: A blocked road sent a trader back our way. Accidental luck for us, terrible luck for them.

entries.intro: A trader brought thread dyed in six different colors. We usually only see undyed.
details: Red, green, yellow, blue, brown, and a deep purple that caught everyone's eye.
Half the settlement bought some. It's been a while since we had a reason to make something colorful.
reactions: Colored thread. Such a small thing, and yet people were smiling over it all evening.

entries.intro: A merchant said every settlement complains about being the last stop on the route. Including {other_settlement}.
details: Doesn't matter where they actually are on the route. Everyone thinks they're getting the worst of it.
The merchant said complaining about position is the most universal thing settlements do.
reactions: Everyone thinks they're last. At least our complaint is shared.

statCheck.pass: The merchant told me, privately, that we actually are near the end. Most settlements just think they are. We're right about it.
statCheck.fail: Maybe we're all wrong. Maybe nobody's last.

entries.intro: A traveler brought a board game they carved themselves. We played it after supper and nobody understood the rules.
details: They kept changing the rules as we went. I think they were making half of it up.
By the end we were laughing too hard to care who won.
reactions: A game nobody understood and everyone enjoyed. Best evening in weeks.

entries.intro: A merchant told me our {resource_type} exports are cleaner and more consistent than last year. Said we're improving.
details: We didn't change anything deliberately. Maybe we're just getting better at what we do through repetition.
The merchant said consistency is worth more than quality on a trade route.
reactions: Improving without trying. I'll take credit for it anyway.

entries.intro: A trader said the merchant who usually supplies us with {resource_type} has retired. Nobody's taken over their route yet.
details: They were our most reliable source. Came like clockwork every six weeks.
Now there's a gap on the route and nobody to fill it.
reactions: One trader retires and suddenly the supply chain has a hole the size of a person.

statCheck.pass: When a route trader retires without a successor, the route stays empty until someone else realizes it's profitable. That gap can last a season or longer, and the settlements on it go without until someone claims the run.
statCheck.fail: I just know our regular trader is gone and nobody's replaced them.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they once got lost for three days in fog between {other_settlement} and here. Walked in circles.
details: No landmarks, no sun, just grey in every direction. They followed a stream until it led somewhere.
They said arriving here felt like waking up from a dream.
reactions: Three days in fog. I can barely imagine the relief of seeing a rooftop.

entries.intro: A merchant gave us first pick of their stock before selling to anyone else on the route. Said we earned it by always paying on time.
details: First pick means the best quality, not the leftovers after bigger settlements have chosen.
It's the first time I've felt like a priority instead of an afterthought.
reactions: First pick for paying on time. Reliability paying off in the most practical way.

entries.intro: A merchant told me a settlement on the route tried to hold their goods hostage until they lowered prices. Refused to let the cart leave.
details: The merchant waited them out. Sat in the cart for two days until the settlement backed down.
They said they'll never visit that settlement again.
reactions: Holding a trader's cart hostage. That's a good way to make sure nobody comes to your settlement.

statCheck.pass: Word of a hostage incident spreads to every trader on the route within weeks. That settlement won't see a cart for a long time, and when they finally do, prices will include a risk premium.
statCheck.fail: I just know I'd never want to be on a trader's bad side like that.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've been counting the days since they left home. Four hundred and twelve.
details: Four hundred days on the road. I can't imagine being away from one place that long.
They said at some point the road becomes home and every settlement is a room in it.
reactions: A year and more on the road. I don't know if that's freedom or something else.

entries.intro: A trader said they're the only merchant left on our stretch of the route. The other two switched to more profitable runs.
details: Three merchants down to one. Our choices just shrank considerably.
One merchant means one price, one schedule, and no alternatives.
reactions: A monopoly not by ambition but by attrition. Same result, though.

statCheck.pass: A last-remaining trader on a route will inevitably raise prices because the competition that kept them fair is gone. It's not greed. It's economics without a counterweight.
statCheck.fail: We're stuck with one trader. I try not to think about what that means.

entries.intro: A traveler asked if we'd ever considered sending someone to learn a trade in {other_settlement}. Said it could change what we're able to do here.
details: The idea isn't bad, but we can't spare anyone for that long.
Losing a pair of hands for a season to gain a skill. That's a gamble not everyone agrees on.
reactions: Trading a person's time now for the settlement's future. Easier said than agreed upon.

entries.intro: A merchant brought fresh fruit wrapped in leaves. It was ripe and sweet, nothing like the dried stuff we usually get.
details: They must have picked it up within a day's travel. Any longer and it would have turned.
I'd forgotten what fresh fruit tastes like. The difference is enormous.
reactions: Fresh fruit. A luxury for us, a normal thing somewhere closer to the source.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they dream about the same road every night. The same stretch, the same hills, the same sky.
details: They said when you walk a route enough times, it lives inside you even when your eyes are closed.
I dream about my house and they dream about a road. Different lives, same repetition.
reactions: Dreaming of the road. That's either peace or a cage, depending on how you feel about it.

entries.intro: A merchant said they'll only trade with us if we can guarantee the same quantity next time. We can't.
details: Our output varies season to season. Some years we have extra, some years we barely cover our own needs.
A guarantee we can't keep is worse than no deal at all.
reactions: Consistency is what they want and exactly what we can't promise.

statCheck.pass: A trader demanding guaranteed volume is building a locked supply chain. Once you commit, they set the price because they know you've already planned around the sale.
statCheck.fail: I just can't promise what I don't control.

entries.intro: A traveler said they measure time by settlements, not days. We're three settlements from {other_settlement} by their count.
details: Days are abstract on the road. Settlements are concrete. You can picture them, plan for them.
I've never thought about distance that way. Makes the world feel smaller.
reactions: Three settlements away. A different unit of distance, but somehow more real.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a sample of {resource_type} from a new source and asked if we wanted to switch suppliers.
details: Better quality and slightly cheaper. The catch is less reliable delivery.
Trading certainty for quality. I told them I'd think about it.
reactions: Every trade decision has a trade-off inside it. That's the nature of choices.

entries.intro: A traveler told me the night sky on the road is brighter than anything we see from the settlement. No smoke, no torches, just stars.
details: They said lying on their back in an open field, the sky looks deeper than it does from anywhere with a roof.
I realized I haven't been outside the settlement after dark in months.
reactions: A sky full of stars. Something the road gives that a settlement takes away.

entries.intro: A merchant brought wax-sealed jars of honey from {other_settlement}. The real thing, not the thin stuff we've been getting.
details: Thick, dark, and tastes like flowers I've never smelled. One jar will last us a month.
The price was steep but I didn't argue. You can't fake honey like that.
reactions: Real honey from a real source. Worth what they asked and then some.

entries.intro: A trader said {other_settlement} is training their own people to be merchants. They want to control the trade themselves.
details: If they succeed, independent traders on this route get squeezed out.
The merchant looked worried when they told me. Their livelihood depends on settlements needing middlemen.
reactions: A settlement cutting out the middleman. Good for them, uncertain for the rest of us who depend on the middleman.

statCheck.pass: Self-trained merchants serve their home settlement's interests first. They'll prioritize filling orders for {other_settlement} and sell leftovers to the rest of the route. We go from customer to afterthought.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how it'll play out. Change on the route always takes time to reach us.

entries.intro: A traveler asked me to describe our settlement in one word. I said 'stubborn.'
details: They laughed and said stubborn is better than 'desperate' or 'dying,' which is what some settlements earn.
Stubborn means we're still here and planning to stay.
reactions: Stubborn. I stand by it.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} told me they've started carrying seeds alongside their usual stock. More settlements are asking for them.
details: Seeds are light, cheap to transport, and every settlement wants different varieties.
They said it started as a favor and turned into the most profitable thing on the cart.
reactions: Seeds as a trade good. Light cargo, heavy impact.

entries.intro: A traveler said they've been robbed twice this year and still keep walking the route.
details: Lost their pack both times. Rebuilt from nothing both times.
They said the road is the only life they know, and losing things is part of it.
reactions: Robbed twice and still walking. Either brave or out of options, probably both.

statCheck.pass: Travelers who get robbed repeatedly on the same route and keep going are usually carrying nothing of value anymore. They've switched to trades that fit in a pocket. The big loads move to guarded caravans and the solo walkers carry knowledge instead of goods.
statCheck.fail: I just hope it doesn't happen near here.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a new kind of firestarter that catches with one strike. Changed everything.
details: No coaxing, no blowing on embers, no hoping. One strike and it's lit.
I bought three of them. One for the house, one for the shed, one for when I lose the first two.
reactions: Fire on the first try. The kind of small improvement that reshapes a daily routine.

entries.intro: A trader said {other_settlement} is struggling to fill their own needs and has stopped exporting. We used to buy from them through middlemen.
details: A settlement that can't feed itself can't feed the route. That ripples outward.
The trader said they're routing around {other_settlement} entirely now.
reactions: Someone else's struggle becoming our shortage. The road connects everything, including the bad.

statCheck.pass: When a settlement stops exporting, it usually means they had a production failure and are consuming their reserves. The length of the export freeze tells you how bad it is. One season is a bad year, two seasons is structural.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what happened there. I just know we're not getting what we used to.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've visited over a hundred settlements in their lifetime. Said each one teaches them something.
details: They said the smallest settlements often have the best solutions to common problems because they can't afford waste.
I asked what we taught them and they said. Patience.
reactions: Patience. I suppose that's what you learn in a place where you wait for everything.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} brought nails. Straight, uniform, and they don't bend when you hammer them.
details: Our homemade nails bend every third strike. These went in clean every time.
I bought a whole pouch. Good nails save time on every project.
reactions: A nail that does what it's supposed to. A relief and slightly embarrassing that our own can't manage it.

entries.intro: A traveler said they won't travel alone after dark anymore. Too many close calls.
details: Not with people. With the terrain. Holes, roots, loose ground, and no light to see them.
They said a twisted ankle on the road with nobody around is as dangerous as anything else out there.
reactions: The road at night. It's not the dark that's dangerous. It's the ground underneath it.

entries.intro: A merchant said they're considering adding our settlement to their priority list. We'd get visits every month instead of every six weeks.
details: Monthly visits would mean fresher goods and less rationing between stops.
They said it depends on whether we keep buying at the current level.
reactions: Monthly visits. That would change how we plan everything.

entries.intro: A trader said the weight of their cart has gone up every year as settlements ask for more and heavier goods.
details: Heavier loads mean slower travel, more wear on the cart, and higher prices to cover the strain.
They said eventually the cart won't be enough and they'll need a wagon.
reactions: The demand grows and the cart groans. Everything has a carrying limit.

entries.intro: A merchant told me {other_settlement} has started paying premiums for {resource_type} and it's pulling supply away from the rest of the route.
details: When one buyer outpays everyone else, the trader sends everything in that direction.
We can't match the price. We'll be waiting for whatever's left over.
reactions: Outbid by a settlement with deeper pockets. It's not unfair, but it feels that way.

statCheck.pass: Premium buyers create artificial scarcity on the route because traders redirect stock to the highest bidder. It's rational for the trader, devastating for the smaller settlements that depended on that supply at normal prices.
statCheck.fail: I just know we're getting less. The reasons don't change what's on our shelf.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they started walking the road after losing their home. Five years later, the road is their home.
details: They said the first year was grief, the second was survival, and the third was something like peace.
Now they can't imagine going back to one place. The idea of walls feels confining.
reactions: Five years and the road became enough. Different life, but they seem steady in it.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they weigh our coin before accepting it. Said counterfeit has been showing up on the route.
details: Light coins clipped at the edges or cast from cheaper metal. Hard to spot by eye.
They showed me the difference on a scale. Our coin was fine, but the fact that they check says a lot.
reactions: Counterfeit coin on the road. One more thing to worry about that I didn't expect.

statCheck.pass: Counterfeit coin on a trade route means someone is minting at volume, which requires equipment and access to base metal. It's organized, not opportunistic, and it erodes trust in every transaction on the route.
statCheck.fail: I don't know where it's coming from. I just know our coins are real.

entries.intro: A traveler left us a hand-drawn map of edible plants they've found along the road. Labeled and everything.
details: Some of the plants grow near us and we didn't know they were edible.
The drawings are rough but clear enough to identify by. We've already tested two.
reactions: Free knowledge, carefully drawn. Worth more than most of what traders bring on their carts.

entries.intro: A merchant said they're raising all their prices next visit. Flat increase, no exceptions.
details: Said their own costs have gone up. Food, tolls, cart repair. Everything costs more so everything they sell costs more.
I can't argue with the logic, but the numbers are going to hurt.
reactions: Prices going up because costs went up. The explanation doesn't make the coin stretch any further.

statCheck.pass: A flat increase across all goods is usually larger than the actual cost increase. The trader rounds up on every item and the rounding becomes profit. Real cost increases hit different goods differently.
statCheck.fail: Costs go up, prices go up. I don't know enough to argue.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they carry a repair kit for everything. Cloth, leather, wood, metal. Fixes anything on the road.
details: Needle, thread, wax, a small file, a strip of rawhide, and a hammer that folds flat.
They said the kit weighs almost nothing and saves them a stop at every other settlement.
reactions: A kit for everything. That's self-sufficiency packed into a pouch.

entries.intro: A trader told me the route between us and {other_settlement} is the quietest stretch they travel. Not good quiet. Empty quiet.
details: No other travelers, no camps, no signs of recent movement. Just road.
They said an empty road between settlements means the connection is weakening.
reactions: An empty road between two settlements. That's isolation measured in footprints.

statCheck.pass: When a road goes empty between two settlements, it means neither has enough to offer the other to justify the trip. Trade dies before roads do. The path stays but the reason to walk it disappears.
statCheck.fail: I noticed the road's been quiet. Didn't think about what it means.

entries.intro: A traveler cooked for us. Used our ingredients and made something we'd never thought to make.
details: Same pantry, completely different result. They combined things we keep separate.
Everyone asked for the method. The traveler wrote it down on a scrap of cloth.
reactions: New ideas from old ingredients. That's the value of someone who's eaten in fifty different places.

entries.intro: A merchant told me our {resource_type} would sell better if we cleaned it before packing. Said presentation matters.
details: I didn't think buyers cared about how it looks as long as the quality is there.
They said two identical products, one clean and one dusty, sell at different prices. Appearance is value.
reactions: An extra step that costs us time but earns us coin. I should have thought of it myself.

entries.intro: A traveler said they keep promises to every settlement they visit. If they say they'll bring something, they bring it.
details: They brought the twine I asked for last time. Remembered without writing it down.
In a world of broken commitments on the road, that kind of memory is worth trusting.
reactions: A traveler who keeps their word. I'll remember that the way they remembered my twine.

entries.intro: A merchant said they've been keeping a ledger of every settlement's {resource_type} orders for five years. We're their most consistent buyers.
details: Same things, same quantities, same timing. We don't surprise them.
They said predictable customers are the backbone of a stable route.
reactions: Predictable. Not exciting, but apparently valuable.

entries.intro: A traveler warned us that a merchant on the route has been selling the same {resource_type} to multiple settlements, promising each one an exclusive supply.
details: They tell each settlement they're getting the only batch, which justifies a higher price.
The same goods, repackaged and relabeled at every stop.
reactions: Exclusive supply sold five times over. The oldest lie on the road, and it still works.

statCheck.pass: A trader selling 'exclusive' supply to multiple buyers is collecting a premium from each one for scarcity that doesn't exist. The only defense is talking to other settlements, which isolated stops rarely do.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if we've been caught by that trick. I'll pay more attention.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a shipment packed in dried lavender. Everything smelled wonderful when we opened it.
details: They said the lavender keeps insects out during transport. Practical and pleasant.
The lavender itself was worth keeping. We dried it further and scattered it in the storage room.
reactions: Packing material that doubles as a gift. Clever and generous.

entries.intro: A trader told me they've stopped accepting barter from us. Coin only from now on.
details: They said our barter goods don't move fast enough on the rest of the route.
We have more goods than coin, so this puts us in a difficult position.
reactions: Coin only. Easy for them to say when we have more to trade than to spend.

statCheck.pass: A trader switching from barter to coin-only is standardizing their operation. Coin spends anywhere, barter goods only sell where someone wants them. It's efficient for the trader but punishes settlements that are asset-rich and coin-poor.
statCheck.fail: I understand the logic. I just can't easily follow it.

entries.intro: A traveler said they've been to settlements where people don't greet strangers at all. Just stare until you state your business.
details: No wave, no nod, just silence and watchful eyes.
They said it makes you feel like you're trespassing even on public ground.
reactions: Silence as a greeting. I'm glad we do things differently.

entries.intro: A merchant said they're carrying more preserved food than trade goods this trip. Too many settlements asking for basics instead of extras.
details: When the cart is mostly food, it means the route is feeding people, not growing them.
They said it's been like this for two seasons now.
reactions: A cart full of food instead of goods. That's a trade route operating at survival level.

statCheck.pass: When merchant carts shift from trade goods to food, it means settlements are choosing survival over development. The route becomes a supply line instead of a marketplace, and the merchant becomes a lifeline instead of a business partner.
statCheck.fail: I just notice the cart looking different. More sacks, fewer crates.

entries.intro: A traveler patched our leaky water barrel with a technique using heated pine resin. Holds perfectly now.
details: We'd been losing water through that crack for weeks. Tried cloth, tried mud, nothing held.
The traveler said they learned it from someone three settlements back. Knowledge traveling the road.
reactions: A fix we couldn't figure out, solved by someone passing through. The road teaches as much as any workshop.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they lost a customer settlement this year because it simply ceased to function. People drifted away one by one.
details: No disaster, no conflict. Just attrition. Fewer people each visit until there was nobody left to trade with.
The merchant said the last time they went, the buildings were still standing but nobody was inside them.
reactions: A settlement that emptied slowly. That's a quieter kind of ending than most.

statCheck.pass: Settlements that lose population by attrition are usually ones where young people leave for better opportunities and the old can't maintain things alone. It's a cycle. As people leave, conditions worsen, which drives more to leave.
statCheck.fail: Sad to think about. I hope it doesn't happen here.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've seen our goods for sale in {other_settlement}. Marked up, of course, but recognizable.
details: Our packing, our marks. Someone's reselling what they buy from us at a profit.
I don't mind. It means what we make is worth more than we're charging.
reactions: Our goods in a settlement I've never visited. That's a kind of reach I didn't expect.

entries.intro: A merchant asked us what we'd want if they could bring anything. We couldn't agree on a single answer.
details: Everyone wanted something different. Better tools, cloth, medicine, lamp oil, fresh seeds.
The merchant said our inability to prioritize is our biggest weakness as customers.
reactions: Can't agree on what we need most. That's either abundance or confusion.

entries.intro: A trader brought us cloth treated against rain. It sheds water instead of soaking it up.
details: We've been using untreated canvas and wringing it out every time it rains.
One sheet of treated cloth over the storage area and everything stays dry.
reactions: Dry storage. The difference between goods that last and goods that rot.

entries.intro: A traveler said they've been walking the route for so long that some settlements remember them by name. We don't.
details: They seemed a little hurt by it. They've been stopping here for years.
I felt bad. Faces blur when everyone's just passing through.
reactions: A regular we didn't recognize. We should pay more attention to the people, not just the goods.

entries.intro: A merchant said they've heard good things about our {resource_type}. Word is spreading along the route.
details: Other settlements have been asking if the trader can bring them some of ours specifically.
Demand for something we make. That's a position I didn't think we'd be in.
reactions: A reputation for quality, earned without trying. The best kind.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they don't sleep well in settlements anymore. Too used to the quiet of the open road.
details: They said the sounds of other people. Coughing, doors, footsteps, keep them awake.
After years of sleeping alone under the sky, a roof feels like a cage.
reactions: A person who can't sleep under a roof. The road changes people in ways you don't expect.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} arrived with half their usual stock. Said the rest was spoken for before they left.
details: Pre-orders from bigger settlements that pay more. We get what's left after the priority customers.
Half a cart is better than no cart, but the choices were thin.
reactions: Getting the leftovers of the leftovers. Our position on the route summed up in one visit.

statCheck.pass: Pre-ordering is how larger settlements lock in supply. They pay upfront and guarantee volume, which pushes smaller settlements to the back of the line. The system rewards who can pay first, not who needs it most.
statCheck.fail: We take what comes. That's all we can do.

entries.intro: A traveler told me this settlement feels like a place where people actually care about each other. Not everywhere does.
details: They said they've been to places where neighbors don't even nod at each other.
Here, everyone checked on the traveler at least once during their stay.
reactions: Caring about each other. We don't think of it as special, but apparently it is.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they're not making the trip to {other_settlement} anymore. Said the road isn't worth the return.
details: They used to do the full loop every season. Now they cut it short and double back.
Fewer stops means fewer chances for us to get what we need.
reactions: One less route on the map. The world feels a little smaller when that happens.

statCheck.pass: When a merchant drops a stop, it's because the margin collapsed. Either the settlement stopped buying or the road got too expensive. Either way, that settlement's supply just dried up.
statCheck.fail: I just know they're not going there anymore. That's all I heard.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they carry a bundle of letters for people between settlements. No charge, just goodwill.
details: Letters from family, short notes about prices, sometimes just a drawing from a child.
They said the letters weigh nothing and the gratitude at each stop is worth more than coin.
reactions: Words carried on foot between settlements. Slow, but they get there.

entries.intro: A trader told me {other_settlement} has been undercutting everyone on {resource_type} prices. Selling it for barely anything.
details: If they can produce it that cheaply, the rest of us can't compete.
The merchant seemed nervous. Cheap goods from one place reshapes the whole route.
reactions: Undercut by someone who can afford to sell for less. Not much to do about that.

statCheck.pass: Dumping cheap goods on the route is usually temporary. They're burning through a surplus or trying to crush competition. The question is whether anyone else can hold out long enough to survive it.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand how they can sell so low. Maybe they know something we don't.

entries.intro: A traveler stopped here just to rest. Didn't buy anything, didn't sell anything, just sat down and breathed.
details: They said some settlements don't let you linger if you're not trading.
Here they sat for an afternoon, ate what we shared, and left quietly the next morning.
reactions: A stop just to rest. I like that we're the kind of place that allows that.

entries.intro: A merchant said they've noticed our orders getting smaller each visit. Asked if everything's alright.
details: We're buying less because we have less to spend. Simple as that.
I told them we're fine. Not sure they believed me.
reactions: Shrinking orders. The merchant sees the numbers before we admit the trend.

statCheck.pass: Declining orders are the first sign a trader looks for when a settlement is contracting. After two or three visits of shrinking orders, they start planning to drop the stop entirely.
statCheck.fail: We just didn't need as much this time. That's all.

entries.intro: A traveler taught one of the kids how to tie a knot that holds under strain. The kid's been tying everything to everything since.
details: Fence posts, barrel lids, tool handles. All knotted with the same loop and pull.
The traveler laughed when they saw what they'd started.
reactions: One good knot passed from a stranger to a child. That's a gift that outlasts anything on a cart.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said they're considering taking on a partner. The route is too long for one person.
details: Splitting the run means they each do half and meet in the middle to swap goods.
They asked if we had anyone who'd be interested. We don't.
reactions: Too much road for one person. I understand the feeling, even without a cart.

statCheck.pass: I thought about volunteering, honestly. Half a route, regular income, a reason to see what's out there. But leaving here means leaving everything I know.
statCheck.fail: Nobody around here has the time or the courage for the road.

entries.intro: A trader said our {resource_type} has gone downhill lately. Not as clean, not as sorted.
details: I didn't notice the drop in quality until they pointed it out.
They said other settlements have been picking up their standards while ours slipped.
reactions: Hard to hear, but the trader doesn't have a reason to lie. We need to do better.

statCheck.pass: Honestly, I think we've been cutting corners because nobody complained until now. Easier to let standards slip when nobody's watching.
statCheck.fail: Could be nothing. Maybe they just had a bad batch to compare against.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they haven't spoken to another person in eleven days. We were the first voices they'd heard.
details: Eleven days without conversation. They said the first few words felt strange coming out of their mouth.
I noticed they talked faster than normal, like they were making up for lost time.
reactions: Eleven days of silence on the road. Makes you appreciate the sound of a simple greeting.

entries.intro: A merchant brought dyed cloth from {other_settlement}. Deep blues and reds we can't get here.
details: The colors don't fade in wash, either. Whatever they use as dye, it sets permanently.
I bought a piece just to have something bright in the house.
reactions: Color from somewhere else. A small luxury that changes how a room feels.

entries.intro: A trader told me two settlements on the route are in a price war over {resource_type}. Both keep lowering their price to win the contract.
details: Good for the buyer, terrible for the producers. Neither can sustain those prices for long.
The trader's just buying from whoever is cheapest that week.
reactions: Two settlements racing to the bottom. Someone wins and both lose.

statCheck.pass: A price war between producers always ends the same way. One gives up, raises prices, and the survivor raises theirs even higher to recoup losses. Everyone pays more in the end than they would have without the fight.
statCheck.fail: I just know the prices keep changing. Can't keep track of why.

entries.intro: A traveler said they've started sketching every settlement they visit. Keeps a leather-bound book of drawings.
details: They showed me their drawing of us. Didn't flatter us, but it was honest.
Something about seeing your home through a stranger's eyes. You notice things you'd stopped seeing.
reactions: Our settlement in someone else's sketchbook. Strange to be remembered that way.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they got cheated on a deal at their last stop. Paid for full weight and got half.
details: They didn't catch it until they were already back on the road. Weighed the sack and it was light.
Going back costs more than the missing goods are worth, so they ate the loss.
reactions: Cheated and can't go back. The road punishes trust in the worst way.

statCheck.pass: Short-weighing a trader only works once, because merchants talk to each other at crossroads and a settlement known for cheating stops getting visits. The short-term gain costs them long-term supply.
statCheck.fail: I'd be furious. Not much you can do about it from the road, though.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they carry a small stone from every settlement they've slept in. Their pouch must weigh a fortune by now.
details: They pulled out ours. A flat grey one from the path near the well.
Said each stone is a night they survived. I thought that was a heavy way to think about it.
reactions: A stone from every stop. Some people carry their history in their pocket.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said they might start visiting us twice a season instead of once. Our demand justifies it.
details: Twice means less rationing, fresher stock, and more options each time.
They said it depends on whether the road stays safe enough for the extra trip.
reactions: Twice a season. Didn't think we'd earned that kind of attention on the route.

statCheck.pass: Twice a season also means the merchant expects us to buy twice as much. If we don't meet that expectation, we go from promising to disappointing in one visit.
statCheck.fail: More visits sounds good. I'll take it.

entries.intro: A trader said they've been hauling {resource_type} for years and the quality varies wildly depending on where it comes from.
details: Some settlements take care with preparation and some just throw it in a sack.
They said ours is middle of the road. Not the best, not the worst.
reactions: Middle of the road. Honest feedback, even if it doesn't feel like a compliment.

statCheck.pass: Middle of the road means the trader charges us mid-range prices too. If we cleaned and sorted better, we'd move up the list. The gap between average and good is mostly effort.
statCheck.fail: We do what we can with what we have.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they once helped push a merchant's cart out of mud for half a day. The merchant gave them free passage on the cart for a week.
details: Half a day of work for a week of riding. The traveler called it the best trade they ever made.
They said most merchants won't let anyone on the cart, but desperation changes the rules.
reactions: A week of rest earned with half a day of mud. That's the road making its own kind of deals.

entries.intro: A merchant said they've been overcharged at every stop this season. Everyone blaming the same excuse. Hard times.
details: Hard times for everyone means higher prices for the trader, which means higher prices for us.
They said the whole route is squeezing them from both ends.
reactions: Everyone's costs go up and nobody absorbs the difference. It just rolls downhill.

statCheck.pass: When every settlement on a route raises prices at the same time, it usually means one large settlement set the new rate and the rest followed. Nobody wants to be the one selling cheap while their neighbor charges more.
statCheck.fail: Everything costs more. That's all I know.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they measure the health of a settlement by how quickly people smile at a stranger. We scored well.
details: They said suspicious stares mean the place is struggling. Quick smiles mean it's comfortable enough to be generous.
I never thought about it that way, but it makes sense.
reactions: Quick smiles. I'll take that as a compliment for the whole settlement.

entries.intro: A trader told me {other_settlement} has stopped accepting certain types of barter. Coin or specific goods only.
details: They've narrowed what they'll take in trade, which means fewer settlements can deal with them.
The merchant said it's efficient for {other_settlement} but frustrating for everyone else.
reactions: Selective barter. Good for the one making the rules, harder for the rest of us.

statCheck.pass: Restricting barter types is how a settlement forces the route onto their terms. Accept only what you need, reject everything else, and suddenly you set the exchange rate for everyone.
statCheck.fail: I don't really follow the details. I just know it's harder to trade there now.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a grinding wheel from {other_settlement}. Better made than anything we have.
details: The balance on it is perfect. Spins smooth, doesn't wobble, holds an edge on the stone.
I watched one of ours try it and their face said everything. We've been making do with junk.
reactions: A proper tool from somewhere that knows how to make them. Embarrassing and wonderful at the same time.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they walk the same route every year and can tell which settlements are growing and which are shrinking just by the state of the road near them.
details: A well-packed road with clear edges means people are maintaining it. Overgrowth means nobody cares.
They said our stretch of road is holding steady. Not better, not worse.
reactions: Steady. I'll take that over shrinking.

statCheck.pass: Holding steady worries me more than they let on. The settlements on either side of us are growing, which means steady is the same as falling behind.
statCheck.fail: Steady sounds fine to me. Could be worse.

entries.intro: A trader said a settlement on the route has been hoarding {resource_type} instead of selling it. Driving prices up for everyone.
details: They're sitting on a stockpile waiting for the price to peak before they release it.
Meanwhile the rest of the route scrapes by with whatever trickles through.
reactions: Hoarding what others need. Legal, I suppose, but it doesn't sit right.

statCheck.pass: Hoarding only works if you can time the release. Hold too long and a new supplier fills the gap, leaving you stuck with a stockpile nobody needs at the price you wanted.
statCheck.fail: I just know we can't get what we need. The reason doesn't help us much.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they once traded a song for a meal at a settlement that had never heard music from outside.
details: They said the whole settlement gathered to listen. Afterward, someone insisted on feeding them for the night.
A song costs nothing to carry and apparently buys a lot in the right place.
reactions: A meal for a song. I like the idea that the road still has room for that kind of trade.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they're carrying fewer luxury items this season. Nobody's buying them.
details: The fancy goods stay on the cart while the basics sell out immediately.
They said when a route shifts to essentials only, it means everyone's tightening up.
reactions: No room for extras when the basics are all you can afford.

statCheck.pass: A shift from luxury to essentials on a trade route usually takes two seasons to reverse. Even after conditions improve, people stay cautious and keep buying basics until they're sure the downturn is over.
statCheck.fail: Tough times for everyone, I suppose. We're no different.

entries.intro: A traveler shared a trick for keeping boots waterproof using rendered fat. Works better than anything we've tried.
details: Rub it into the leather, let it sit overnight, and the water beads off like rain on a leaf.
I tried it on my oldest pair and they shed water for the first time in years.
reactions: A traveler's trick worth more than most of what we buy. Simple and it works.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they won't extend credit to us anymore. Cash up front or nothing.
details: We've always paid eventually, but 'eventually' isn't good enough for a trader who has costs every day.
I can't blame them. But it means we have to save up between visits instead of buying what we need when we need it.
reactions: No more credit. Fair from their side, painful from ours.

statCheck.pass: A trader cutting credit usually means they've been burned by another settlement that never paid. One default poisons the whole route because the trader can't risk it again.
statCheck.fail: I don't like it, but I understand. We'll manage.

entries.intro: A traveler said they met another traveler on the road heading the opposite direction. They sat down, compared notes on every settlement, and parted ways richer for it.
details: Between the two of them they knew what every settlement needed and what every settlement had to sell.
They said that single conversation saved them both a week of wasted stops.
reactions: Two travelers comparing notes. Information traveling the road on foot, one conversation at a time.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said they were robbed of half their stock two days out. Arrived with almost nothing.
details: What they had left was damaged and poorly repacked. They sold it at a steep discount just to cover costs.
I felt bad haggling, but we needed what they had.
reactions: A merchant robbed on the road and we're the ones picking through the leftovers. Nobody won that day.

statCheck.pass: I haggled harder than I should have. They were desperate and I knew it. I'm not proud of that, but the goods were there and the price was right.
statCheck.fail: Terrible luck for them. We did what we could.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've been to a settlement where every building has a rain barrel. Smart and simple.
details: They said it takes almost no effort and gives the settlement water independence during dry stretches.
I mentioned it to a few people here. Got a lot of shrugs.
reactions: A good idea from the road. Whether we use it is another matter.

entries.intro: A trader told me {other_settlement} is producing {resource_type} so good that settlements farther down the route are specifically requesting it by name.
details: Named-source goods fetch higher prices because buyers trust the quality.
Meanwhile our version of the same thing sits on the cart until someone takes it at a discount.
reactions: Their name on it makes it worth more. Reputation is its own kind of currency.

statCheck.pass: Named-source demand is self-reinforcing. Once buyers start asking for it, the price rises, which funds better production, which increases the quality gap. We'd need years of investment to compete.
statCheck.fail: I'm sure they're doing something we're not. Can't say what.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they always carry extra socks. Said dry feet are the difference between making the next settlement and not.
details: They showed me their pack. Three extra pairs, each rolled tight and wrapped in waxed cloth.
Simple thing, but after walking all day in wet boots, I understood completely.
reactions: Dry socks as survival gear. The road teaches priorities no settlement would.

entries.intro: A merchant told me a settlement they used to visit has started producing everything it needs on its own. Doesn't need traders anymore.
details: Self-sufficient sounds good until you realize it means they're cut off from anything they can't make themselves.
The trader said they still stop by out of habit, but the cart barely loses a thing.
reactions: A settlement that doesn't need the route. I can't decide if that's strength or isolation.

statCheck.pass: Self-sufficiency means no single shortage can collapse you, but it also means no outside innovation reaches you. The settlements that trade the most are usually the ones that improve the fastest.
statCheck.fail: Good for them, I suppose. I wouldn't want to do without the traders, though.

entries.intro: A traveler from {other_settlement} said they left because they wanted to see what else was out there. Five settlements later, they're still looking.
details: They said every settlement has something the last one didn't, and every one is missing something the last one had.
I asked if they'd found what they were looking for. They laughed and said they'd stopped looking and started collecting.
reactions: Still searching after five stops. Maybe the searching is the point.

entries.intro: A merchant brought candles from {other_settlement} that burn twice as long as ours. I bought a dozen.
details: They use a thicker wick and a harder wax. The flame's smaller but steadier.
Twice the burn time means half the cost in the long run. Easy purchase.
reactions: A better candle. Small improvement, but it changes every evening.

entries.intro: A trader said they've heard complaints about our {resource_type} from three different settlements. All saying the same thing. Inconsistent quality.
details: One batch good, the next poor. Buyers don't like surprises.
The merchant said if we don't sort it out, they'll source from somewhere else.
reactions: Three settlements, same complaint. That's a pattern we can't ignore.

statCheck.pass: Inconsistent quality is worse than consistently poor quality in trade. A buyer who knows what to expect can plan around it. A buyer who gets surprised starts looking for a new source immediately.
statCheck.fail: I hear the complaint. I just don't know how to fix it from my end.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've been on the road so long they can tell what region they're in by the taste of the water.
details: Sweet water means limestone country, metallic means iron in the ground, and clean and flat means deep wells.
I tried our water after they left and couldn't taste anything. I suppose that's what familiar means.
reactions: Tasting the landscape through the water. A skill I'll never need and can't stop thinking about.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they're adding a markup for our settlement specifically because we always order at the last minute.
details: Rush orders mean the trader can't plan the cart efficiently. We cost them space.
I tried to argue, but they're right. We never think about what we need until the cart arrives.
reactions: A surcharge for bad planning. Hard to complain when it's our own fault.

statCheck.pass: The truth is we don't plan because nobody wants to commit to decisions. Everyone waits for someone else to make the list, and by the time the cart arrives, it's a scramble.
statCheck.fail: We'll try to be better about it. Probably won't, though.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they never carry more than they can run with. Said it's the one rule that's kept them alive.
details: Everything they own fits in one pack and weighs less than a child.
They said comfort on the road is not about having enough. It's about having only what matters.
reactions: Travel light, move fast. A philosophy that doesn't translate to settlement life, but I respect it.

entries.intro: A merchant brought salted fish from a settlement near the coast. Best thing I've tasted in months.
details: Firm, clean salt, and it keeps for weeks. Nothing like the dried-out stuff we usually get.
They said the coastal settlements have perfected the cure. Years of practice show.
reactions: Good fish from far away. Proof that the route connects us to things we could never have on our own.

entries.intro: A trader said {other_settlement} has been refusing to sell to certain merchants. Only trades with ones they've approved.
details: An approved list means they control who profits from their goods. Smart for them, limiting for the route.
The trader wasn't on the list. They were furious.
reactions: A settlement choosing its partners. Makes sense for them, but it locks the rest of us out.

statCheck.pass: Approved merchant lists are how a settlement centralizes control over its trade. The real power isn't in what they sell, it's in who they let sell it. Unapproved traders either comply with their terms or find another source.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand the politics. I just know fewer merchants means fewer choices for us.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they always arrive at a settlement before noon. Said afternoon arrivals get less hospitality.
details: Morning means people are fresh, the day ahead. Afternoon means people are tired and thinking about supper.
I thought about it and they're right. I'm more generous in the morning.
reactions: Timing your arrival for a warmer welcome. The road teaches you to read people before you meet them.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they found our {resource_type} mixed in with another settlement's stock at a market. Someone had rebundled it.
details: Our marks were still on the wrapping underneath. The outer label was someone else's.
The merchant said it happens more than you'd think. Settlements resell under their own name.
reactions: Our goods under someone else's name. I'm not sure whether to be flattered or annoyed.

statCheck.pass: Relabeling goods is a margin game. Buy cheap from the source, slap your own name on it, sell at a premium. It only works because the end buyer doesn't know where things actually come from.
statCheck.fail: I just thought it was strange seeing our wrapping somewhere it shouldn't be.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've been walking the route for thirty years. Started as a young person with nothing and now they know every settlement by name.
details: Thirty years of the same road, and they said it's never the same twice. Seasons change, settlements grow or shrink.
They said the road is the only honest thing. It's always there, it always goes both ways.
reactions: Thirty years on one road. A lifetime of walking and they don't seem tired of it.

entries.intro: A merchant brought rope from {other_settlement} that doesn't fray at the ends. Whatever they treat it with, it works.
details: Our rope falls apart within weeks at the cut points. This stuff holds together clean.
I bought three lengths. Good rope saves you in ways you don't think about until the bad rope breaks.
reactions: Rope that holds. A simple thing that makes a real difference.

entries.intro: A trader said they're thinking of retiring from the route. Their knees can't take it anymore.
details: They've been walking this route longer than most of us have lived here.
Said they'd sell the route knowledge to whoever takes over, but finding a replacement isn't easy.
reactions: A trader retiring. We lose a person who knows what we need before we ask.

statCheck.pass: When a veteran trader retires, the route usually goes through a rough patch because the replacement doesn't know which settlements need what, who pays on time, or which roads are passable in rain. That knowledge takes years to rebuild.
statCheck.fail: I hope whoever replaces them is half as reliable.

entries.intro: A traveler asked if we had any messages to send to {other_settlement}. We didn't, but it was nice to be asked.
details: They said most settlements don't bother sending word to their neighbors. Everyone assumes someone else will.
I thought about writing something, but what would I say? 'We're still here' felt too obvious.
reactions: A chance to send word and nothing to say. Maybe that means things are fine.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a new kind of soap from a settlement along the coast. Lathers in cold water.
details: I didn't know soap could work in cold water. Ours just sits there unless you heat it.
Bought a block. My hands haven't been this clean since summer.
reactions: Soap that works in cold water. The kind of small discovery that makes the trade route worth having.

entries.intro: A trader told me they're being pressured by {other_settlement} to carry their goods exclusively. No other settlement's products on the cart.
details: Exclusive carriage means they'd drop us entirely unless we match the offer.
We can't match it. We don't have the volume or the coin.
reactions: Squeezed out by a bigger settlement. The route favors whoever has more to offer.

statCheck.pass: Exclusive carriage deals are how wealthy settlements capture entire trade routes. The merchant becomes their agent, not an independent trader, and every other settlement on the route becomes an afterthought.
statCheck.fail: I just hope they don't take the deal. We need that cart.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've noticed more people walking the road than usual this season. Families, not just traders.
details: Families on the road means people are moving, not visiting. Looking for somewhere better.
They said most of them come from the same direction and none of them look happy.
reactions: Families walking the road. That's never a cheerful sign.

statCheck.pass: Mass movement of families along a trade route means a settlement failed. Either its economy collapsed or living conditions dropped below what people would tolerate. The receiving settlements get the labor but also the burden.
statCheck.fail: I don't know where they're coming from. I just hope they find what they need.

entries.intro: A merchant left us a spare wheel spoke as a gift. Said they noticed ours was cracking.
details: They didn't charge for it. Just pointed at the wheel, handed over the spoke, and moved on.
A small kindness that saved us from a broken cart at the worst possible time.
reactions: A spare part as a gift. That's a merchant who sees us as more than a stop on the route.

entries.intro: A trader told me the road between here and {other_settlement} is getting worse every season. Ruts deeper, stones looser.
details: Nobody maintains the road between settlements. Each side assumes the other will do it.
The merchant said their cart broke an axle on that stretch last year.
reactions: A road nobody maintains. It connects us but neither side takes care of it.

statCheck.pass: We could fix our half in a week with enough hands. Nobody volunteers because the benefit is invisible until the road fails completely, and by then it costs ten times more to repair.
statCheck.fail: I know it's bad. Not sure whose job it is to fix it.

entries.intro: A traveler showed me a trick for starting a fire in wet conditions using shaved bark and a flat rock. Worked first try.
details: I've been struggling with wet tinder for years. This method cuts through the dampness.
They said everyone on the road knows it. We're the ones who needed to learn.
reactions: Fire in the wet. Road knowledge that would have saved me a hundred cold mornings.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they've been doing this route so long that they know our consumption patterns better than we do.
details: They predicted exactly what we'd order before we opened our mouths. Had it loaded on the cart already.
I don't know whether to be impressed or unsettled that a stranger knows our habits that well.
reactions: A merchant who knows us better than we know ourselves. That's either excellent service or too much attention.

statCheck.pass: A trader who knows your patterns also knows your weaknesses. What you'll overpay for, what you're desperate for, and when you're most likely to buy impulsively. That knowledge is leverage.
statCheck.fail: Good service, I suppose. Nothing more to it.

entries.intro: A trader said {other_settlement} just started producing their own {resource_type}. They used to buy ours.
details: Losing a buyer means our surplus has nowhere to go.
The trader said it happens every few years. A settlement invests in producing what it used to import.
reactions: One less customer. We'll need to find another or produce less.

statCheck.pass: When a settlement starts producing what it used to buy, the transition period is the dangerous time. Their quality is low but they subsidize it to build capacity. Meanwhile, the original producer loses the customer permanently.
statCheck.fail: I guess they don't need us anymore. That's the way of things.

entries.intro: A traveler told me our well water is the best they've tasted on the route. Made a point of filling every container they had.
details: They said most settlement water has a taste to it. Mineral, mud, or stale. Ours is clean and sweet.
We never think about our water. It's just water.
reactions: Good water. Something we have that we didn't even know was special.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they keep a list of settlements that owe them and we're on it. Small amount, but it's been three visits now.
details: I didn't even remember the debt. They did.
Three visits of reminders. At this point the embarrassment is worse than the coin.
reactions: Owing a trader. The kind of small debt that becomes a big reputation problem.

statCheck.pass: The truth is we could have paid it last visit, but we just kept putting it off because nobody wanted to be the one to spend the coin. Laziness dressed up as poverty.
statCheck.fail: We'll sort it out next time. Probably.

entries.intro: A traveler gave us a bundle of dried herbs they picked along the road. Said they didn't need them and we might.
details: Good for flavoring, some of them medicinal. We'd been low on both.
They asked for nothing in return. Just set the bundle down and said they'd been carrying too much.
reactions: Free herbs from a passing stranger. The road brings gifts when you least expect them.

entries.intro: A trader said the route between {other_settlement} and here used to be the busiest stretch. Now it's one of the quietest.
details: Traffic shifted to a different route that serves bigger settlements. We got bypassed.
The trader still comes because they're loyal, but the others moved on.
reactions: Bypassed by a busier route. Hard to argue with it when you see the logic.

statCheck.pass: When a trade route shifts, it's almost never temporary because merchants follow each other for shared camps, shared intelligence, and shared safety. Once the traffic leaves, it attracts more traffic to the new path and less to the old one.
statCheck.fail: The road's quieter. I try not to think too hard about why.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they keep a journal of every settlement's best dish. They want to remember the flavors.
details: They wrote down what we served them. Made it sound better than it was.
I asked what the best meal on the route was and they said a settlement two weeks north that makes a stew worth walking for.
reactions: A food journal of the route. I like someone who measures places by their meals.

entries.intro: A merchant told me a settlement on the route is charging tolls just to enter. Traders have to pay before they can sell.
details: The toll gets added to the price of everything the merchant sells after that stop.
We end up paying their toll in our prices. It ripples down the route.
reactions: A toll that gets passed to everyone else. We're paying for a gate we've never seen.

statCheck.pass: Tolls are a tax on movement that settlements impose when they control a chokepoint, and every downstream buyer absorbs the cost. It's the most effective way to extract value from a route without producing anything.
statCheck.fail: Tolls somewhere else, higher prices here. I don't fully understand how it connects.

entries.intro: A traveler fixed our gate latch without being asked. Just noticed it was broken, pulled out a tool, and fixed it.
details: Took them about a quarter hour. We'd been propping it shut with a rock for weeks.
They said idle hands on the road make them nervous. Fixing things keeps them useful.
reactions: A gate latch fixed by a stranger. We should have done it ourselves weeks ago.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they track the weather on every stretch of the route. Said our stretch is the worst for sudden rain.
details: Clear sky one moment, downpour the next. Their goods get soaked if they don't cover up fast.
They've started wrapping everything in double layers before they reach us. Extra work because of our weather.
reactions: Known for bad weather. Not the reputation I'd have chosen, but the rain doesn't care.

statCheck.pass: Double wrapping means double the packing cost, which they've quietly added to our prices. We're already paying a weather tax and didn't even realize it.
statCheck.fail: Rain is rain. We're used to it even if the traders aren't.

entries.intro: A trader said they brought extra {resource_type} because the last three settlements on the route were all short. Demand is outpacing supply.
details: Everyone wants the same thing and there isn't enough to go around.
The trader's rationing now. Smaller portions at each stop to make the cart last the full route.
reactions: Rationed supply on the route. We get some, but not as much as we need.

statCheck.pass: When a trader starts rationing, it means they've calculated that no single settlement's order is worth filling completely because the profit comes from serving everyone partially. The route becomes a breadline.
statCheck.fail: We take what we can get. Not much else to do.

entries.intro: A traveler said they make a point of learning one new thing at every settlement they visit. Here, they learned how we store grain.
details: They said our method is simpler than most but keeps the grain drier. Impressed them.
Funny to think someone found our storage interesting. We don't think about it at all.
reactions: A traveler impressed by how we store grain. You never know what's worth learning until someone else values it.

entries.intro: A merchant said they won't be back for two seasons. Taking a different route that pays better.
details: Two seasons without a regular trader. We'll have to rely on whoever else comes through.
They apologized, but I could tell the decision was already made.
reactions: Two seasons. A long time to go without a reliable visit.

statCheck.pass: When a trader leaves for a better route, they almost never come back. The new route builds relationships, the old one fades, and by the time two seasons pass, the trader has a new set of regular stops.
statCheck.fail: I hope they come back. But I wouldn't bet on it.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they judge a settlement by its bread. Ours passed.
details: They said you can tell a lot from the crust. How well the ovens work, how good the flour is, how much people care.
I handed them a second piece and they didn't refuse.
reactions: Judged by our bread and we passed. I'll take it.

entries.intro: A trader said {other_settlement} is stockpiling goods and not selling. Driving scarcity on the route.
details: If they're holding back, it's because they expect prices to climb. Everyone else suffers in the meantime.
The trader said they've seen this before. One settlement hoards, the rest scramble.
reactions: Someone else's strategy, our shortage. The route doesn't let anyone act alone without affecting everyone.

statCheck.pass: Stockpiling is a bet. Hold supply, wait for scarcity to peak, then release at maximum price. It only fails if another source appears before they sell, and right now there's no other source.
statCheck.fail: I don't know enough about their thinking. I just see the empty shelves.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they once walked three days without seeing a single person. Just empty road and sky.
details: They said by the second day, the silence stops being peaceful and starts being heavy.
When they finally saw smoke from a chimney, they nearly wept with relief.
reactions: Three days alone. The road is generous with quiet and stingy with company.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a salve from {other_settlement} that heals cracked hands in a day. I tried it and it works.
details: My hands have been cracked since autumn. One application and the splits started closing.
They said it's made from tallow and some herb they grow near {other_settlement}. Simple ingredients, remarkable result.
reactions: Healed hands from a jar someone else made. Worth every coin.

entries.intro: A trader told me they've started sleeping with their goods instead of leaving them on the cart overnight. Trust on the route has dropped.
details: They said two stops ago someone opened their cart during the night. Nothing taken, but the message was clear.
Sleeping on your goods. That's not a trade route. That's a guard posting.
reactions: A merchant sleeping on their cart. The route is harder than it used to be.

statCheck.pass: When traders start guarding their own goods at night, it means the informal trust that keeps a route running has broken down. The next step is armed escorts, and after that, abandoned routes.
statCheck.fail: Rough out there, I guess. Glad I'm not on the road.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've been to settlements where nobody under twenty remembers a time before trade routes. They think merchants have always come.
details: The older folks remember isolation. No outside goods, no news, no variety. The young ones can't imagine it.
The traveler said that's the surest sign a route is established. When nobody remembers life without it.
reactions: A generation that's never known isolation. I suppose that's what stability looks like.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said they've been training their child to take over the route. The kid's already learning the settlements.
details: The child sat on the cart taking notes while the parent made deals. Serious little face.
A trade route passed from parent to child. There's something steady about that.
reactions: A family route. Means we'll know the next trader before they're a stranger.

entries.intro: A trader said our settlement is the last profitable stop before a long stretch of nothing. Everything past us is a loss.
details: We're the edge of what's worth the trip. Past us, the settlements are too small or too far.
The trader said they turn around here. Used to go farther, but the numbers don't work anymore.
reactions: The last profitable stop. Better than being past the cutoff, I suppose.

statCheck.pass: Being the last stop on a profitable stretch gives us leverage. The trader needs us to justify the trip length. But if we ever shrink enough to stop being worth it, the turnaround point moves closer and we lose the route entirely.
statCheck.fail: At least they still come here. That's what matters.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they once stayed in a settlement for an entire season because they broke their ankle on the road. Said it changed how they think about settled life.
details: Three months in one place after years of walking. They said the first week was torture and the last was peaceful.
When the ankle healed, they stood up and walked out without looking back.
reactions: A season in one place cured their ankle and reminded them why they walk. Different lessons from the same stay.

entries.intro: A merchant brought thread from {other_settlement} that's stronger than anything we make. Doesn't snap under tension.
details: I pulled on it as hard as I could and it held. Our homemade thread would have broken halfway through.
Bought every spool they had. Good thread means every repair lasts longer.
reactions: Strong thread. The kind of basic improvement that quietly changes everything you fix with it.

entries.intro: A trader said {other_settlement} has been buying up all the {resource_type} before it reaches us. Nothing left by the time the cart gets here.
details: They're first on the route and they take everything. We're last and we get the empty cart.
The trader said they can't turn down a buyer who wants the whole load.
reactions: First stop takes all. Our position on the route decides what we get.

statCheck.pass: Route position determines access. First stops get first pick, last stops get remainders. The only way to change it is to offer enough to make the trader save some for you, which means paying a premium for the privilege of being served last.
statCheck.fail: We just don't get there first. Simple as that.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they carry a flint and steel that they've used every single day for six years. Never fails.
details: The steel is worn smooth on one side from striking. They said it's the only possession they'd fight to keep.
Six years of daily use. That's a tool that's earned its place in the pack.
reactions: One tool, six years, never failed. There's a lesson in that about what actually matters.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they accidentally brought twice as much stock as usual. Offered us the surplus at a discount.
details: Overstocked and far from home. They'd rather sell cheap than haul it back.
We bought everything. A mistake on their end, a windfall on ours.
reactions: Someone else's bad math, our good luck. Doesn't happen often.

entries.intro: A trader told me {other_settlement} is trying to establish a second trade route that doesn't go through us. A direct path to settlements further along.
details: If the new route works, we lose our position as a waypoint. Traders would skip us entirely.
The trader said it's still just talk, but the surveys have already been done.
reactions: A route that goes around us. Talk for now, but talk becomes road eventually.

statCheck.pass: New route surveys are expensive and settlements don't commission them idly. If {other_settlement} is surveying a bypass, they've already decided we're not worth the detour. The survey is the final step before commitment, not the first.
statCheck.fail: I try not to worry about things that haven't happened yet. Doesn't always work.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've noticed that every settlement has one thing they're proud of. They asked what ours is.
details: I had to think about it. We're not the best at anything specific, but we're dependable.
The traveler said dependable is rarer than exceptional. Most settlements are neither.
reactions: Dependable. Not exciting, but the traveler made it sound like it mattered.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a set of metal needles so fine they go through leather without a punch. Beautiful work.
details: Our bone needles break every other use. These metal ones are practically indestructible.
I bought a set for the household and a spare set for when I inevitably lose one.
reactions: Proper needles. You don't think about your tools until someone shows you better ones.

entries.intro: A trader told me they've noticed a pattern. Settlements that invest in storage always do better than ones that don't.
details: Good storage means you can buy when prices are low and use when prices are high. It's a buffer against bad timing.
They said we'd benefit from better storage. I know they're right.
reactions: Storage as strategy. We use ours for keeping things dry. They're saying we should use it for keeping prices fair.

statCheck.pass: Settlements with surplus storage can stockpile when traders bring oversupply and sell when there's scarcity, turning a passive stop into an active trader. The investment pays for itself within two seasons.
statCheck.fail: I know our storage isn't great. One thing at a time.

entries.intro: A traveler arrived soaking wet and shivering. We dried them off, fed them, and they were on their way by morning.
details: They said not every settlement would have opened the door. Some would have made them wait outside until morning.
A hot meal and a dry place to sit. Cost us almost nothing and meant everything to them.
reactions: Hospitality in bad weather. The easiest kindness to give and the hardest to forget.

entries.intro: A merchant said the demand for {resource_type} has dropped across the whole route. Nobody's buying.
details: If nobody's buying, the price drops. Good if you're a buyer, terrible if you're a producer.
We produce it. So this hurts us directly.
reactions: Falling demand for what we make. Not a crisis, but a worry.

statCheck.pass: A route-wide demand drop usually means a substitute has appeared or a major buyer has changed suppliers. The drop rarely reverses on its own. You either find new buyers or produce something else.
statCheck.fail: I just hope it picks back up. We don't have a lot of alternatives.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they learned to read the sky for weather on the road. Said it's the first skill any traveler masters.
details: Red in the morning means rain by afternoon, high thin clouds mean clear for two days. They rattled it off like a list.
I know some of it from farming, but they had a precision to it that comes from sleeping outdoors every night.
reactions: Reading the sky. Different need on the road than in the field, but the same skill.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said they've added a healer's supplies to their cart. Every settlement asks for them now.
details: Bandages, poultices, dried herbs for tea. Simple things, but nobody has enough.
They said healer's supplies are the one thing that never sits on the cart unsold.
reactions: Medicine on the trade route. Says something that every settlement is short on it.

statCheck.pass: Universal demand for healer's supplies means nobody on the route has enough trained healers or enough local herbs. It's the most basic gap and the hardest to close because you can't rush growing medicinal plants.
statCheck.fail: I know we need them. Everyone does, apparently.

entries.intro: A trader told me they watched a settlement refuse a merchant and then beg the next one for the same goods at a higher price.
details: Turned away the first cart thinking a better deal was coming. It wasn't.
The merchant said pride costs more than coin in the long run.
reactions: Holding out for a better deal that never came. A lesson we should remember.

statCheck.pass: We almost did the same thing last season. Turned down a fair price hoping to negotiate, and the trader left. We ended up buying worse goods from the next cart at a markup.
statCheck.fail: Other settlements' mistakes. Nothing to do with us.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they collect seeds from every region they pass through. Started a little garden at a settlement that let them stay.
details: Plants from ten different stretches of road, all growing in one patch of dirt. Some of them took, some didn't.
They said it's the closest they have to a home. A garden they visit once a year.
reactions: A garden tended once a year by a traveler who can't stay. There's something beautiful and sad about that.

entries.intro: A merchant told me our last batch of {resource_type} was the best they've seen from us. Asked what we did differently.
details: We didn't do anything different. Sometimes the output is just better for no clear reason.
They want us to repeat it. I told them we'd try, but I can't promise what I don't understand.
reactions: A good batch we can't explain. I'll take the compliment and hope for the best.

entries.intro: A trader told me a new merchant has started working the route. Younger, faster, and willing to undercut everyone.
details: Low prices now, but once the old merchants quit, the new one can charge whatever they want.
The established traders are worried. They can't match the pace or the prices.
reactions: A new merchant shaking up the route. Good for us now, uncertain for later.

statCheck.pass: New merchants undercutting established ones is a cycle. The newcomer runs at a loss to build the route, the veterans drop out, and the survivor raises prices above where they started. The customer celebrates twice and pays three times.
statCheck.fail: Cheaper prices. I'm not going to argue with that.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they learned to cook with whatever grows within reach of the road. Said it beats carrying food that spoils.
details: Wild greens, roots, the occasional berry bush. They know what's safe and what isn't by sight alone.
They made us a soup from things growing near the settlement that we'd been walking past and ignoring.
reactions: A meal from what grows underfoot. Makes you feel foolish for not seeing it yourself.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said they've been getting requests for things they've never heard of. Settlements are asking for goods from farther away than the route reaches.
details: Requests for spices, metals, and cloth that only exist beyond the route's endpoints.
The trader said they can't go that far. The route is as long as their cart and legs will carry them.
reactions: Wanting what's beyond the route. Ambition that outpaces the road.

statCheck.pass: Demand for goods beyond the route's reach means there's profit waiting for anyone brave or foolish enough to extend the run. The first trader to connect two routes would become the most important person on both.
statCheck.fail: Interesting to think about. Probably nothing that affects us.

entries.intro: A trader said they nearly lost their entire stock to a river crossing last month. Water rose faster than expected.
