details: They got the cart across but everything on the bottom layer was soaked through.
Lost about a third of the goods. The rest they dried and sold at a discount.
reactions: A river crossing gone wrong. The road takes its toll in ways you can't predict.

statCheck.pass: The 'discounted' goods were still wet inside the wrapping. I didn't say anything because the price was low enough, but I know we paid for damaged stock and called it a deal.
statCheck.fail: Bad luck at a crossing. Could happen to anyone on the road.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they always pay something at every settlement, even if they don't need to buy. Said it keeps them welcome.
details: A small purchase, a coin for water, something to show they're not just taking.
They said a traveler who only receives eventually stops being invited.
reactions: Paying to stay welcome. The road has its own kind of manners.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a jar of preserved plums from a settlement none of us have heard of. Tasted like nothing I've had before.
details: Sweet, tart, and something else. A flavor I don't have a word for.
One jar. The merchant said they only bring them when they find them, which is rare.
reactions: A taste from somewhere far away. A reminder of how much exists beyond what we know.

entries.intro: A trader said {other_settlement} has started inspecting every incoming shipment. Measuring, weighing, checking for quality before they'll accept it.
details: Inspections slow the trader down by half a day at every visit.
The trader said they understand the reason, but it makes {other_settlement} a stop they dread.
reactions: Inspections at the gate. Quality control that costs everyone time.

statCheck.pass: Mandatory inspections are a power move. The settlement sets the standard and the trader either meets it or leaves empty-handed. The real effect is that traders bring their best goods there and dump the rejects on less demanding stops like ours.
statCheck.fail: I know it slows things down. Beyond that, I don't know.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've been to a settlement where people trade skills instead of goods. You teach them something, they teach you something.
details: No coin changes hands. A blacksmithing lesson for a weaving lesson.
The traveler said they learned three trades in a single visit and left richer in knowledge than any merchant leaves in coin.
reactions: Skill for skill. A different kind of economy that makes sense the more you think about it.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they've had three cart wheels break this season already. The roads are eating them alive.
details: Wheel repair costs eat into their profit. At this rate, the route barely breaks even.
They said they're spending more on maintenance than on new stock.
reactions: A cart falling apart on bad roads. The merchant pays the price for infrastructure nobody maintains.

statCheck.pass: Three wheel breaks means the trader's buying cheap wheels or the roads have deteriorated past what normal equipment can handle. Either way, the cost gets baked into our prices eventually.
statCheck.fail: Rough roads, broken wheels. That's the route for you.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they recognize our settlement by smell before they see it. Said every settlement has a scent.
details: Wood smoke, cooking, the particular dust of our ground. They said it's as distinct as a face.
I've never noticed our own smell. You stop smelling home after the first year.
reactions: Known by our scent. A kind of identity I never thought about.

entries.intro: A merchant said they've started offering {resource_type} on credit to settlements that can't pay up front. We're one of them.
details: Credit means we get what we need now and owe next visit. It also means we start the next visit in debt.
The merchant said it's not charity. The interest is baked into the next price.
reactions: Buying on credit. Feels like relief now but I know it's tomorrow's problem.

statCheck.pass: Credit from a trader is a trap that looks like a favor. Each visit starts with debt, so you never negotiate from a position of strength. After a few cycles, you're buying at the trader's price because you can't afford to say no.
statCheck.fail: We needed the goods. The terms are the terms.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they always check on the children of settlements they visit. Said it tells them more than anything else about how a place is doing.
details: Happy children, fed children, children playing. That's a settlement doing well. Quiet, thin children are the other kind.
They said ours are doing fine. I was glad to hear it from an outside eye.
reactions: Judged by our children. The most honest measure there is.

entries.intro: A trader told me they lost a whole day waiting out a storm on the road. Arrived here a day late with wet goods.
details: The delay means their schedule is pushed back for every settlement after us.
Wet goods sell for less. A storm costs everyone on the route, not just the trader.
reactions: A storm on the road and everyone downstream pays for it. The route carries the weather's consequences.

statCheck.pass: I think the goods were wetter than they admitted. They wrapped them to hide the damage and sold them as 'slightly damp.' We'll find out when we open the inner layers.
statCheck.fail: Weather happens. Not much anyone can do about it.

entries.intro: A traveler said they used to travel in groups for safety. Now they travel alone because the groups argued too much.
details: Three travelers with three opinions on pace, route, and camps. Faster to just walk alone.
They said solitude is a fair price for not having to negotiate every decision.
reactions: Alone for the peace of it. I understand that better than I should.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} brought a new type of lamp that burns cleaner. Less smoke, brighter light.
details: Our lamps leave soot on the ceiling. This one burned for hours without a trace.
Expensive, but the light it gives is worth twice what our candles manage.
reactions: A better lamp. The kind of thing that makes you realize how dim your evenings have been.

entries.intro: A trader told me the merchant who used to supply us with {resource_type} has been undercut by a competitor offering lower quality at half the price.
details: Cheaper means worse, but most settlements don't care as long as it's functional.
Our old supplier can't compete. They're losing customers one settlement at a time.
reactions: Cheaper wins and quality loses. The route doesn't reward the best. It rewards the cheapest.

statCheck.pass: When cheap goods drive out quality goods, the market adjusts permanently. Buyers forget what good quality felt like, the quality producer goes under, and when the cheap goods eventually fail, there's nobody left making the good ones.
statCheck.fail: I just buy what I can afford. The rest is above me.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they write down the name of every person who shows them kindness on the road. Said the list is longer than they expected.
details: A meal here, a direction there, a warning about the path ahead. Small things that add up.
They showed me the list. Some of the names are from settlements I've never heard of.
reactions: A list of kindnesses. I hope our name is on it.

entries.intro: A merchant said they won't carry anything fragile anymore. Too many breaks on the road, too many losses.
details: Pottery, glass, anything that shatters. Gone from the cart. Only goods that survive rough roads.
It means we can't get fine goods through the route anymore. Only the sturdy stuff makes it.
reactions: No fragile goods. The road decides what we can have, and it decided against anything delicate.

statCheck.pass: Dropping fragile goods is usually the first step before dropping a route entirely. The merchant is cutting the hardest-to-carry items first because the whole run is becoming marginal.
statCheck.fail: Makes sense from their side. I just miss having options.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they time their visits to settlements by the season. Come in spring when people are optimistic and buying.
details: Spring means planting's done, spirits are up, and coin flows freer.
In winter, nobody buys what they don't desperately need. The road teaches you when to show up.
reactions: Selling to the season, not the settlement. Smart thinking from someone who reads people for a living.

statCheck.pass: We spend more in spring than we should because the mood is better, and the traders know it and price accordingly. Our spring prices are always higher than autumn, and nobody questions it because the sun is out.
statCheck.fail: Makes sense to visit when the weather's good, I suppose.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} told me they're adding our settlement to their emergency route. If their usual route fails, they come through us.
details: Emergency route means we'd see them in bad times, not good ones.
Still, any visit is better than none. We'll take what we can get.
reactions: A backup stop. Not first choice, but at least we're on the map.

statCheck.pass: Being on the emergency route means they think their primary route might actually fail. They're not being cautious, they're planning for collapse. That should worry us more than it reassures us.
statCheck.fail: Backup plan. Better than nothing, I suppose.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they can tell which settlements have good leadership just by looking at the fences. Ours are straight.
details: Straight fences mean someone cares about order. Leaning fences mean nobody's in charge.
I've never thought of fences as a sign of anything except property lines.
reactions: Straight fences as a sign of good management. I'll look at them differently now.

entries.intro: A trader told me {other_settlement} is demanding that merchants carry a sealed document proving their goods' origin. No papers, no sale.
details: Papers for goods. Another layer of slowdown on the route.
The trader said they had to go back to their source and get the documents before they could sell anything.
reactions: Paperwork for trade. The route gets more complicated every season.

statCheck.pass: Origin documents are a way for settlements to reject goods from competitors while claiming quality control. They can refuse any trader whose source isn't on their approved list, and the paperwork makes it look legitimate.
statCheck.fail: Papers and rules. I'm glad we don't require that here.

entries.intro: A traveler said they met an elderly traveler on the road who gave them a piece of advice. Always carry more water than you think you need.
details: Simple advice, but they said it saved them twice in dry stretches where springs were empty.
An extra waterskin weighs a lot at the start and nothing at the end.
reactions: Carry more water. Advice that sounds obvious and almost nobody follows.

entries.intro: A merchant said they brought exactly what we asked for last time. Every item, every quantity.
details: We gave them a list on their last visit and they fulfilled it to the letter.
I didn't expect them to remember, let alone follow through. That kind of service earns loyalty.
reactions: An order filled exactly as requested. That's a merchant worth keeping.

entries.intro: A trader told me a merchant they know has been diluting goods before selling them. Watering down oils, cutting grain with chaff.
details: Hard to catch unless you're paying close attention. The weight's right, but the quality's half.
They said settlements that don't check their shipments are easy targets.
reactions: Diluted goods sold at full price. Trust on the route depends on people not doing exactly that.

statCheck.pass: Dilution is the most common trade fraud on any route because it's almost undetectable in bulk. The defense is weighing net content against a known standard, but most settlements don't have accurate scales.
statCheck.fail: I wouldn't know diluted goods from real ones. That's the problem.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they prefer settlements where nobody asks why they're traveling. Just feeds them and lets them go.
details: Some places interrogate every stranger. Where are you from, where are you going, what do you carry.
They said the best settlements trust first and ask later.
reactions: Trust without questions. A rare thing that says more about the settlement than about the traveler.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a shipment of dried mushrooms from {other_settlement}. Packed tight and they keep for months.
details: We don't have the right conditions to grow mushrooms here. These fill a gap we've had for years.
They rehydrate well and add flavor to everything. A small thing that improves a lot of meals.
reactions: Mushrooms from somewhere that grows them. A gap filled by the route.

entries.intro: A trader told me two merchants on the route have started traveling together for safety. Sharing camps and taking turns keeping watch.
details: Safer, but slower. Two carts move at the speed of the worst wheel.
They said it's a sign of the times that traders can't walk alone anymore.
reactions: Merchants pairing up. Practical, but it says something about the road that they feel they need to.

statCheck.pass: Paired merchants also coordinate prices. Two traders traveling together won't undercut each other, which means the competition that kept prices fair just disappeared. Safety for them, higher costs for us.
statCheck.fail: I suppose the road is rougher than it used to be.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've started carrying a small mirror. Said it's the most traded item on the road. Everyone wants a look at themselves.
details: A quick look in a mirror is worth a meal in most settlements. People are vain even when they're hungry.
They lent it to half the settlement before they left. Nobody wanted to give it back.
reactions: A mirror on the road. Vanity as currency. Can't say I blame anyone.

statCheck.pass: I looked in it myself and didn't recognize the face staring back. You forget what you look like when there's no mirror around. That shook me more than I expected.
statCheck.fail: Interesting thing to carry. I suppose people want what they can't easily get.

entries.intro: A merchant said they've had to turn down settlements that can't pay because they've run out of credit to extend. Everyone owes them.
details: A trader who's owed by every settlement has no cash to buy new stock. The chain stops.
They said one more bad season and they'll have to abandon the route entirely.
reactions: Everyone owes the merchant and the merchant owes their supplier. A chain of debts with nobody at the end to absorb it.

statCheck.pass: When a whole route runs on debt, one failure collapses everything. The merchant can't restock, settlements can't resupply, and the route goes dormant until someone with fresh capital starts over.
statCheck.fail: I know things are tight. I didn't realize how tight for the traders.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they always leave a mark on the road near good settlements. Nothing obvious. Just a scratch on a particular stone.
details: Other travelers know to look for it. A scratch means safe rest, fair people, clean water.
They said our stone has a mark. I didn't know whether to be touched or worried that strangers mark our roads.
reactions: A mark near our settlement. An invisible recommendation carved in stone.

entries.intro: A merchant said they're considering raising their minimum order. Stops that buy too little aren't worth the time.
details: Minimum orders mean we either buy more than we need or don't buy at all.
The trader said it's about efficiency. Small sales take the same time as large ones but earn a fraction.
reactions: Buy more or get nothing. A choice that doesn't feel like a choice.

statCheck.pass: Minimum order thresholds are how traders prune their routes. Set the bar just above what the smallest settlements can afford and you drop them without officially refusing service.
statCheck.fail: I understand the logic, even if I don't like it.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they always carry a length of rope and it's saved them more times than any weapon.
details: River crossings, hauling packs up steep ground, tying down a shelter in wind. All rope.
They said a traveler without rope is a traveler who hasn't needed it yet.
reactions: Rope over weapons. Different priorities on the road than in the stories.

entries.intro: A merchant brought a block of salt so pure it was almost translucent. Finest quality I've ever handled.
details: Our usual salt has grit in it. This dissolved clean and tasted of nothing but salt.
Worth the price. Good salt preserves better, seasons better, and goes further.
reactions: Pure salt. One of those goods that seems simple until you try a better version.

entries.intro: A trader told me {other_settlement} is pressuring merchants to only visit settlements that trade exclusively with them. A closed network.
details: Trade with us and you can't trade with them. That's the demand.
Most merchants won't choose us over a larger settlement. We'd lose.
reactions: Forced to choose sides on a trade route. Nobody asked us, but we'll feel the result.

statCheck.pass: Exclusive trade networks are how powerful settlements build dependency. Once a merchant commits to the network, every settlement outside it becomes unreachable. The network grows by absorbing options until there's no alternative.
statCheck.fail: I just hope the merchants don't have to choose. We'd come up short.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've noticed that settlements with dogs are always friendlier than ones without.
details: They said a barking dog at the edge of a settlement means the place is alive and watchful. Silence means something's off.
We've got dogs. They barked at the traveler for ten minutes before settling down.
reactions: Dogs as a measure of welcome. I never thought of our noisy mutts as ambassadors.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they keep a list of which settlements pay the fastest. We're near the bottom.
details: Quick payment gets you first pick of the next shipment. Slow payment gets you whatever's left.
I didn't realize our payment speed affected what we receive.
reactions: Slow payers, last served. A connection I should have made sooner.

statCheck.pass: We pay slow because we argue about who owes what internally, and by the time we sort ourselves out, the merchant's already moved on. It's not poverty. It's disorganization.
statCheck.fail: We do our best. Not everyone has coin sitting ready.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they sleep lighter every year on the road. Said they wake at the smallest sound now.
details: A branch snapping, an animal moving, rain starting. Everything wakes them.
They said they haven't had a full night's sleep in years but they've never been caught off guard.
reactions: Alert and exhausted. The road asks for one and gives the other.

entries.intro: A merchant said they've started keeping a record of every settlement's {resource_type} quality over time. Ours has been improving.
details: Slow improvement over three years. They showed me the notes. Consistent upward trend.
I didn't notice the improvement because it happened gradually. Takes an outside eye to see patterns.
reactions: Improving without realizing it. A good trend to be on.

entries.intro: A trader said they've been avoiding a particular stretch of road because the ground has been unstable. Detours add days to the trip.
details: The detour means they arrive later with less energy and a worse mood.
Extra days on the road means extra food consumed, which means less profit.
reactions: A bad road that adds days. We pay for it in delayed deliveries and higher prices.

statCheck.pass: Unstable ground on a trade route usually means the drainage failed and the road is washing out from below. It gets worse every season until someone rebuilds the drainage, and nobody's going to do that.
statCheck.fail: Bad stretch of road. I don't know the details.

entries.intro: A traveler gave us a recipe for a poultice that draws out splinters. Works faster than anything we had.
details: Just clay, vinegar, and a crushed leaf they identified from our own surroundings.
I tried it on a splinter that had been bothering me for days. Out in an hour.
reactions: A traveler's remedy from our own ingredients. The knowledge was the missing piece.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they've seen our settlement grow since they started the route. More buildings, more people, more to trade.
details: They said most settlements on the route are steady or shrinking. Growing is unusual.
I don't feel like we've grown. But a trader who visits twice a year sees the change we miss.
reactions: Growing. A compliment from someone who watches us in slow motion.

entries.intro: A trader told me a dispute between two settlements on the route has spilled over into trade. Each one refuses to sell to the other's allies.
details: We're not allied with either, but the disruption affects what's available on the cart.
The merchant said they're caught in the middle. Carry goods for one side and lose the other.
reactions: Someone else's argument, our missing goods. The route doesn't care about neutrality.

statCheck.pass: Trade disputes between settlements are proxy conflicts. The real issue is usually territorial or personal, but the weapon is supply restriction. Every neutral settlement on the route takes collateral damage.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand what they're fighting about. I just notice the cart's lighter.

entries.intro: A traveler said they always carry a second pair of shoes. Told me the first pair failed them on a mountain pass once and they walked the last day barefoot.
details: Barefoot on stone for a full day. They showed me the scars on their feet.
Since then, spare shoes. Heavier pack, but they never worry about their feet giving out.
reactions: Spare shoes as insurance. Every traveler has a story behind their most important possession.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they brought leather from {other_settlement} that's been treated with something that keeps it supple. Doesn't crack in the cold.
details: Our leather goes stiff and splits every winter. This stuff bends like cloth.
Expensive, but anything that lasts through cold without breaking is worth the cost.
reactions: Leather that survives winter. A material solution to a seasonal problem.

entries.intro: A trader told me they're the only source of news for five settlements on this route. What they say is what people believe.
details: They try to be accurate, but they admitted that sometimes they exaggerate to make a better story.
We have no way to check what they tell us. We just take their word.
reactions: A single source of truth on the route. That's a lot of influence for one person with a cart.

statCheck.pass: A sole news carrier controls the narrative between settlements. They can inflate demand for goods they carry, talk down competitors, or shape perceptions of settlements they don't like. Information monopoly is the most invisible kind of power.
statCheck.fail: I just listen to what they say. Haven't thought much about whether it's all true.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've been walking the route for so long that their body has adapted. They can go farther on less food and less sleep than when they started.
details: They said the first year nearly killed them. Now the same distance feels like a morning walk.
The body adjusts. The mind takes longer.
reactions: A body shaped by the road. Different kind of strength than we build in a settlement.

entries.intro: A merchant told me {other_settlement} has been sending their own people to buy directly from producers, cutting out the middleman.
details: Direct buying means better prices for them but fewer goods on the cart for the rest of us.
The trader said if more settlements do this, the route won't need independent merchants at all.
reactions: Cutting out the middleman. Efficient for them, uncertain for anyone who depends on the middleman.

statCheck.pass: Direct purchasing only works for settlements large enough to send buyers. Smaller settlements will always need the merchant route, but they'll pay more because the trader has fewer profitable stops to subsidize the trip.
statCheck.fail: I'm not sure what it means for us. We'll see.

entries.intro: A traveler said they once walked into a settlement at exactly the right moment. A feast day. Best luck they ever had.
details: Free food, music, and a warm bed for the night. They didn't know the occasion and nobody cared.
They said timing on the road is everything. A day earlier, they'd have slept in the rain.
reactions: A feast by accident. The road hands out luck as randomly as it hands out storms.

entries.intro: A trader told me they've been keeping track of which settlements have the most honest scales. We ranked well.
details: Honest scales mean fair trade. The merchant said they always double-check at stops they don't trust.
We didn't even know we were being tested. Passed anyway.
reactions: Trusted for honest scales. A reputation worth more than any one sale.

entries.intro: A merchant said the {resource_type} market has shifted. What used to be our best seller barely moves now.
details: Tastes change, needs change. What people wanted last year isn't what they want this year.
The trader said we should diversify, but that's easier said than done with what we have.
reactions: A shifting market and we're slow to shift with it. That's the risk of relying on one thing.

statCheck.pass: Market shifts on a trade route rarely reverse. Once settlements find an alternative source or a substitute good, the original demand doesn't come back. The smart move is to pivot early, but most settlements wait until the old product is worthless.
statCheck.fail: Things change. We'll figure it out.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they once helped deliver a baby at a settlement on the road. Nobody else knew what to do.
details: They'd seen it done once before and that was enough to be the most qualified person in the room.
Mother and child both fine. The traveler stayed an extra day to make sure.
reactions: A baby delivered by a stranger passing through. The road puts people where they're needed sometimes.

entries.intro: A merchant told me they're switching from a cart to a pack animal. Less capacity but faster travel.
details: Faster means more stops per season. Less on the animal but more frequent visits.
They said the math works out better. Speed over volume.
reactions: Less goods, more visits. A different rhythm for the route.

statCheck.pass: Switching to a pack animal usually means the trader can't afford cart repairs anymore. They frame it as efficiency, but it's really about cutting the one cost that was eating their profit.
statCheck.fail: Different setup, same trader. I'm sure it'll work out.

entries.intro: A trader said {other_settlement} has been marking their goods with a stamp of origin. You can tell exactly where everything came from.
details: A stamped good carries the settlement's reputation with it. Good or bad, it's accountable.
The trader said stamped goods sell faster because buyers trust the source.
reactions: Stamps of origin. A smart idea we should probably copy.

statCheck.pass: Stamping is accountability that cuts both ways. When your goods are good, the stamp sells them faster. When your goods are bad, the stamp makes sure everyone knows exactly who to blame.
statCheck.fail: Seems like a good idea. I don't know the details.

entries.intro: A traveler arrived here sick and we let them rest until they recovered. Took four days.
details: We weren't sure about letting a sick stranger stay. But sending them back onto the road in that state didn't feel right.
They recovered, thanked everyone individually, and left looking better than when they arrived.
reactions: Four days for a stranger's health. Time well spent, I think.

entries.intro: A merchant told me a fire destroyed a warehouse at a settlement on the route. Goods meant for five stops, all gone.
details: The trader who stored goods there lost everything. No shipments for weeks.
We felt it as an empty cart when the replacement merchant finally came through.
reactions: A fire somewhere else and an empty cart here. The route connects the consequences.

statCheck.pass: Centralized storage for a whole route is efficient but fragile. One fire wipes out supply for every settlement downstream. The smarter approach is smaller stocks at multiple points, but nobody wants to pay for that.
statCheck.fail: A fire and an empty cart. That's all I know about it.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they've met people on the road who've never been to a settlement. Born traveling, raised walking.
details: No home, no walls, just the road and whoever walks with them. It's all they've known.
They said those people are the best navigators and the worst at staying still.
reactions: Born on the road. A different way to grow up entirely.

entries.intro: A merchant from {other_settlement} said they've started wrapping fragile goods in straw from a specific farm. The straw is softer and absorbs shocks better.
details: Better packing means fewer broken goods. The farmer who grows the straw doesn't even know it's being used this way.
Small details in the supply chain that make big differences at the destination.
reactions: Straw that protects better. Someone's accident became someone else's solution.

statCheck.pass: Better packing is also how traders justify raising prices on fragile goods. 'Premium wrapping' becomes a line item, and you're paying for straw that costs them almost nothing.
statCheck.fail: Clever idea. I'm sure it helps.

entries.intro: A trader told me they're going to stop carrying {resource_type} entirely. The profit margin has collapsed.
details: Too many producers, not enough buyers. The price dropped below what it costs to haul.
We relied on that supply. Now we'll have to find it somewhere else or go without.
reactions: A good dropped from the cart. We lose access because someone else lost profit.

statCheck.pass: When a trader drops a product, the settlements that depend on it have no recourse unless another trader picks it up. In practice, nobody picks up what's unprofitable, so the supply simply vanishes from the route.
statCheck.fail: I just know we can't get it anymore. That's enough to worry about.

entries.intro: A traveler told me they keep a mental map of every clean water source between settlements. Said it's more valuable than any physical map.
details: Springs, streams, wells at abandoned homesteads. They know them all by feel and distance.
They said they'd share the knowledge if we had travelers of our own to send out. We don't.
reactions: A map of water carried in someone's head. The kind of wealth you can't steal or buy.
